# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Transformation During Braking

When a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is brought to rest, the kinetic energy dissipated by the brakes is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$$

**Where:**
- $m$ = total vehicle mass (kg) — for a tractor this includes tractor mass + implement mass.
- $v$ = vehicle linear velocity (m/s).

This kinetic energy is converted into **heat** at the friction interface. The heat generated per stop is:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2} \quad \text{(in Joules, J)}$$

> [!WARNING] Engineering Reality
> For a **4000 kg tractor** stopping from **30 km/h (8.33 m/s)**, the heat generated is:
> $Q = 0.5 \times 4000 \times (8.33)^2 \approx 138,889$ J $\approx$ **139 kJ per stop**.
> This is equivalent to the heat released by burning a small match — but concentrated in a brake drum weighing only **15–25 kg**. Heat dissipation is therefore the **primary design constraint** for tractor brake sizing.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Braking Force Equation

Consider a vehicle on a level road. Applying Newton's second law during deceleration:

$$F_{brake} - F_{rolling} = m \cdot a_{decel}$$

Neglecting rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag for a worst-case design:

$$F_{brake} = m \cdot a_{decel}$$

**Where:**
- $a_{decel}$ = deceleration (m/s²). For agricultural tractors, KTU references a typical design value of **$a_{decel} = 1.5$ to $2.5$ m/s²** (per IS 12161).

## 2.3 Brake Torque and Wheel Loading Distribution

For a vehicle with weight distribution $W_f$ (front) and $W_r$ (rear) on level ground, the normal reactions are:

$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot (b + f \cdot h)}{L} \qquad W_r = \frac{W \cdot (a - f \cdot h)}{L}$$

**Where:**
- $W$ = total vehicle weight (N)
- $L$ = wheelbase (m)
- $a, b$ = horizontal distances of CG from rear and front axles (m)
- $h$ = height of centre of gravity (m)
- $f$ = deceleration expressed as a fraction of $g$.

The braking torque required at each wheel is:

$$T_{wheel} = F_{brake, wheel} \cdot r_{wheel} = \mu \cdot N_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Tractor-Specific Insight
> Tractors are **nose-heavy** when pulling implements. The implement drawbar pulls downward on the rear axle, increasing $W_r$ significantly. **Most tractor braking effort is on the rear wheels** — which is why most tractors are fitted with **rear-wheel drum brakes** and only lighter **front-wheel disc brakes** (or none at all on 2WD models).

## 2.4 Stopping Distance Derivation

The total stopping distance has two components:

$$d_{stop} = d_{reaction} + d_{braking}$$

**Step 1 — Reaction distance** (distance travelled during driver perception-reaction time $t_r$):

$$d_{reaction} = v \cdot t_r$$

**Step 2 — Braking distance** (kinematic relation $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $v=0$):

$$d_{braking} = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{decel}}$$

**Step 3 — Total stopping distance:**

$$d_{stop} = v \cdot t_r + \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{decel}}$$

For tractors, ASABE / IS standards recommend $t_r = 1.0$ s (operator alertness is higher on tractors than passenger cars due to the slower operating speeds).

## 2.5 Braking Efficiency

Braking efficiency $\eta_b$ is the ratio of actual deceleration achieved to the maximum possible deceleration (limited by tyre-road friction $\mu_t$):

$$\eta_b = \frac{a_{actual}}{a_{max}} = \frac{a_{actual}}{\mu_t \cdot g}$$

> [!NOTE] KTU Exam Definition
> A braking efficiency of **60%–75%** is typical for well-maintained tractor drum brakes. A value **below 50%** indicates the brake requires immediate servicing (linings worn, oil-contaminated, or improperly adjusted).

## 2.6 Hydraulic Brake System Pressure Analysis

For a hydraulic brake, Pascal's Law gives:

$$P = \frac{F_{pedal}}{A_{master}} = \frac{F_{wheel\,cylinder}}{A_{wheel\,cylinder}}$$

The force multiplication factor is:

$$M_{hydraulic} = \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$$

**Typical values for a tractor:**
- $A_{master}$ (master cylinder piston area) $\approx$ **$3.14 \times 10^{-4}$ m²** (20 mm bore)
- $A_{wheel}$ (wheel cylinder piston area) $\approx$ **$5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²** (25 mm bore)
- $M_{hydraulic} = 5.0 / 3.14 \approx$ **1.6×** (hydraulic multiplication alone)

The pedal lever itself provides a further mechanical advantage of **4× to 6×**, so total pedal-to-wheel-cylinder force multiplication is:

$$M_{total} = M_{pedal} \times M_{hydraulic} = 5 \times 1.6 = 8\times$$

Combined with the wheel-cylinder-to-shoe leverage and the brake drum friction, the **driver's foot force of 200 N** can generate a **braking torque of 1500–2500 N·m per wheel**.

## 2.7 Air Brake System Pressure Analysis (Trailer/Heavy Tractor)

For pneumatic brakes, the compressor delivers air to a reservoir at regulated pressure $P_{res}$ (typically **6–8 bar = 600–800 kPa** for farm equipment).

The braking force generated by a diaphragm-actuated chamber:

$$F_{push} = P_{res} \cdot A_{diaphragm} = P_{res} \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D_{d}^{2}}{4}$$

This push-rod force is then amplified by the brake camshaft lever (mechanical advantage $M_{lever} \approx 3$ to $4$) and converted into shoe-expansion force:

$$F_{shoe} = F_{push} \cdot M_{lever}$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Memorise These Equations
> The following table summarises **every key equation** for the Brakes and Braking System topic, organised for rapid KTU exam revision.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \mu \cdot N$ | Friction force, coeff. of friction, normal load | N, —, N | Core brake law |
| 2 | $T = F \cdot r = \mu \cdot N \cdot r$ | Brake torque | N·m | Drum/disc sizing |
| 3 | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy | J | Heat dissipation |
| 4 | $Q = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Heat generated per stop | J | Brake thermal design |
| 5 | $F_{b} = m \cdot a_{decel}$ | Braking force = mass × decel | N | Vehicle dynamics |
| 6 | $a_{decel} = \mu \cdot g \cdot \eta_b$ | Deceleration accounting for efficiency | m/s² | Performance eval |
| 7 | $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_{r} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a_{decel}}$ | Total stopping distance | m | Safety analysis |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{F_{pedal}}{A_{master}}$ | Hydraulic pressure | Pa (or bar) | Hydraulic brake |
| 9 | $M_{hyd} = \dfrac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$ | Hydraulic force multiplication | — | Brake design |
| 10 | $F_{push} = P_{res} \cdot A_{diaph}$ | Air brake push-rod force | N | Pneumatic brake |
| 11 | $\eta_b = \dfrac{a_{actual}}{\mu_{t} g}$ | Braking efficiency | % or fraction | Service rating |
| 12 | $N = \dfrac{T}{\mu \cdot r_{eff}}$ | Required normal force | N | Sizing brake shoe |
| 13 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot A_{liner} \cdot r_{m}}$ | Max pressure on lining (long shoe) | Pa | Lining wear analysis |
| 14 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat dissipation rate (convection) | W | Drum cooling |
| 15 | $t_{stop} = \dfrac{v}{a_{decel}}$ | Time to stop | s | Stop dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT] Notation Warning
> In all KTU board exam scripts, **always define every symbol** before you use it. Examiners award partial marks for **systematic variable definition** even if the final number is incorrect.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Braking Torque for a Single Internal-Expanding Shoe Brake

### Given
- Drum radius $R$ (m)
- Friction lining width $b$ (m)
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ (dimensionless)
- Actuating force on the shoe $F$ (N)
- Geometry parameter $c$ = distance from shoe pivot to drum centre (m), $a$ = half-length of friction lining (m), $h$ = offset of lining from pivot (m).

### To Derive
The self-energising (or self-de-energising) torque produced by the shoe.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The pressure distribution on a short curved shoe is non-uniform due to the moment created by friction. The radial pressure $p(\theta)$ at angle $\theta$ from the shoe centre is governed by the moment-equilibrium equation of the friction element.

For a **short shoe** where lining length $2a \ll c$ (i.e., the lining subtends a small angle), the pressure can be approximated as uniform, and the friction torque is simply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Normal force on lining:} \quad N &= \int p \cdot b \cdot R \, d\theta \approx p \cdot b \cdot 2a \cdot R \\[6pt]
\text{Equating to actuating force:} \quad F \cdot c &= N \cdot h \quad \text{(moment balance about shoe pivot)} \\[6pt]
\text{Solving for } p: \quad p &= \dfrac{F \cdot c}{2 a b R h} \\[6pt]
\text{Friction force on lining:} \quad F_f &= \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot p \cdot b \cdot 2a R = \dfrac{\mu F c}{h} \\[6pt]
\text{Braking torque:} \quad T &= F_f \cdot R = \dfrac{\mu \cdot F \cdot c \cdot R}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{\mu \cdot F \cdot c \cdot R}{h}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU short-shoe brake equation**. It captures the **self-energising effect** — when the friction force creates a moment $F_f \cdot c$ that *assists* the actuating moment $F \cdot c$, the shoe is called a **leading (or self-energising) shoe**, and when it opposes, it is a **trailing (self-de-energising) shoe**.

> [!NOTE] Self-energising → also called "Servo action"
> A leading shoe produces approximately **2×** the torque of a trailing shoe of identical geometry, which is why most drum brake designs place the **leading shoe at the front** (in the direction of drum rotation) and the **trailing shoe at the rear**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stopping Distance for a Tractor Hauling an Implement

### Given
- Tractor mass $m_t = 3000$ kg
- Implement mass $m_i = 1500$ kg
- Initial speed $v_0 = 25$ km/h
- Driver reaction time $t_r = 1.0$ s
- Braking deceleration $a_{decel} = 2.0$ m/s²
- Gradient $\theta = 0°$ (level road)

### Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units:**

$$v_0 = 25 \text{ km/h} = 25 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 6.944 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reaction distance (tractor continues at $v_0$ for time $t_r$):**

$$d_{reaction} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 6.944 \times 1.0 = 6.944 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Braking distance (kinematic equation $v^2 = v_0^2 - 2 a d$, with $v=0$):**

$$d_{braking} = \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \cdot a_{decel}} = \frac{(6.944)^{2}}{2 \times 2.0} = \frac{48.22}{4.0} = 12.06 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Total stopping distance:**

$$d_{stop} = d_{reaction} + d_{braking} = 6.944 + 12.06 = 19.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Stopping time:**

$$t_{stop} = t_r + \frac{v_0}{a_{decel}} = 1.0 + \frac{6.944}{2.0} = 1.0 + 3.472 = 4.47 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Total kinetic energy dissipated (assuming combined mass $m = 4500$ kg):**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_0^{2} = 0.5 \times 4500 \times (6.944)^{2} = 22.5 \times 48.22 = 108{,}500 \text{ J} = 108.5 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Interpretation
> This 108.5 kJ of heat must be absorbed by **four brake drums** (or two drums + two discs). If each drum has mass 20 kg with specific heat $c_p = 500$ J/(kg·°C), the theoretical temperature rise per drum is:
> $\Delta T = \dfrac{108500 / 4}{20 \times 500} = 2.7°$C. Well within safe limits — because tractor speeds are low. **At higher speeds (40 km/h on road) this would quadruple.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Pressure and Force Transmission

### Given
- Master cylinder bore $d_m = 20$ mm $\Rightarrow A_m = \pi d_m^2 / 4 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- Wheel cylinder bore $d_w = 25$ mm $\Rightarrow A_w = \pi d_w^2 / 4 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- Pedal lever ratio $L_1 / L_2 = 5$ (where $L_1$ is the long arm, $L_2$ the short arm)
- Driver foot force $F_{pedal} = 300$ N

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Force on master cylinder piston (after lever mechanical advantage):**

$$F_{master} = F_{pedal} \times \frac{L_1}{L_2} = 300 \times 5 = 1500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Hydraulic pressure generated (Pascal's law):**

$$P = \frac{F_{master}}{A_m} = \frac{1500}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.77 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 47.7 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Force on each wheel cylinder:**

$$F_{wheel} = P \times A_w = 4.77 \times 10^{6} \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} = 2342 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic force multiplication factor:**

$$M_{hyd} = \frac{A_w}{A_m} = \frac{4.909}{3.142} = 1.5625$$

**Step 5 — Total system mechanical advantage:**

$$M_{total} = M_{pedal} \times M_{hyd} = 5 \times 1.5625 = 7.81$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check:** the wheel cylinder force should be:

$$F_{wheel} = M_{total} \times F_{pedal} = 7.81 \times 300 = 2343 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE] Exam Tip
> The numerical consistency check (Step 6) is what examiners look for. **Always show the consistency cross-check** in KTU board exams to earn full marks on derivations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Drum vs Disc Brake

> [!IMPORTANT] Engineering Trade-offs
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** ("Compare drum and disc brakes with respect to construction, performance, heat dissipation, and applications in tractors").

| Parameter | Drum Brake | Disc Brake |
|---|---|---|
| **Friction element** | Internal expanding shoes against drum inner surface | Pads clamped against rotating disc |
| **Self-energising** | Yes (leading shoe gives ~2× torque) | No (purely friction-based) |
| **Heat dissipation** | Poor — heat trapped inside drum | Excellent — disc exposed to air |
| **Fading resistance** | High temperatures cause fade | Excellent anti-fade performance |
| **Wet performance** | Poor (water trapped inside drum) | Good (centrifugal action sheds water) |
| **Brake force for given pedal effort** | Higher (due to self-energising) | Lower (needs servo assist) |
| **Weight** | Heavier (drum adds unsprung mass) | Lighter |
| **Cost & complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Adjustment** | Requires periodic manual shoe adjustment | Self-adjusting in most designs |
| **Tractor application** | **Rear wheels (primary)**, parking brake | **Front wheels (optional)**, modern tractors |
| **Typical life** | 30,000–50,000 km | 60,000–100,000 km |
| **Failure mode** | Drum scoring, ovalisation | Disc warping, pad glazing |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Heat Dissipation from a Brake Drum

The brake drum loses heat by **convection** from its outer surface and by **radiation**. Convection is dominant up to ~300°C, and radiation becomes significant above 400°C.

Convective heat loss rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{conv} = h_c \cdot A_{surface} \cdot (T_{drum} - T_{ambient})$$

**Where:**
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient $\approx$ **30–60 W/(m²·K)** for still air, **80–150 W/(m²·K)** for moving air.
- $A_{surface}$ = lateral cylindrical area of the drum $\approx 2 \pi R_{drum} \cdot L_{drum}$
- $T_{drum}$ = average drum temperature (K)
- $T_{ambient}$ = ambient air temperature (K)

**Numerical example** (drum radius 0.15 m, width 0.08 m, $h_c = 50$, $T_{drum} = 400$ K, $T_{amb} = 308$ K):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{surface} &= 2 \pi \times 0.15 \times 0.08 = 0.0754 \text{ m}^{2} \\[4pt]
\dot{Q}_{conv} &= 50 \times 0.0754 \times (400 - 308) = 50 \times 0.0754 \times 92 \\[4pt]
&= 346.8 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means a single drum can dissipate only **347 W continuously**. For tractor operations requiring continuous braking (e.g., descending a long slope), **water-cooled disc brakes or hydraulic retarders** are used instead.

> [!WARNING] Design Pitfall
> Students often forget that **drum temperature can reach 600°C** during continuous downhill braking — at this point the friction lining begins to **fade** (μ drops from 0.4 to 0.15), and brake failure becomes imminent. This is the famous **"brake fade"** phenomenon.

```mermaid
    nodeEX2["Right Shoe Expander"]
    nodeDRL["Left Brake Drum"]
    nodeDRR["Right Brake Drum"]

    nodePED --> nodeLNK
    nodeLNK --> nodeEQ
    nodeEQ --> nodeTRQ
    nodeTRQ --> nodeBCAM
    nodeBCAM --> nodeEX1
    nodeBCAM --> nodeEX2
    nodeEX1 --> nodeDRL
    nodeEX2 --> nodeDRR
```

> [!NOTE] Reading the Diagrams
> The pedal architecture above shows the **interconnected pedal arrangement** typical of Indian tractors (Mahindra, John Deere, Massey Ferguson). When both pedals are latched together (for road use), both rear brakes act simultaneously. When unlatched (for field use), each pedal operates its respective wheel brake **independently**, enabling the driver to execute a tight "steering by braking" turn.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define a brake. List the essential functional requirements of a tractor braking system.**

> [!IMPORTANT] Model Answer (3 marks)
> **Definition (1 mark):** A brake is a device used to retard or stop a moving vehicle by converting its kinetic energy into heat through friction.
>
> **Essential Requirements (2 marks):**
> 1. The brake must be capable of **stopping the tractor within the specified distance** as per IS 12161, even when the tractor is fully loaded with the heaviest recommended implement.
> 2. The braking system must provide **independent left/right wheel braking** for field operations (turning assistance).
> 3. The system must include a **parking brake** capable of holding the tractor stationary on a **20° gradient** (per ASABE standards).
> 4. The brake must have **adequate heat dissipation capacity** to prevent brake fade during continuous or repeated braking on slopes.
> 5. The system must be **fail-safe** — loss of hydraulic pressure must result in automatic brake application (spring-applied, hydraulically released design).

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the phenomenon of "self-energisation" in a drum brake. Why is it beneficial?**

> [!IMPORTANT] Model Answer (3 marks)
> **Explanation (2 marks):** In an internal expanding drum brake, the friction force generated by the rotating drum on the brake lining creates a frictional moment about the shoe pivot. When this frictional moment acts in the **same direction** as the actuating force moment (i.e., assists the shoe in pressing harder against the drum), the shoe is called a **leading (or self-energising) shoe**. The normal pressure on the lining increases automatically without any additional actuation force.
>
> **Benefit (1 mark):** Self-energisation provides nearly **double the braking torque** for the same pedal effort, which is why leading shoes are placed at the front of the drum in the direction of rotation. This makes the brake highly effective for heavy tractor applications with modest actuation forces.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **hydraulic braking system** used in tractors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The master cylinder of a tractor hydraulic brake has a bore of 22 mm and the wheel cylinder has a bore of 28 mm. If the pedal lever ratio is 5:1 and the driver applies a foot force of 350 N, calculate:
   (i) The hydraulic pressure developed in the system.
   (ii) The force exerted at each wheel cylinder.
   (iii) The total mechanical advantage of the system.
   (iv) The braking torque developed at each wheel, if the effective brake drum radius is 0.18 m and the coefficient of friction between lining and drum is 0.38. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)**

> **Construction (3.5 marks):** A tractor hydraulic braking system consists of the following key components:
>
> 1. **Brake Pedal** — Foot-operated lever mounted in the operator platform. The lever pivots about a fixed point and has a mechanical advantage ratio of typically **4:1 to 6:1**.
> 2. **Master Cylinder** — A cylindrical housing containing a piston, cup seals, return spring, and a reservoir port. As the pedal is depressed, the piston moves forward, pressurising the brake fluid.
> 3. **Brake Fluid Reservoir** — Mounted on top of the master cylinder; supplies fluid to compensate for leakage and thermal expansion.
> 4. **Hydraulic Lines** — Steel or flexible brake hoses connecting the master cylinder to the individual wheel cylinders, forming a **dual-circuit** (tandem) system for safety.
> 5. **Wheel Cylinders** — Located inside each brake drum, they contain opposed pistons that push the brake shoes outward when pressurised.
> 6. **Brake Shoes with Friction Linings** — Curved steel shoes with friction material (semi-metallic or organic compound) bonded to the outer surface. The shoes are pivoted at one end and expanded by the wheel cylinder at the other.
> 7. **Brake Drums** — Cast iron drums bolted to the wheel hubs, providing the friction surface.
> 8. **Return Springs** — Pull the shoes back to the released position when hydraulic pressure is removed.
> 9. **Adjusting Mechanism** — Automatic or manual star-wheel adjuster to compensate for lining wear.

> **Working (3.5 marks):**
> When the driver presses the brake pedal, the lever amplifies the foot force by its mechanical ratio and pushes the master cylinder piston forward. The piston seals the reservoir port and pressurises the brake fluid (typically **DOT-3 or DOT-4 fluid**, glycol-ether based). This pressure is transmitted instantaneously (Pascal's Law) through the hydraulic lines to all four wheel cylinders. Inside each wheel cylinder, the fluid pressure pushes the opposed pistons outward, which in turn push the brake shoes against the inner surface of the rotating brake drum. The friction between the linings and the drum generates a braking torque that opposes wheel rotation. When the pedal is released, the return springs retract the master cylinder piston (opening the reservoir port) and pull the brake shoes back to the resting position, releasing the braking action.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Master cylinder bore $d_m = 22$ mm $= 0.022$ m
- Wheel cylinder bore $d_w = 28$ mm $= 0.028$ m
- Pedal lever ratio $L_1 / L_2 = 5$
- Driver foot force $F_{pedal} = 350$ N
- Effective drum radius $r = 0.18$ m
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.38$

**Step 1 — Force on master cylinder piston (after lever amplification):**

$$F_{master} = F_{pedal} \times \frac{L_1}{L_2} = 350 \times 5 = 1750 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Master cylinder piston area:**

$$A_m = \frac{\pi \cdot d_m^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.022)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4.84 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 3.801 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Hydraulic pressure developed:**

$$P = \frac{F_{master}}{A_m} = \frac{1750}{3.801 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.604 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 46.0 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4 — Wheel cylinder piston area:**

$$A_w = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.028)^{2}}{4} = 6.158 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Force exerted at each wheel cylinder:**

$$F_{wheel} = P \times A_w = 4.604 \times 10^{6} \times 6.158 \times 10^{-4} = 2834 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Total mechanical advantage of the system:**

$$M_{total} = \frac{F_{wheel}}{F_{pedal}} = \frac{2834}{350} = 8.10$$

**Cross-check using the formula:**

$$M_{total} = M_{pedal} \times M_{hydraulic} = 5 \times \frac{A_w}{A_m} = 5 \times \frac{6.158}{3.801} = 5 \times 1.620 = 8.10 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — (iv) Braking torque at each wheel:**

The friction force between lining and drum is:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot F_{wheel} = 0.38 \times 2834 = 1076.9 \text{ N}$$

The braking torque is:

$$T = F_f \cdot r = 1076.9 \times 0.18 = 193.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answer Tabulation:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic pressure | $P$ | **46.0 bar (4.60 MPa)** |
| Force at wheel cylinder | $F_{wheel}$ | **2834 N** |
| Total mechanical advantage | $M_{total}$ | **8.10** |
| Braking torque per wheel | $T$ | **193.8 N·m** |

---

### Question B [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Compare the construction and working of **drum brakes and disc brakes**. Discuss their relative advantages and disadvantages for tractor applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tractor-trailer combination has a total mass of 6500 kg and is moving at 36 km/h on a level road. The brakes are applied with a deceleration of 2.2 m/s². Calculate:
   (i) The total braking force required.
   (ii) The kinetic energy dissipated.
   (iii) The total stopping distance assuming a driver reaction time of 1.2 seconds.
   (iv) The heat generated per brake, if braking is shared equally among 4 wheels. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Drum vs Disc Brake Comparison (7 marks)**

The comparison must be presented in **tabular form** in the answer script for clarity. Key points to cover:

> **Drum Brake (3.5 marks):**
> - **Construction:** A cast iron drum rotates with the wheel. Inside the drum, two curved brake shoes carrying friction linings are mounted on a fixed backing plate. A wheel cylinder or mechanical expander pushes the shoes outward against the drum.
> - **Working:** When actuated, the shoes press against the inner drum surface. The friction torque opposes rotation. Self-energising effect of the leading shoe amplifies the braking force.
> - **Advantages in tractors:** Compact size, high torque due to self-energising, low cost, integrated parking brake function (drum hand-brake mechanism can be built into the same drum).
> - **Disadvantages:** Poor heat dissipation (heat trapped inside drum), prone to brake fade on long descents, water accumulation reduces efficiency in wet field conditions, requires periodic adjustment as lining wears.

> **Disc Brake (3.5 marks):**
> - **Construction:** A ventilated or solid cast iron disc rotor is bolted to the wheel hub. A caliper straddles the disc and contains pistons that press brake pads (linings) against both faces of the disc.
> - **Working:** Hydraulic pressure pushes the pistons, which clamp the pads against the rotating disc. Friction torque opposes rotation. There is no self-energising effect.
> - **Advantages in tractors:** Superior heat dissipation (disc is exposed to air flow), consistent performance under repeated heavy braking, self-cleaning action (centrifugal force throws off water and dust), minimal maintenance, lighter weight.
> - **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, more complex, larger unsprung mass, no self-energising so requires larger hydraulic pressure or servo assist, parking brake integration is more complex (often requires a small drum-in-disc or electric caliper).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Total mass $m = 6500$ kg
- Initial velocity $v_0 = 36$ km/h $= 36 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 10$ m/s
- Deceleration $a_{decel} = 2.2$ m/s²
- Reaction time $t_r = 1.2$ s
- Number of brakes $n = 4$

**Step 1 — (i) Total braking force:**

$$F_{brake} = m \cdot a_{decel} = 6500 \times 2.2 = 14{,}300 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Kinetic energy dissipated:**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_0^{2} = 0.5 \times 6500 \times (10)^{2} = 325{,}000 \text{ J} = 325 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — (iii) Total stopping distance:**

Reaction distance:

$$d_{reaction} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 10 \times 1.2 = 12.0 \text{ m}$$

Braking distance:

$$d_{braking} = \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \cdot a_{decel}} = \frac{(10)^{2}}{2 \times 2.2} = \frac{100}{4.4} = 22.73 \text{ m}$$

Total stopping distance:

$$d_{stop} = d_{reaction} + d_{braking} = 12.0 + 22.73 = 34.73 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — (iv) Heat generated per brake (equal sharing among 4 wheels):**

$$Q_{per\,brake} = \frac{KE}{n} = \frac{325{,}000}{4} = 81{,}250 \text{ J} = 81.25 \text{ kJ}$$

**Final Answer Tabulation:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Total braking force | **14,300 N (14.3 kN)** |
| Kinetic energy dissipated | **325 kJ** |
| Total stopping distance | **34.73 m** |
| Heat per brake | **81.25 kJ** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Where Students Lose Marks
> 1. **Forgetting the reaction distance** — Many students compute only the braking distance and omit the reaction component, losing **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Unit conversion error** — Speeds in km/h must be converted to m/s **before** substituting into the formula $F = ma$ or $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$. Mixing units is the most common error in KTU scripts.
> 3. **No symbol definition** — Every symbol must be defined immediately after first use. Scripts without symbol lists lose **1–2 marks** on definition.
> 4. **Confusing the two mechanical advantages** — In hydraulic brake problems, students often multiply by the pedal ratio twice or by the hydraulic ratio twice. Clearly label each step as "pedal mechanical advantage" and "hydraulic mechanical advantage".
> 5. **Ignoring self-energising in drum brakes** — When asked for the torque of a drum brake with both shoes, students often forget that the **leading shoe produces roughly 2× the torque** of the trailing shoe. The total is **not** simply $2 \times$ the single-shoe torque.
> 6. **Wrong effective radius** — Use the **drum inner radius** for friction torque calculations, not the outer radius or the wheel radius.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist

**Core Concepts (MUST KNOW):**
- [ ] Brake = device to convert kinetic energy into heat via friction
- [ ] Fundamental equation: $F = \mu N$ and $T = \mu N r$
- [ ] Tractor brakes serve **dual function**: safety + steering assist
- [ ] Self-energising (leading) shoe gives ~2× torque of trailing shoe
- [ ] Drum brake: compact, high torque, poor cooling
- [ ] Disc brake: excellent cooling, consistent, no self-energising

**Key Equations (MUST MEMORISE):**
- [ ] $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ — energy to dissipate
- [ ] $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a}$
- [ ] $P = \dfrac{F_{master}}{A_{master}} = \dfrac{F_{wheel}}{A_{wheel}}$ (Pascal's law)
- [ ] $M_{total} = M_{pedal} \times M_{hydraulic}$
- [ ] $F_{push} = P_{res} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$ (air brake)
- [ ] $T = \dfrac{\mu F c R}{h}$ (short shoe brake)

**Practical Numbers to Remember:**
- [ ] Tractor typical deceleration: **1.5–2.5 m/s²**
- [ ] Braking efficiency (good condition): **60–75%**
- [ ] Air brake reservoir pressure: **6–8 bar**
- [ ] Hydraulic brake pressure (tractor): **40–60 bar**
- [ ] Driver reaction time (tractor): **~1.0 second**
- [ ] Tractor parking brake gradient holding capacity: **20° slope**

**KTU Exam Strategy:**
- [ ] Always **define symbols** at the start of every numerical answer.
- [ ] Always **show all steps** — examiners award partial marks.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, expect **3–4 sub-parts** with one numerical.
- [ ] Common choice: Hydraulic brakes (theory) + Drum/Disc comparison OR numerical on stopping distance + hydraulic pressure.
- [ ] Memorise the **master table of formulas** (Section 2.8) for last-minute revision.

**Common Comparison Questions (14-mark favourites):**
- [ ] Drum brake vs Disc brake
- [ ] Hydraulic brake vs Air brake
- [ ] Mechanical brake vs Hydraulic brake
- [ ] Leading shoe vs Trailing shoe
- [ ] Service brake vs Parking brake

> [!TIP] Final KTU Exam Tip
> For **internal choice questions**, always pick the option that has a **numerical sub-part with a clear "calculate" stem**. Numerical questions are easier to score full marks on because the working steps can earn partial credit, whereas descriptive questions require perfect recall of theory. In Module 1, the choice between "Hydraulic brake description" and "Drum/Disc comparison + numerical" should be made based on which suits your strength.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheels
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheels

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of **PEMUT523 (Tractors, Farm Equipments and Special Types of Vehicles)**, a **wheel** is defined as a load-bearing, ground-contact rotating assembly consisting of a **rim**, a **tire (pneumatic or solid)**, a **disc/wheel center**, and a **hub**, mounted on an axle spindle. On a farm tractor, wheels serve two simultaneous functions:

1. **Supporting the static and dynamic weight** of the machine over soft, deformable agricultural soil.
2. **Developing the tractive effort (TE)** required to pull implements, by converting axle torque into useful horizontal pull through tire–soil interaction.

A standard agricultural tractor typically employs **two distinct wheel categories**:
- **Front (steering) wheels** — smaller in diameter, used only for steering guidance.
- **Rear (drive) wheels** — larger in diameter, fitted with lug tires that develop the drawbar pull.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Wheels):**
> The 2024 scheme specifically expects students to differentiate between **pneumatic, solid, and dual wheels**, understand **tire size designation (e.g., 18.4–30)**, explain **tread width adjustment**, describe **ballast and wheel weights**, and analyse **wheel slip & tractive efficiency**. These are the highest-weightage concepts in the syllabus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a person walking on dry sand versus on a paved road.

- On **dry sand**, the foot slips — the person pushes backward, but the sand deforms, and forward motion is lost. This is exactly what happens to a **tractor wheel with insufficient weight or wrong tire type** on loose soil: the tire "spins out" and energy is wasted as heat and soil deformation.
- On **paved road**, the foot (or a rubber shoe) grips firmly, and every push becomes motion. This is what happens when the wheel has the **correct weight, lug pattern, and inflation pressure**.

A tractor wheel is, therefore, the **mechanical handshake between the engine's torque and the earth's soil**. The stronger and smarter this handshake, the more fuel efficiently a tractor can plough, harrow, or sow.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

The following bolded metrics are universally standardized for tractor wheels:

- **Static weight distribution (2WD tractor):** **45% on front axle, 55% on rear axle** (with implement attached it shifts to ~30/70).
- **Standard tire aspect ratio (agriculture):** typically **85 % to 100 %** (near-sectional or wide-section tires).
- **Tire pressure (field operation):** **0.8 – 1.2 bar (12 – 18 psi)** for rear drive tires.
- **Ply rating (PR):** **4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14** for agricultural tires.
- **Coefficient of traction (μ):** **0.4 – 0.6** on firm soil; **0.2 – 0.35** on loose/tilled soil.
- **Wheel slip (optimum for traction):** **8 % – 15 %**; if > 20 %, performance degrades rapidly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter:** In KTU university problems, the value of slip, tractive efficiency, and pull are *always* quoted within these ranges. Memorizing the bolded figures above is non-negotiable for scoring.

### 1.4 Components of a Tractor Wheel (Quick View)

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Tire** | Contact with soil; provides grip and cushioning. |
| 2 | **Rim** | Holds the tire; mounts on the disc. |
| 3 | **Disc / Wheel Centre** | Connects rim to hub; allows tread-width adjustment. |
| 4 | **Hub** | Houses bearings; mounts on axle spindle. |
| 5 | **Lugs (on drive tires)** | Bars protruding from tire face for traction. |
| 6 | **Valve Stem** | For air inflation (pneumatic tires only). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Wheels Used on Tractors and Farm Equipment

#### 2.1.1 Pneumatic (Air-Filled) Wheels
- **Construction:** Rubber tire inflated with compressed air over a steel rim.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Bias-ply** — multiple rubber plies cross diagonally.
  - **Radial-ply** — plies run radially (90° to the tread centreline); gives larger contact patch and longer life.
  - **Tubeless** — air is held by tire–rim bead seal.
- **Advantages:** Shock absorption, higher speed capability, lower soil compaction (at low pressure).
- **Disadvantages:** Puncture risk; requires pressure monitoring.

#### 2.1.2 Solid (Rubber / Polyurethane) Wheels
- **Construction:** Single-piece molded rubber or polyurethane bonded to a steel centre.
- **Used on:** Trailers, transport implements, indoor material-handling vehicles, scrapers.
- **Advantages:** Puncture-proof, low maintenance, high load capacity.
- **Disadvantages:** No shock absorption, transmits full impact to bearings and chassis.

#### 2.1.3 Dual Wheels
- **Construction:** A second complete wheel bolted to the existing drive wheel.
- **Purpose:** To **reduce soil compaction** by distributing the same load over a larger contact area, and to **increase tractive capacity** in soft soil.
- **Used on:** Heavy tractors (> 60 PTO hp) during deep ploughing or puddling.

#### 2.1.4 Cage Wheels (Steel / Straddle Wheels)
- **Construction:** Open cage of steel bars and lugs.
- **Used on:** **Puddling (wetland preparation) of paddy fields** where pneumatic tires would slip excessively. The steel bars dig into the mud to provide grip.
- **Unique feature:** They destroy the soil's permeability barrier, which is desired in puddling.

### 2.2 Wheel Drive Configurations

- **2WD (Two-Wheel Drive):** Only the rear wheels are driven. Simple, low cost, suitable for light operations.
- **4WD / MFWD (Mechanical Front-Wheel Drive):** All four wheels driven. Up to **30 % more tractive efficiency** in field operations.
- **Track (Crawler):** Wheels replaced by endless tracks. Used in very soft soil or steep terrain; not technically a "wheel" but conceptually a wheel-replacement.

### 2.3 Wheel Slip — The Heart of the Theory

> **Wheel slip** is the difference between the theoretical forward speed (calculated from wheel rotation, assuming no soil deformation) and the actual forward speed of the tractor.

**Why slip exists:**
When a torque is applied to a drive wheel, the tire pushes backward on the soil. The soil resists with a friction-like force. The tire *must* move backward relative to the soil slightly to engage that friction. That backward relative movement IS the slip.

**Operational effect:**
- **0 % slip:** Tire is rolling perfectly on hard surface; no torque transfer possible.
- **8 – 15 % slip:** Optimal tractive efficiency.
- **> 25 % slip:** Wasted energy, soil pulverization, fuel waste.

### 2.4 Ballasting of Wheels

**Ballast** is added dead weight to wheels to:
1. Increase downward force → higher soil shear strength → more pull.
2. Counter implement-induced weight transfer.
3. Reduce wheel hop at high draft loads.

Two common ballasting methods:
- **Cast-iron wheel weights** bolted to the rim disc.
- **Water-ballasting** — filling the tire cavity up to 75 % with water (plus antifreeze in cold climates). Water adds ~ 0.4 kg per litre to the wheel.

> A 75 % water-filled rear tire can add **150 – 400 kg per wheel** of ballast.

### 2.5 Tread-Width Adjustment

Agricultural wheels are mounted on **adjustable disc centres** that can be re-bolted in two or more positions on the rim:
- **Narrow tread:** For row-crop work (e.g., cotton, sugarcane) where wheels must pass between rows.
- **Wide tread:** For field ploughing where stability and floatation matter.
- **Standard tread for tractors:** ~ 1.5 m (front), ~ 1.5 – 2.0 m (rear).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in the table to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Symbol | Unit | Remarks |
|-------|----------|----------------------|--------|------|---------|
| 1 | Theoretical velocity | $V_t = \omega \cdot r$ | $V_t$ | m/s | $\omega$ in rad/s, $r$ = rolling radius |
| 2 | Actual velocity | $V_a$ (measured) | — | m/s | From GPS or fifth-wheel |
| 3 | Wheel slip | $S = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100$ | $S$ | % | Optimal: 8 – 15 % |
| 4 | Rolling radius | $r = \dfrac{D + 2H}{2 \cdot 1000}$ approx. | $r$ | m | $D$ = rim dia (mm), $H$ = section height |
| 5 | Angular speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $\omega$ | rad/s | $N$ = rpm |
| 6 | Net tractive force | $F_t = W_d \cdot \mu$ | $F_t$ | N | $W_d$ = dynamic weight on drive wheel |
| 7 | Drawbar pull | $DP = F_t - R_r$ | $DP$ | N | $R_r$ = rolling resistance |
| 8 | Rolling resistance | $R_r = W \cdot \rho$ | — | N | $\rho$ = rolling resistance coefficient |
| 9 | Tractive efficiency | $\eta_t = \dfrac{P_{db}}{P_{axle}} \times 100$ | $\eta_t$ | % | $P_{db}$ = drawbar power |
| 10 | Weight on drive wheel | $W_d = W \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_f}{L}\right)$ | $W_d$ | N | $L_f$ = distance from front axle to CG |
| 11 | Tire size (designation) | 18.4 – 30 | — | inch | 18.4 = section width, 30 = rim dia |
| 12 | Ply rating factor | PR 6, 8, 10 … | — | — | Higher PR = higher load capacity |
| 13 | Tire load capacity | $Q = K \cdot PR \cdot W_s$ | $Q$ | kg | $K$ = empirical constant |
| 14 | Air volume in tire | $V_{air} = \pi \cdot W_s \cdot H \cdot D$ | — | m³ | Approx. for water ballasting |
| 15 | Coefficient of traction | $\mu = \dfrac{DP}{W_d}$ | $\mu$ | — | 0.4 – 0.6 firm soil |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of Wheel Theory in Engineering

1. **Fuel economy in ploughing:** A 5 % reduction in wheel slip (e.g., 18 % → 13 %) can save **up to 15 % diesel** per hectare.
2. **Soil conservation:** Correct tire pressure and dual wheels reduce subsoil compaction, preserving root-zone porosity.
3. **Design of autonomous tractors:** Wheel slip sensors feed back into the electronic control unit (ECU) to modulate implement depth in real time.
4. **Special vehicle design (Module 5 context):** Amphibious and marsh vehicles use wheel-shaped pontoons where the same "wheel theory" applies under buoyancy-stability constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wheel Slip Equation

The slip of a drive wheel is the most fundamental and most-asked derivation under this topic. Below is the complete, exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the rolling (no-slip) reference case.**
If the tire were rolling without any slip, the linear distance the wheel centre travels in one revolution would equal the circumference of the tire:

$$
s_{\text{ideal}} = 2 \pi r
$$

Here, $r$ is the **rolling radius** of the tire (m), measured from the wheel centre to the hard surface — slightly less than the unloaded tire radius because of tire deflection.

**Step 2 — Convert to theoretical (no-slip) velocity.**
If the wheel rotates at an angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s), the *theoretical* velocity of the wheel centre — assuming zero slip — is:

$$
V_t = \omega \cdot r
$$

**Step 3 — Define the actual velocity.**
In reality, the tire slips, so the wheel centre moves slower than $V_t$. The actual forward speed of the tractor is measured experimentally (e.g., fifth wheel, GPS):

$$
V_a = \text{measured forward speed (m/s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Express slip as a normalized relative difference.**
Slip is defined as the *fraction of the theoretical motion lost* to tire deformation and soil shear:

$$
S = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage for engineering use.**

$$
\boxed{\,S \,(\%) = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100\,}
$$

**Logic summary (in words):**
> Numerator = how much motion was "lost" to slip. Denominator = motion that *would have occurred* with no slip. The ratio is dimensionless; multiplying by 100 converts it to percent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Tractive Efficiency ($\eta_t$)

**Step 1 — Define axle power (input at the wheel hub):**
$$
P_{\text{axle}} = T \cdot \omega
$$
where $T$ is the axle torque (N·m) and $\omega$ is the wheel angular velocity (rad/s).

**Step 2 — Define drawbar power (useful output at the hitch):**
$$
P_{\text{db}} = DP \cdot V_a
$$
where $DP$ is the drawbar pull (N) and $V_a$ is the actual forward velocity (m/s).

**Step 3 — Identify the losses:**
- **Slip loss** (energy lost in tire–soil shear).
- **Rolling resistance loss** (energy lost in tire deflection and soil compaction).

The total mechanical power lost is:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{axle}} - P_{\text{db}}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the efficiency ratio:**

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{P_{\text{db}}}{P_{\text{axle}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{DP \cdot V_a}{T \cdot \omega}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of slip.** Since $V_a = V_t(1 - S)$ and $V_t = \omega r$:

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{DP \cdot \omega r (1 - S)}{T \cdot \omega} = \dfrac{DP \cdot r \cdot (1 - S)}{T}
$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed form:**

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_t = \dfrac{DP}{T/r} \cdot (1 - S) = \dfrac{DP}{F_t} \cdot (1 - S)\,}
$$

where $F_t = T/r$ is the total tractive force at the tire contact patch.

**Key insight:** Tractive efficiency peaks when slip $S \approx 8\text{–}12\,\%$ and rolling resistance is minimized.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Weight on Drive Wheel

When an implement is being pulled, weight transfers from the front to the rear axle.

**Step 1 — Static weight on rear wheel (no pull):**
$$
W_{d,\text{static}} = W \cdot \dfrac{L_f}{L}
$$
where:
- $W$ = total tractor weight (N)
- $L_f$ = horizontal distance from front axle to CG (m)
- $L$ = wheelbase (m)

**Step 2 — Weight transfer due to drawbar pull $DP$ applied at hitch height $h$:**
$$
\Delta W = \dfrac{DP \cdot h}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Total dynamic weight on drive wheel:**
$$
\boxed{\,W_d = W \cdot \dfrac{L_f}{L} + \dfrac{DP \cdot h}{L}\,}
$$

**Logic in words:**
> Pulling an implement creates a moment about the CG. This moment loads the rear axle and lifts weight off the front. The formula above is the static distribution plus this dynamic transfer.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A 2WD tractor has a total weight $W = 30\,000$ N. Wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m. Distance from front axle to CG $L_f = 1.2$ m. Hitch height $h = 0.5$ m. The tractor is pulling an implement with a drawbar pull $DP = 8\,000$ N. The rolling radius of the drive wheel is $r = 0.75$ m, and the wheel rotates at $N = 600$ rpm. The actual forward speed is $V_a = 2.4$ m/s. Find:
> (a) Static weight on drive wheel.
> (b) Dynamic weight on drive wheel.
> (c) Wheel slip.
> (d) Tractive efficiency.

#### (a) Static weight on drive wheel

$$
W_{d,\text{static}} = W \cdot \dfrac{L_f}{L} = 30\,000 \times \dfrac{1.2}{2.5}
$$

$$
W_{d,\text{static}} = 30\,000 \times 0.48 = 14\,400 \text{ N}
$$

#### (b) Dynamic weight on drive wheel

First compute the weight transfer:

$$
\Delta W = \dfrac{DP \cdot h}{L} = \dfrac{8\,000 \times 0.5}{2.5} = 1\,600 \text{ N}
$$

Then add to static:

$$
W_d = 14\,400 + 1\,600 = 16\,000 \text{ N}
$$

#### (c) Wheel slip

Theoretical velocity:

$$
V_t = \dfrac{2 \pi N r}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 600 \times 0.75}{60}
$$

$$
V_t = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 450}{60} = 15 \pi \approx 47.124 \text{ m/s}
$$

Slip percentage:

$$
S = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{47.124 - 2.4}{47.124} \times 100
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{44.724}{47.124} \times 100 \approx 94.9 \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The slip is absurdly high because $N = 600$ rpm at $r = 0.75$ m gives a ridiculously high theoretical speed. In real KTU problems, you must first convert everything to consistent units. The above illustrates the *method*; the student should sanity-check inputs. (A more realistic value would be $N = 30$ rpm for 2.4 m/s forward speed.)

#### (d) Tractive efficiency (re-run with corrected $N = 30$ rpm)

Recompute:

$$
V_t = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 30 \times 0.75}{60} = \dfrac{45 \pi}{60} = 2.356 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{2.356 - 2.4}{2.356} \times 100 = -1.86 \%
$$

A *negative* slip indicates the wheel is essentially idling or coasting — meaning $V_a > V_t$, which can occur on a downhill. The magnitude $|S| = 1.86\%$ is reported as **negative slip**.

The corrected $V_t$ gives a normal slip regime; this teaches the student to **always check unit consistency first**.

For a more typical field problem where $V_t = 2.8$ m/s and $V_a = 2.4$ m/s:

$$
S = \dfrac{2.8 - 2.4}{2.8} \times 100 = 14.29 \%
$$

Then:

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{DP \cdot V_a}{T \cdot \omega} = \dfrac{DP \cdot V_a}{F_t \cdot V_t}
$$

If $F_t = W_d \cdot \mu = 16\,000 \times 0.5 = 8\,000$ N:

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{8\,000 \times 2.4}{8\,000 \times 2.8} \times 100 = 85.71 \%
$$

> The tractive efficiency of 85.71 % at 14.29 % slip is a perfectly reasonable KTU result.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Wheel

| Operating Condition | Recommended Wheel Type | Reason |
|---------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Dry, hard soil ploughing | Pneumatic, low pressure (0.8 bar) | Larger contact patch, higher traction |
| Wet paddy puddling | **Cage wheel** | Steel bars grip mud, pneumatic would slip |
| Transport on road (> 25 km/h) | Pneumatic, high pressure (1.6 bar) | Low rolling resistance, less wear |
| Heavy 4WD tractor on soft soil | **Dual pneumatic wheels** | Distribute load, reduce compaction |
| Indoor warehouse trolley | Solid rubber / PU | Puncture-proof, durable |
| Row-crop (cotton, sugarcane) | Pneumatic, narrow tread setting | Passes between rows without crushing plants |
| Dryland farming, light soil | Pneumatic + water ballast | Extra weight → better traction at low slip |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tractor Wheel System

Because a true physical rendering of a wheel is not feasible in Mermaid, the following **block-level functional flow** maps the *engineering interactions* of all wheel-related sub-systems in a modern tractor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Engine Torque Output] --> B[Transmission Gearbox]
    B --> C[Drive Shaft and Differential]
    C --> D[Rear Axle Hub Assembly]
    D --> E[Drive Wheel Sub-system]
    E --> F[Pneumatic Tire with Lugs]
    E --> G[Rim and Disc Centre]
    E --> H[Wheel Weights and Ballast]
    E --> I[Tread Width Adjuster]
    F --> J[Soil Contact Patch]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Slip Sensor]
    J --> L[Traction Force Output]
    K --> M[ECU and Operator Display]
    M --> N[Implement Depth Control]
    N --> B
    L --> O[Drawbar and Towed Implement]
    P[Front Steering Axle] --> Q[Front Wheel Sub-system]
    Q --> R[Steering Angle Actuator]
    R --> S[Steered Direction Vector S]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Force & Slip Balance at the Drive Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Drive Wheel Forces"]
        Wd[Wd = Dynamic Weight on Drive Wheel]
        DP[DP = Drawbar Pull, horizontal]
        Fr[Fr = Rolling Resistance]
        Ft[Ft = Total Tractive Force at Contact Patch]
    end
    A --> B[Force Balance: Ft = DP + Fr]
    A --> C[Slip: S = Vt minus Va divided by Vt times 100]
    A --> D[Tractive Efficiency: eta_t = DP times Va divided by Ft times Vt]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Pneumatic Tire Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Tire[Tire Cross Section] --> T1[Tread with Lugs and Bars]
    Tire --> T2[Sidewall with Ply Rating Marking]
    Tire --> T3[Bead Seat on Steel Rim]
    Tire --> T4[Inner Liner and Air Chamber]
    Tire --> T5[Carcass Plies: Bias or Radial]
    T1 --> T6[Contact Patch with Soil]
    T2 --> T7[Flex Zone absorbs Shock]
    T3 --> T8[Air Tight Seal]
    T4 --> T9[Inflation Pressure psi]
    T5 --> T10[Load Carrying Structure]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Decision Flow for Choosing a Wheel Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Wheel Type] --> Q1{Soil Condition}
    Q1 -->|Dry firm| Q2{Tractor Power hp}
    Q1 -->|Wet puddled| CageWheel[Use Cage Wheel]
    Q1 -->|Soft loose| Q3{Compaction Sensitive}
    Q3 -->|Yes| DualWheel[Use Dual Pneumatic Wheels]
    Q3 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Above 60 hp| WideRim[Wide Rim, Low Pressure Pneumatic]
    Q2 -->|Below 60 hp| StandardRim[Standard Pneumatic with Ballast]
    Q4{Crop Type} -->|Row crop| NarrowTread[Set Narrow Tread Width]
    Q4 -->|Broadcast crop| StandardTread[Set Standard Tread Width]
    CageWheel --> End[Final Configuration]
    DualWheel --> End
    WideRim --> End
    StandardRim --> End
    NarrowTread --> End
    StandardTread --> End
```
