## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define wheel slip in a tractor. State the optimum range of slip for maximum tractive efficiency."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Wheel slip is defined as the relative difference between the theoretical forward velocity of a drive wheel (computed from its angular velocity and rolling radius, assuming zero soil deformation) and the actual forward velocity of the tractor, expressed as a percentage:

$$
S = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100
$$

The **optimum range of wheel slip for maximum tractive efficiency is 8 % to 15 %**. Below this, available traction is under-utilized; above 20 %, the energy wasted in soil pulverisation sharply reduces efficiency. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the function of dual wheels on a heavy tractor. Why are cage wheels used in paddy cultivation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Dual wheels** (two pneumatic wheels bolted side-by-side on the same hub) are used on heavy tractors to **distribute the axle load over a larger soil contact area**, which reduces soil compaction and increases tractive capacity in soft field conditions. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Cage wheels** (steel-lugged open wheels) are used during **paddy puddling** because pneumatic tires slip excessively on saturated mud. The steel lugs dig into the mud, providing positive grip, and their churning action also **seals the puddled soil to reduce percolation**, which is desirable for water-retention in rice fields. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe with neat sketches the constructional features of a pneumatic tractor drive wheel. Explain the significance of tire size designation and ply rating.

> **(b) [7 marks)** A 2WD tractor weighing 28 000 N has a wheelbase of 2.4 m. The centre of gravity lies 1.1 m behind the front axle. The implement is attached 0.45 m above the ground and requires a draft of 7 500 N. The drive-wheel rolling radius is 0.7 m and the wheel rotates at 28 rpm. The actual forward speed is 2.2 m/s. Determine:
> (i) Static and dynamic weight on the drive wheel.
> (ii) Wheel slip.
> (iii) Tractive efficiency, assuming coefficient of traction = 0.5.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Constructional Features of a Pneumatic Drive Wheel (7 marks):**

The pneumatic drive wheel of a tractor consists of the following parts (describe each in 0.5 – 1 mark):

1. **Tire:** Made of natural rubber + synthetic blend. Has deep, angled **lugs (R-1 agricultural tread pattern)** for soil biting. Sidewall shows size designation and ply rating.
2. **Rim:** Heavy-gauge pressed steel, often with a drop-centre profile to ease tire mounting. Sized to the tire (e.g., 30-inch rim for an 18.4-30 tire).
3. **Disc / Wheel Centre:** Welded or riveted to the rim. Has multiple bolt-hole patterns to allow **tread width adjustment** (narrow, medium, wide settings).
4. **Hub:** Cast iron, mounted on the axle spindle, houses tapered-roller bearings.
5. **Lug Bolts and Nuts:** Secure the disc to the hub and the wheel weights to the disc.
6. **Valve Stem:** For inflation; protected by a rubber cap.

**Tire Size Designation (e.g., 18.4 – 30):**
- **18.4** = Section width in inches.
- **30** = Rim diameter in inches.
- A wider section at the same pressure → larger contact patch → higher traction.
- A larger rim → larger overall diameter → higher ground clearance and better rolling-over-obstacles ability.

**Ply Rating (PR):**
- Indicates the tire's load-carrying capacity and recommended inflation pressure.
- Higher PR = more plies (or equivalent) = stiffer sidewall, higher load capacity.
- For a tractor: PR 6, 8, 10 are common.

**(7 marks: 4 for construction + 1.5 for size designation + 1.5 for ply rating.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$W = 28\,000$ N, $L = 2.4$ m, $L_f = 1.1$ m, $h = 0.45$ m, $DP = 7\,500$ N, $r = 0.7$ m, $N = 28$ rpm, $V_a = 2.2$ m/s, $\mu = 0.5$.

**(i) Static and Dynamic Weight on Drive Wheel (3 marks):**

Static:
$$
W_{d,\text{static}} = W \cdot \dfrac{L_f}{L} = 28\,000 \times \dfrac{1.1}{2.4} = 28\,000 \times 0.4583 = 12\,833.3 \text{ N}
$$

Weight transfer:
$$
\Delta W = \dfrac{DP \cdot h}{L} = \dfrac{7\,500 \times 0.45}{2.4} = 1\,406.25 \text{ N}
$$

Dynamic:
$$
W_d = 12\,833.3 + 1\,406.25 = 14\,239.6 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Wheel Slip (2 marks):**

Theoretical velocity:
$$
V_t = \dfrac{2 \pi N r}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 28 \times 0.7}{60} = \dfrac{123.15}{60} \approx 2.052 \text{ m/s}
$$

Slip:
$$
S = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.052 - 2.2}{2.052} \times 100 = -7.21 \%
$$

The negative slip indicates $V_a > V_t$, suggesting coasting or a measurement/data inconsistency. Magnitude = **7.21 %**. (The student should comment on this as a sign of either downhill operation or input-data error. **For valuation: full marks awarded with the comment.**)

**(iii) Tractive Efficiency (2 marks):**

Total tractive force:
$$
F_t = W_d \cdot \mu = 14\,239.6 \times 0.5 = 7\,119.8 \text{ N}
$$

Tractive efficiency (using absolute slip for engineering interpretation, $S = 7.21\% = 0.0721$):
$$
\eta_t = (1 - S) \times 100 = (1 - 0.0721) \times 100 = 92.79 \%
$$

Or via the power method:
$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{DP \cdot V_a}{F_t \cdot V_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{7\,500 \times 2.2}{7\,119.8 \times 2.052} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{16\,500}{14\,609.8} \times 100 \approx 112.93 \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The 112.93 % > 100 % is impossible in reality.** It confirms the data has an internal contradiction (negative slip). The student should have noted this in part (ii) itself. The KTU examiner expects the student to **highlight the inconsistency and then compute $\eta_t$ using the proper slip expression**. Full credit is given if the student identifies this.

A more realistic KTU problem will have $V_t > V_a$. In that case the same formulae apply and give a sensible $\eta_t$ in the 60 – 90 % range.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **tractive efficiency** of a drive wheel. Discuss the effect of wheel slip, ballast, and tire pressure on tractive efficiency.

> **(b) [7 marks]** A 4WD tractor (front and rear equal-sized wheels) operates on firm soil where the coefficient of traction is 0.55. The dynamic weight on each drive axle is 9 000 N. The rolling radius is 0.65 m, axle torque is 2 100 N·m, and forward speed is 2.5 m/s. Calculate the (i) total tractive force, (ii) drawbar pull, assuming rolling resistance coefficient = 0.06, and (iii) tractive efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Tractive Efficiency Block Diagram & Discussion (7 marks):**

(Student is expected to draw a flow diagram similar to the one in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

- Define $\eta_t$ as the ratio of useful drawbar power to axle power.
- **Effect of wheel slip:** $\eta_t$ increases with slip up to ~ 10 %, then decreases. Plot a curve of $\eta_t$ vs $S$.
- **Effect of ballast:** Adding ballast increases $W_d$, which raises tractive force, but only up to the soil-bearing limit. Over-ballasting causes soil compaction and rolling-resistance loss.
- **Effect of tire pressure:** Lower pressure → larger contact patch → less sinkage → less rolling resistance → higher $\eta_t$. But too low pressure causes sidewall flex failure.

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for slip discussion, 1.5 for ballast, 1.5 for tire pressure.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\mu = 0.55$, $W_d$ (per axle) = 9 000 N, so total $W_d = 18\,000$ N, $r = 0.65$ m, $T = 2\,100$ N·m, $V_a = 2.5$ m/s, $\rho = 0.06$.

**(i) Total Tractive Force (2 marks):**
$$
F_t = W_d \cdot \mu = 18\,000 \times 0.55 = 9\,900 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Drawbar Pull (2.5 marks):**

Total weight $W = 18\,000$ N (assumed equally distributed since 4WD).

Rolling resistance:
$$
R_r = W \cdot \rho = 18\,000 \times 0.06 = 1\,080 \text{ N}
$$

Drawbar pull:
$$
DP = F_t - R_r = 9\,900 - 1\,080 = 8\,820 \text{ N}
$$

**(iii) Tractive Efficiency (2.5 marks):**

Theoretical velocity:
$$
V_t = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{T \cdot V_a}{DP \cdot r / V_a} \quad \text{(rearranged)}
$$

Easier route — use the efficiency formula directly:

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{DP}{F_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{8\,820}{9\,900} \times 100 = 89.09 \%
$$

**Final Answer Summary (2 marks):**
- Total tractive force: **9 900 N**
- Drawbar pull: **8 820 N**
- Tractive efficiency: **89.09 %**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to multiply $W_d$ by 2 in a 4WD tractor. The question clearly says "front and rear drive axles."
> - **Common error (–1 mark):** Using $W_d$ instead of total $W$ in the rolling resistance formula.
> - **Common error (–1 mark):** Forgetting units of $W_d$ (must be Newtons, not kgf or kN).
> - **Common error (–2 marks):** Calculating $V_t$ and then using the slip formula when the question only asks for efficiency — a unit penalty is applied for extraneous time spent but **not** for the extra step. However, **report the slip value too** if you have computed it, for the examiner's benefit.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on the topic "Wheels" (Module 1):**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing rpm with rad/s without conversion. Always convert.
> 2. **Sign of slip** — forgetting that $S$ can be negative (coasting); absolute values only after analysis.
> 3. **Static vs dynamic weight** — using static weight when drawbar pull is present; this gives inflated tractive force and incorrect efficiency.
> 4. **Tire size confusion** — confusing section width (in inches) with section height; confusing rim diameter with overall diameter.
> 5. **Missing block diagrams** — KTU explicitly awards 1–2 marks for labelled diagrams in long-answer questions. Skipping the diagram costs you directly.
> 6. **Pneumatic vs solid vs cage wheel** — students often write "cage wheel" when they mean "pneumatic" or vice-versa. Be precise.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A tractor has **front (steering) + rear (drive)** wheels; 2WD vs 4WD is the drive configuration.
- ✅ **Pneumatic wheels** are the most common; **solid wheels** for puncture-proof use; **dual wheels** for heavy loads; **cage wheels** for wet paddy puddling.
- ✅ **Tire size designation** is *width – rim diameter* in inches (e.g., **18.4 – 30**).
- ✅ **Ply rating (PR)** indicates load capacity and required inflation pressure.
- ✅ **Tread width** is adjustable via the disc-bolt pattern (narrow, standard, wide).
- ✅ **Ballast** = cast-iron weights or water inside the tire; increases $W_d$ → increases traction.
- ✅ **Wheel slip formula:** $S = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100$, optimum 8 – 15 %.
- ✅ **Tractive efficiency:** $\eta_t = \dfrac{DP \cdot V_a}{F_t \cdot V_t} \times 100 \approx \dfrac{DP}{F_t} \times 100$ when using $F_t$ computed from $W_d$ and $\mu$.
- ✅ **Dynamic weight on drive wheel:** $W_d = W \cdot \dfrac{L_f}{L} + \dfrac{DP \cdot h}{L}$.
- ✅ **Coefficient of traction:** 0.4 – 0.6 (firm soil), 0.2 – 0.35 (loose/tilled soil).
- ✅ **Rolling resistance coefficient** $\rho \approx 0.06 – 0.12$ for pneumatic tires on agricultural soil.
- ✅ Lower tire pressure → larger contact patch → less compaction → higher $\eta_t$.
- ✅ Always include a **labelled block diagram** in long-answer questions — it is a guaranteed 1–2 marks.
- ✅ Always **sanity-check** $V_t > V_a$ for valid slip values; flag negative slip explicitly.
- ✅ **4WD** gives up to **30 % higher tractive efficiency** in field operations.
- ✅ Cage wheels **seal the puddled soil** — that's a feature, not a bug, of their design.
- ✅ Tire pressure for fieldwork: **0.8 – 1.2 bar (12 – 18 psi)** for rear drive tires.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rims
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rims

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **rim** is the outer circular metal component of a wheel that forms the well or base on which the tire bead is seated and retained. In the context of tractors, farm equipment, and special-purpose off-highway vehicles, the rim is a critical load-bearing structural member that transfers the vehicle's weight, dynamic loads, and traction forces between the tire and the axle hub. The rim is typically an interchangeable, standardized component manufactured to specifications defined by bodies such as the Tire and Rim Association (TRA), ETRTO (European Tyre and Rim Technical Organisation), and ISO (International Organization for Standardization).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 1 of PEMUT523, the term "Rims" refers specifically to the **wheel rims used in tractors and farm machinery**, with emphasis on:
> - **Constructional types** (solid disc, divided/spoke, and demountable rims)
> - **Rim contours** (DW, TW, SW designations for agricultural tires)
> - **Adjustable track-width rims** (since tractors must adapt to row-crop spacing)
> - **Special rims for dual-wheel systems** and heavy implements

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are wearing a wristwatch. The **face of the watch** is like the wheel disc, but the **metal ring (bezel) that holds the watch glass in place** is exactly like a rim. Just as that bezel must be strong enough to clamp the glass without breaking it under pressure, an automotive/tractor rim must be strong enough to hold the tire bead tightly against the wheel disc, even when the tire is inflated to high pressures (typically **20-30 psi for tractor rear tires**) and carrying loads of several tonnes.

**For tractors specifically**, a rims role is even more interesting. Unlike a car rim that sits on a fixed axle, a tractor rim is often **reversible and adjustable** — a farmer can literally slide the rim inboard or outboard on the axle to change the tractor's track width, allowing the same tractor to be used for different crops (e.g., narrow for sugarcane, wide for rice paddy). This adjustability is a defining feature of agricultural rims.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Rim Diameter ($D$)**: Standard sizes include 16", 20", 24", 28", 30", 34", 38" (in inches) for tractors.
> - **Rim Width ($W$)**: Usually between **W6 to W15** for tractors (W = width in inches).
> - **Material**: Hot-rolled or cold-rolled steel sheets (thickness **3-6 mm**), or cast steel/iron.
> - **Operating Load per Rim (Tractor Rear)**: Up to **2000-3000 kg per wheel** under field conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tractor / Farm Equipment Rims

Tractor rims are classified based on **construction, mounting style, and adjustability**. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for KTU exam questions.

#### A. By Construction Type

- **Solid Disc Rim (One-Piece Rim):**
  - The rim and disc are formed as a single integral unit.
  - Used in **light tractors, power tillers, and trailers**.
  - Simple, cheap, but not adjustable for track width.

- **Divided Rim (Two-Piece / Split Rim):**
  - The rim consists of two halves bolted together at the wheel center.
  - Allows easy tire mounting and demounting.
  - **Disadvantage**: Bolts may corrode and fail in muddy field conditions.

- **Demountable Rim (Multi-Piece Rim):**
  - Consists of a **base rim, a side ring, and a lock ring** (3-piece) or with a gutter channel.
  - The side ring and lock ring can be removed for tire service.
  - **Industry standard** for medium and heavy tractors.

#### B. By Rim Contour (Tire & Rim Association Standard)

This is a **very high-yield KTU topic** — examiners frequently test this.

- **DW (Deep Well)**: Standard contour for tractor drive tires (rear). The "well" gives extra room for the tire bead during mounting.
- **TW (Tractor Well)**: Tapered well, used for certain tractor tire sizes.
- **SW (Shallow Well)**: For light agricultural equipment, trailers, and front tractor tires.
- **W (Flat/Well)**: Generic designation where the number following (e.g., **W12**, **W7**) indicates the rim width in inches.

#### C. By Adjustability (Track-Width Rims)

- **Fixed Rims:** Welded or fixed-bolt pattern; no track adjustment.
- **Adjustable Rims:** Use a **slotted hub or reversible disc** so the rim can be flipped (in-to-out) to change the track width by **2 × offset distance** (typically **100-300 mm** per side).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Rim Functions

- **Step 1 — Load Transfer:** The tire (inflated with air) carries the vehicle weight. This weight pushes the tire bead radially outward.
- **Step 2 — Bead Seating:** The rim's well (DW/TW/SW contour) provides a **flange** against which the tire bead is forced by inflation pressure.
- **Step 3 — Centering:** The bead seat (tapered or straight) on the rim **centers the tire** on the wheel and prevents wobble at speed.
- **Step 4 — Torque Transfer:** When the engine drives the wheel, torque passes from the **axle → hub bolts → disc → rim base → tire → ground**.
- **Step 5 — Steering / Steering Angle (Front Wheels):** For front wheels, the rim must allow the wheel to pivot around the kingpin without binding.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Do NOT use the `|` symbol inside any table row. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation / Symbol | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tire + Rim Outer Diameter | $D_{total} = D_{rim} + 2 \cdot H_{tire\,section}$ | mm | Overall vehicle clearance |
| 2 | Rim Width Designation | $W = \text{Width in inches}$ (e.g., W12 = 12") | inches | Tire-rim compatibility |
| 3 | Rim Load Capacity | $W_{load} = P_{inflation} \cdot A_{contact}$ | kg or N | Field load limits |
| 4 | Inflation Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F_{load}}{A_{contact\,patch}}$ | kPa or psi | Field inflation guide |
| 5 | Adjustable Track Width | $T_{new} = T_{old} \pm 2 \cdot d_{offset}$ | mm | Crop-row adaptation |
| 6 | Rim Offset | $O = \dfrac{D_{rim} - d_{hub}}{2}$ | mm | Hub-to-tire centering |
| 7 | Bolt Circle (PCD) | $d_{PCD} = \text{pitch circle dia. of studs}$ | mm | Hub-rim compatibility |
| 8 | Bead Seat Angle | $\theta_{bead} = 5°$ or $15°$ typical | degrees | Bead retention |
| 9 | Allowable Radial Load | $F_{rad} \le \sigma_{yield} \cdot t \cdot L$ | N | Rim strength check |
| 10 | Recommended Inflation | $P_{rec} = \dfrac{2 \cdot W_{axle}}{n \cdot A_{tire}}$ | kPa | Multi-tire systems |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the field, the rim choice directly affects:
- **Soil Compaction:** Wider rims → wider tires → reduced soil compaction (preserves crop yield).
- **Fuel Efficiency:** Correct rim-tire match reduces rolling resistance by up to **8-12%**.
- **Tractor Stability:** Wrong rim offset shifts the center of gravity, increasing rollover risk on slopes.
- **Crop Compatibility:** Track-width adjustment via reversible rims lets one tractor serve **multiple row-crop spacings** (e.g., 60 cm, 75 cm, 90 cm).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Wheel Diameter from Rim and Tire Dimensions

**Problem Context:** A tractor is fitted with a **W12 × 28** rim (12 inches wide, 28 inches diameter) and a tire marked **16.9/14-28**. The "16.9" denotes the tire section width in inches, and "28" matches the rim diameter. Find the **overall static diameter** of the wheel assembly.

#### Step 1: Identify Given Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{rim} &= 28 \text{ in} \\
W_{tire\,section} &= 16.9 \text{ in} \\
\text{Tire Aspect Ratio (computed from 16.9/14-28)} &\Rightarrow H_{section} \approx \dfrac{14}{100} \cdot W_{tire} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In tractor tire nomenclature "16.9/14-28", the 14 is the rim diameter in inches. The "16.9" is the section width. The aspect ratio is **approximately 85% for R-1 agricultural tires**.

#### Step 2: Compute Tire Section Height

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{tire\,section} &= 0.85 \cdot W_{tire\,section} \\
&= 0.85 \cdot 16.9 \\
H_{tire\,section} &= 14.365 \text{ in}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Total Wheel Diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= D_{rim} + 2 \cdot H_{tire\,section} \\
&= 28 + 2 \cdot (14.365) \\
D_{total} &= 28 + 28.73 \\
D_{total} &= 56.73 \text{ in}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Convert to SI Units (KTU Preferred)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total\,(mm)} &= 56.73 \cdot 25.4 \\
D_{total\,(mm)} &= 1440.94 \text{ mm} \approx 1.44 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Analytical Commentary
This diameter (~1.44 m) is typical for a **45 HP agricultural tractor** with rear tire size 16.9-28. The large diameter is necessary to provide high tractive effort at low slip — a defining feature of tractor rear wheels.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Adjustable Track Width Using Reversible Rims

**Problem Context:** A tractor currently has its rear wheels set to a track width of **$T_{old} = 1500 \text{ mm}$**. The rims are reversible, with an offset of **$d_{offset} = 150 \text{ mm}$**. The farmer wants the track width to be **1800 mm** for row-crop work. Find the new configuration and confirm the change in offset direction.

#### Step 1: Apply the Track Width Adjustment Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{new} &= T_{old} \pm 2 \cdot d_{offset} \\
1800 &= 1500 \pm 2 \cdot 150 \\
1800 &= 1500 \pm 300
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Solve the Two Cases

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Case A (Outboard flip):} \quad T_{new} &= 1500 + 300 = 1800 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \\
\textbf{Case B (Inboard flip):} \quad T_{new} &= 1500 - 300 = 1200 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(rejected, doesn't match requirement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Conclusion and Engineering Interpretation

- The rims must be **flipped outboard** (taller side of the disc facing outward).
- The new track width $T_{new} = 1800 \text{ mm}$ matches the **row spacing of cotton or soybean** (~90 cm row spacing × 2 rows).
- A common practical insight: with $d_{offset} = 150 \text{ mm}$, one rim reversal gives **300 mm change in total track width**.

#### Step 4: Cross-Check with the Inverse Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{offset} &= \dfrac{T_{new} - T_{old}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{1800 - 1500}{2} \\
d_{offset} &= 150 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(matches the given value, confirming correctness)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rim Load-Bearing Capacity (Analytical)

**Problem Context:** A tractor rear rim (W12 × 28) is loaded with a vertical force of **$F_{load} = 2500 \text{ kgf}$**. The tire's contact patch has an area of **$A_{contact} = 0.18 \text{ m}^2$**. Find the required inflation pressure.

#### Step 1: Convert Load to Newtons

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{load} &= 2500 \cdot 9.81 \\
F_{load} &= 24525 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Inflation Pressure Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{F_{load}}{A_{contact}} \\
P &= \dfrac{24525}{0.18} \\
P &= 136250 \text{ Pa} \\
P &= 136.25 \text{ kPa} \approx 19.8 \text{ psi}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Compare with TRA Recommended Pressure

The Tire and Rim Association (TRA) recommends a **rear tire inflation of 12-20 psi for agricultural tractors** under field work. Our calculated value of **19.8 psi** falls within the upper end, indicating the tractor is operating near its maximum rear-axle load. This is consistent with **deep-tillage or heavy ploughing operations**.

#### Step 4: Compute Load on Each Rim (Dual Wheel Configuration)

If the tractor uses **dual rear wheels** (2 rims per side):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{per\,rim} &= \dfrac{F_{load}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{2500}{2} \\
F_{per\,rim} &= 1250 \text{ kgf} \quad \text{(safe for the rim material, which typically handles up to 2000 kgf)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Rim-Type Selection for Tractor Applications

| Tractor Type / Application | Recommended Rim | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Tiller / Walking Tractor** | Solid Disc (SW6 × 12) | Light load, low cost, no adjustment needed |
| **Compact Tractor (≤25 HP)** | Divided Rim (W8 × 16) | Easy tire service, moderate load |
| **Standard Tractor (30-50 HP)** | Demountable (W12 × 28) | Adjustable track, handles ploughing load |
| **Heavy Tractor (≥60 HP)** | Demountable (W15 × 34) | High tractive effort, dual-wheel capable |
| **Row-Crop Tractor** | Reversible Demountable | Track-width adjustment for various row spacings |
| **Combine Harvester** | Heavy-Duty DW (DW20 × 26) | High cyclic loading from ground unevenness |
| **Trailer / Trolley** | Solid Disc (W7 × 16) | Static loading, infrequent service |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rim Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Rims for Tractors and Farm Equipment]
    nodeB[By Construction]
    nodeC[Solid Disc Rim]
    nodeD[Divided Rim Two Piece]
    nodeE[Demountable Rim Multi Piece]
    nodeF[By Contour Standard]
    nodeG[SW Shallow Well]
    nodeH[DW Deep Well]
    nodeI[TW Tapered Well]
    nodeJ[By Track Width]
    nodeK[Fixed Rim]
    nodeL[Reversible Rim]
    nodeM[Offset Slotted Hub]
    nodeN[By Application]
    nodeO[Drive Wheels Rear]
    nodeP[Steering Wheels Front]
    nodeQ[Implement Wheels]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeJ
    nodeA --> nodeN
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeQ
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional View of a Demountable Rim (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    hub1[Axle Hub]
    bolt1[Hub Bolt]
    disc1[Wheel Disc]
    baserim1[Base Rim]
    well1[DW Well Contour]
    flange1[Outer Flange]
    sidemember1[Side Ring]
    lockring1[Lock Ring]
    bead1[Tire Bead]
    tire1[Tire Body]

    hub1 --> bolt1
    bolt1 --> disc1
    disc1 --> baserim1
    baserim1 --> well1
    well1 --> flange1
    flange1 --> sidemember1
    sidemember1 --> lockring1
    lockring1 --> bead1
    bead1 --> tire1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block flow above represents the **load and assembly path** of a typical 3-piece demountable rim. Starting from the axle hub, the load travels through hub bolts → wheel disc → base rim → DW well → outer flange → side ring → lock ring → tire bead → tire body → ground. Each block represents a critical structural element that must be inspected during field service.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tractor Wheel Assembly

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph LOAD TRANSFER CHAIN
        loadbox1[Axle Load Input]
        loadbox2[Hub and Bearings]
        loadbox3[Wheel Disc and Bolts]
        loadbox4[Rim Base]
        loadbox5[Rim Flange and Bead Seat]
        loadbox6[Tire Bead]
        loadbox7[Ground Contact Patch]
    end

    loadbox1 --> loadbox2 --> loadbox3 --> loadbox4 --> loadbox5 --> loadbox6 --> loadbox7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The rim is the **interface block** between the mechanical (hub/disc) and pneumatic (tire) sub-systems. A failure in this block — such as a cracked flange or corroded lock ring — can cause a catastrophic tire blowout, especially in **high-load field operations like deep ploughing or harvester operation at full bin**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Define a "rim" as used in a tractor wheel. List any two standard rim contours used for agricultural tractor drive tires.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **rim** is the outer metal circular component of a wheel on which the tire is mounted and retained. It forms the bead seat and provides the structural interface between the hub/disc and the tire.
>
> Two standard rim contours used for agricultural tractor drive (rear) tires are:
>
> 1. **DW (Deep Well)** — provides a deeper well for the tire bead, standard for tractor rear tires.
> 2. **TW (Tapered Well)** — has a tapered bead seat for certain tractor tire sizes.
>
> *(Award full 3 marks for definition + 2 contour names with brief purpose.)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]

**Q: Why are tractor rear rims often designed to be "reversible" or "adjustable"? State the practical benefit with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Tractor rear rims are made reversible/adjustable to enable **track-width adjustment** so the same tractor can be used for crops with different row spacings.
>
> **Example:** A tractor fitted with reversible rims of **150 mm offset** can change its rear track width from **1500 mm to 1800 mm** by simply flipping the rims. This allows the tractor to be configured for crops like **sugarcane (90 cm row spacing)** or **cotton (75 cm row spacing)** without buying new wheels.
>
> *(Award 2 marks for the reason, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse rim diameter with rim width** in the designation (e.g., W12 × 28: W12 = width 12", 28 = diameter 28"). Marks are deducted if these are swapped.
> - In derivation questions, **forgetting unit conversion** (inches ↔ mm) costs 1-2 marks. Always show the conversion step.
> - Do **not** confuse "rim offset" with "wheel offset." The rim offset is a property of the rim-disc interface, not the wheel-to-hub position.
> - In short-answer questions, examiners expect the **standard abbreviation** (DW, TW, SW) along with its full form.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**A tractor is fitted with rear rims designated W12 × 28. The tire section width is 16.9 inches and the aspect ratio is 85%.**

**(a) (7 marks)** Calculate the **total static diameter** of the wheel assembly in mm. Hence determine the **rolling radius** of the wheel (assume rolling radius = 0.95 × static radius).

**(b) (7 marks)** The tractor is being reconfigured for row-crop work. The original rear track width is **1500 mm** and the rims are reversible with an offset of **150 mm**. Calculate the **new track width** if the rims are flipped outboard. If the rim offset were instead 100 mm, by how much would the track width change compared to the 150 mm offset case?

---

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Identify the given data (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rim designation} &= W12 \times 28 \\
\Rightarrow W_{rim} &= 12 \text{ in (width)},\quad D_{rim} = 28 \text{ in (diameter)} \\
W_{tire\,section} &= 16.9 \text{ in} \\
\text{Aspect Ratio} &= 85\% = 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate tire section height (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{section} &= 0.85 \times 16.9 \\
H_{section} &= 14.365 \text{ in}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate total static diameter (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= D_{rim} + 2 \cdot H_{section} \\
D_{total} &= 28 + 2 \times 14.365 \\
D_{total} &= 28 + 28.73 \\
D_{total} &= 56.73 \text{ in}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to mm (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total\,(mm)} &= 56.73 \times 25.4 \\
D_{total\,(mm)} &= 1440.94 \text{ mm} \approx 1441 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate rolling radius (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{static} &= \dfrac{D_{total}}{2} = \dfrac{1441}{2} = 720.5 \text{ mm} \\
R_{rolling} &= 0.95 \times R_{static} \\
R_{rolling} &= 0.95 \times 720.5 \\
R_{rolling} &= 684.5 \text{ mm} \approx 0.685 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Apply the track width equation for 150 mm offset (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{new,150} &= T_{old} + 2 \cdot d_{offset} \\
T_{new,150} &= 1500 + 2 \times 150 \\
T_{new,150} &= 1500 + 300 \\
T_{new,150} &= 1800 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the same equation for 100 mm offset (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{new,100} &= T_{old} + 2 \cdot d_{offset} \\
T_{new,100} &= 1500 + 2 \times 100 \\
T_{new,100} &= 1500 + 200 \\
T_{new,100} &= 1700 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the difference (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{new,150} - T_{new,100} \\
\Delta T &= 1800 - 1700 \\
\Delta T &= 100 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation (1 mark)**

> The 100 mm offset rim gives a track width of **1700 mm**, which is **100 mm narrower** than the 1800 mm achieved with the 150 mm offset. A farmer selecting the 100 mm offset is better suited for **narrower row crops** (e.g., wheat at 15-20 cm row spacing consolidated into multi-row beds) or for **orchard work** requiring a narrower machine profile.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **classification of tractor rims** based on construction, contour, and adjustability. State at least **two applications** for each type.

**(b) (7 marks)** A tractor's rear rim is loaded with **$F_{load} = 2200 \text{ kgf}$**. The tire contact patch area is **$A = 0.16 \text{ m}^2$**. If the tractor uses **dual rear wheels** (2 rims per side), calculate:
- (i) The **required inflation pressure** in kPa and psi.
- (ii) The **load carried by each rim** in kgf and N.
- (iii) Comment on whether the inflation pressure is suitable for **field tillage operations** (typical range: 12-20 psi).

---

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**

> Tractor rims are classified into three major categories based on the engineering requirements of farm machinery: (1) Construction, (2) Contour, and (3) Adjustability.

**Step 2: Construction-based classification (2 marks)**

- **Solid Disc Rim:** Used in power tillers, walking tractors, light trailers.
- **Divided Rim (Two-Piece):** Used in light to medium tractors; allows tire service.
- **Demountable Rim (Multi-Piece):** Used in standard and heavy tractors; allows field service without special tools.

**Step 3: Contour-based classification (2 marks)**

- **DW (Deep Well):** Tractor rear drive tires; allows bead slip during mounting.
- **TW (Tapered Well):** Specific tractor tire sizes; tapered for better retention.
- **SW (Shallow Well):** Front tires, trailers, implements.

**Step 4: Adjustability classification (1 mark)**

- **Fixed Rims:** No track-width change; used in transport and standard applications.
- **Reversible Rims:** Allow track-width adjustment by flipping the disc; used in row-crop tractors.

**Step 5: Block Diagram (1 mark)**

> [Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 — the student is expected to draw a similar tree/hierarchy showing the three classification bases and at least two examples under each.]

---

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Convert load to Newtons (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{load} &= 2200 \times 9.81 = 21582 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate inflation pressure (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{F_{load}}{A_{contact}} \\
P &= \dfrac{21582}{0.16} \\
P &= 134887.5 \text{ Pa} \\
P &= 134.89 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to psi (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{(psi)} &= \dfrac{P_{(kPa)}}{6.895} \\
P_{(psi)} &= \dfrac{134.89}{6.895} \\
P_{(psi)} &= 19.56 \text{ psi} \approx 19.6 \text{ psi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Load per rim (dual wheel) (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{per\,rim} &= \dfrac{F_{load}}{2} = \dfrac{2200}{2} = 1100 \text{ kgf} \\
F_{per\,rim\,(N)} &= 1100 \times 9.81 = 10791 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Suitability comment (1 mark)**

> The calculated inflation pressure of **19.6 psi** falls **within the upper end of the typical field-tillage range (12-20 psi)**. This indicates the tractor is operating under **near-maximum field load**, which is consistent with **deep ploughing, disc harrowing, or sub-soiling**. The rim load of 1100 kgf is well within the safe capacity of a standard W12 × 28 rim (rated ~2000 kgf). **Hence the configuration is suitable** for the operation, though operators should monitor for tire slip and soil compaction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Rims" Module 1**

- **Definition:** A rim is the outer metal component of a wheel that retains the tire bead. Not to be confused with the **wheel disc** (the central plate).
- **Three construction types:** Solid Disc, Divided (Two-Piece), Demountable (Multi-Piece).
- **Three contour types:** **SW** (Shallow Well) for front/implements, **DW** (Deep Well) for rear tractor, **TW** (Tapered Well) for specific sizes.
- **Rim designation "W12 × 28":** W = well-base, 12 = width in inches, 28 = diameter in inches.
- **Tractor rear rims are typically W12 to W15 in width and 24" to 38" in diameter.**
- **Reversible rims** allow **track-width adjustment** for row-crop work; formula: $T_{new} = T_{old} \pm 2 \cdot d_{offset}$.
- **Standard rear tire pressure:** 12-20 psi (83-138 kPa) for tractors in field operations.
- **Material:** Hot-rolled steel (most common), cast steel/iron (heavy-duty), aluminum alloy (rare, premium tractors).
- **Bead seat angle:** Typically **5°** for agricultural tires.
- **Dual wheels** are used to **reduce soil compaction** and increase tractive effort in soft soil.
- **Total wheel diameter** $D_{total} = D_{rim} + 2 \cdot H_{tire}$ (H = tire section height ≈ 0.85 × tire width for R-1 ag tires).
- **Rolling radius ≈ 0.95 × Static radius** (this factor is empirical and widely accepted in KTU textbook references).
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing rim offset (rim-to-disc distance) with wheel offset (wheel-to-hub distance).
- **Always convert inches to mm** using **1 inch = 25.4 mm** when working in SI units.
- **One mark is reserved for unit consistency** in derivation problems — never skip the conversion step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyres and Accessories of Wheeled Tractors and Crawler Tractors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tyres and Accessories of Wheeled Tractors and Crawler Tractors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Tractor Tyres — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tyre (Pneumatic Tyre for Agricultural Tractor):** A flexible, air-pressurized, toroidal elastomeric component mounted on the wheel rim of a tractor, designed to transmit driving effort (tractive force) to the soil, support the static and dynamic weight of the machine, cushion it against ground irregularities, and provide steering control with minimum soil compaction.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a tractor tyre is a *power-transmitting soil-engaging element* that replaces the rigid steel wheel of early tractors. It is the only part of the tractor in direct contact with the ground, and therefore it governs **traction, fuel efficiency, soil compaction, ride comfort, and field speed**.

### 1.2 Crawler Tractor Accessories — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Crawler (Track) Tractor:** A tracked vehicle in which propulsion and steering are achieved through an endless flexible chain (track) wrapped around driving sprockets, idlers, and supported by a system of rollers — the *track assembly* being collectively referred to as the "crawler accessories" of the tractor.

The "accessories" here include the track frame, track shoes, track pins, bushings, sprockets, idler wheels, track rollers, carrier rollers, and the track-tensioning mechanism. They collectively convert engine torque into drawbar pull on soft, wet, or paddy-field soils where pneumatic tyres would fail.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — The Shoe Analogy for Tyres:** Think of tractor tyres as the **shoes of a farmer working in a paddy field**. A soft rubber sole with deep tread (lug) acts like a studded football boot — it "bites" into soft soil without sinking. Ballast added to the tyre is like a heavy backpack; it presses the shoe harder into the mud, giving a stronger grip. Too little weight = slipping (wasted fuel); too much weight = sinking deep (soil compaction). The art of tractor ballasting is choosing the *right shoe weight* for the *right soil*.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — The Tank vs. Mountain-Bike Analogy for Crawlers:** A mountain bike on sand digs in and stops. A tank moves easily on the same sand. Why? The tank spreads its weight over a long, wide metal **belt** (track), so the pressure on the ground is *very small*. A crawler's track works exactly like this belt. Every link in the track is like a small flat "foot" that briefly touches the ground, sharing the tractor's weight. A wheeled tractor concentrates all that weight on four small contact patches — which is why crawlers win on soft, wet soils.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in This Module

- **Tyre inflation pressure (kPa / psi):** typically **80 – 200 kPa** for farm drive tyres.
- **Ply Rating (PR):** a strength index; common tractor drive tyres are **6 PR, 8 PR, 10 PR**.
- **Tyre section width (mm):** for drive tyres, typically **300 – 600 mm**.
- **Overall diameter (mm):** for drive tyres, typically **1200 – 1900 mm**.
- **Static loaded radius (mm):** deflection-dependent, used in tractive-effort equations.
- **Specific soil pressure of a crawler (kPa):** typically **30 – 50 kPa**, which is roughly **5 – 8 times lower** than a wheeled tractor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tractor Tyres (By Position)

| Position | Function | Common Designation (KTU) | Example Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Front — Steering (Steer Tyre)** | Steering control, small traction | Rib (F-2) or Implement (I-1) | 7.50 – 16 |
| **Rear — Drive Tyre** | Transmits tractive effort to soil | Agricultural Lug (R-1), Rice (R-2), Turf (R-3), Industrial (R-4) | 18.4 – 38, 16.9 – 28 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** R-1 (regular agricultural tread) is the *standard drive tyre* syllabus expects you to identify. R-2 (deep lug, for wet paddy) and R-4 (industrial, for construction) are commonly confused — remember the *2 in R-2 = paddy* (more aggressive).

### 2.2 Tyre Construction — The 'Why' Behind Each Component

- **Carcass (Ply):** Layers of rubberised nylon/polyester cords. *Why?* The cords carry inflation pressure and resist punctures.
  - **Bias (Diagonal) Ply:** cords cross at **30° – 45°** to the centreline. Stiffer sidewall, cheaper, traditional.
  - **Radial Ply:** cords run **90°** to the centreline, with steel-belted stabiliser. Greater contact patch (~25% larger), lower rolling resistance, longer tread life, more expensive.
- **Bead:** Steel wire ring that seats the tyre onto the rim. *Why?* Holds air pressure against rim flange.
- **Sidewall:** Protects carcass; carries size, ply rating, brand markings. *Why?* Flexes on deflection, absorbs shocks.
- **Tread & Lug:** The lug is the raised bar that pushes against the soil. *Why?* Generates horizontal tractive force.
- **Inner Liner:** Halobutyl rubber. *Why?* Holds air — no inner tube in modern tubeless tractor tyres.

### 2.3 Tyre Size Designation (Decoding a Sidewall Stamp)

> [!NOTE]
> Example sidewall reading: **18.4 – 30  8 PR  R-1**

- **18.4** = Section width in **inches** (≈ 467 mm).
- **30** = Rim diameter in **inches** (≈ 762 mm).
- **8 PR** = 8 Ply Rating (load index equivalent).
- **R-1** = Agricultural standard lug tread.

If an "**R**" appears between width and rim (e.g., **18.4 R 30**), it is a **Radial** tyre; if the symbol is missing or replaced by a hyphen, it is **Bias**.

### 2.4 Crawler Tractor — Functional Components (Accessories)

| Accessory | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **Track frame (chassis)** | Rigid side structure carrying all rollers, sprocket, idler |
| **Track shoe (grouser plate)** | Ground-contact element; carries lugs to engage soil |
| **Track link & pin** | Articulating joints; the pin takes shear load |
| **Bushing** | Rotates around pin; reduces wear between pin and link |
| **Drive sprocket (rear)** | Power input from final drive; engages sprocket teeth into track bushings |
| **Idler wheel (front)** | Guides track, sets track length, hosts tensioner |
| **Track roller (bottom)** | Carries the tractor's weight; rolls along the inner track |
| **Carrier roller (top)** | Supports the upper span of the track |
| **Track tensioner** | Hydraulic or grease-pump adjuster; keeps track at correct sag |
| **Sealed track (sealed-and-lubricated)** | Pins and bushings sealed with grease; extends track life |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Sealed Track matters (KTU 2024):** In conventional unsealed tracks, mud and grit enter the pin-bushing joint, causing rapid wear. Sealed tracks use *floating seals* to retain grease — the syllabus expects you to know this as a key engineering improvement.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions in Part A/B of this module.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net traction (gross traction − rolling resistance)** | $T_{net} = T_{gross} - RR$ | **N (Newton)** | Available draft for implements |
| 2 | **Rolling Resistance of a wheeled vehicle** | $RR = \rho \cdot W$ | N | $\rho$ = coeff. of rolling resistance |
| 3 | **Rolling Resistance of a tracked vehicle** | $RR = \rho_{t} \cdot W$ | N | $\rho_{t}$ ≈ 0.05 – 0.07 (typically) |
| 4 | **Tractive Efficiency** | $\eta_{t} = \dfrac{T_{net}}{T_{gross}} = 1 - \dfrac{RR}{T_{gross}}$ | dimensionless | 0.55 – 0.75 normal range |
| 5 | **Wheel slip (travel reduction ratio)** | $s = 1 - \dfrac{V_{a}}{V_{th}}$ | dimensionless | $V_{a}$ actual, $V_{th}$ theoretical |
| 6 | **Specific Soil Pressure (Crawler)** | $p = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot b \cdot L_{c}}$ | **kPa** | $b$ = shoe width; $L_{c}$ = ground contact length |
| 7 | **Specific Soil Pressure (Wheel)** | $p = \dfrac{W_{w}}{A_{c}}$ | kPa | $A_{c}$ = contact patch area |
| 8 | **Contact patch area (approx.)** | $A_{c} = 0.85 \cdot b \cdot l$ | m² | $b$ = section width, $l$ = contact length |
| 9 | **Maximum tractive force (soil-strength limited)** | $H_{max} = A_{c} \cdot c + W \cdot \tan \phi$ | N | $c$ = cohesion, $\phi$ = soil friction angle |
| 10 | **Drawbar Power** | $DBP = T_{net} \cdot V_{a}$ | **W (Watt) or kW** | Output at the hitch |
| 11 | **Weight on drive wheels (with ballast)** | $W_{d} = W_{static} + W_{ballast}$ | N | Liquid + dry ballast combined |
| 12 | **Tyre load carrying capacity (per tyre)** | $L = k \cdot PR \cdot p_{i} \cdot A$ | N | Empirical, $p_{i}$ = inflation pressure |
| 13 | **Coefficient of traction (μ)** | $\mu = \dfrac{T_{net}}{W_{d}}$ | dimensionless | 0.3 – 0.7 for tyres on field soil |
| 14 | **Travel reduction (track)** | $s = 1 - \dfrac{V_{a}}{V_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{r_{s} \cdot \omega}{V_{th}}$ | dimensionless | $r_{s}$ = sprocket pitch radius |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Field

- **Fuel economy:** Tractive efficiency losses of just 10% in slippage can burn an extra **1.5 – 2 L of diesel per hectare** during ploughing.
- **Soil conservation:** A wheel exerting > **150 kPa** specific pressure compacts subsoil, reducing yield by **15 – 30%** in the following crop. Crawlers hold **30 – 50 kPa**, hence their dominance in paddy and soft-soil operations.
- **Component life:** Correct ballast and inflation pressure extend tyre life from ~2,000 hours to ~4,000 hours.
- **Safety:** Improper inflation on a heavily ballasted tyre can cause sidewall failure, leading to loss of steering control.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Slip, Tractive Efficiency, and Drawbar Power

**Starting premise:** A driven wheel of dynamic loaded radius $r_d$ rotates at angular speed $\omega$ rad/s. With *zero slip*, the theoretical forward speed is $V_{th} = r_d \cdot \omega$. With slip, the actual forward speed is reduced to $V_a$.

**Step 1:** Define wheel slip.

$$s = 1 - \frac{V_a}{V_{th}}$$

**Step 2:** Express theoretical and actual speed.

$$V_{th} = r_d \cdot \omega \quad ; \quad V_a = V_{th} \cdot (1 - s)$$

**Step 3:** Gross traction is the total force the wheel exerts on the ground at the contact patch.

$$T_{gross} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{V_{th}}$$

**Step 4:** Net traction (force available at the drawbar) is reduced by rolling resistance $RR$.

$$T_{net} = T_{gross} - RR$$

**Step 5:** Drawbar power is the useful power delivered to the implement.

$$P_{DBP} = T_{net} \cdot V_a = (T_{gross} - RR) \cdot V_{th} \cdot (1 - s)$$

**Step 6:** But the input power to the wheel from the axle is $P_{wheel} = T_{gross} \cdot V_{th}$. Hence tractive efficiency:

$$\eta_t = \dfrac{P_{DBP}}{P_{wheel}} = (1-s) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{RR}{T_{gross}}\right)$$

**Step 7 (final, KTU-ready form):**

$$\boxed{\eta_t = (1 - s) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho \cdot W}{T_{gross}}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Tractive efficiency is the *product* of two efficiency terms — (1−s) the *slip efficiency*, and the bracketed *resistance efficiency*. Both must be high. Even with zero slip, if rolling resistance is high, ηₜ collapses.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Soil Pressure of a Crawler

**Given:** A crawler tractor of total operating weight $W$ (N) has two parallel tracks. Each track has a ground-contact length $L_c$ (m) and shoe width $b$ (m). The contact is approximately a rectangle.

**Step 1:** Total ground contact area (two tracks).

$$A_{total} = 2 \cdot b \cdot L_c$$

**Step 2:** By definition, average specific soil pressure is weight divided by area.

$$p = \dfrac{W}{A_{total}} = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot b \cdot L_c}$$

**Step 3 (numerical example):** A 60 kN crawler tractor, with $L_c = 1.6$ m and $b = 0.4$ m.

$$p = \dfrac{60\,000}{2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 1.6} = \dfrac{60\,000}{1.28} \approx 46\,875 \text{ Pa} \approx 46.9 \text{ kPa}$$

This falls within the **30 – 50 kPa** range that allows operation in saturated paddy fields without sinking.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tyre Load Capacity Using the Empirical Boussinesq / ASAE Relation

**Given:** For an agricultural drive tyre, load capacity is empirically related to inflation pressure, ply rating, and tyre volume.

**Step 1:** Load on a tyre is approximated by the contact area times the inflation pressure (Bekker approximation).

$$L \approx p_i \cdot A_c$$

**Step 2:** Contact area for a tyre of section width $b$ and contact length $l$ (approximated as a flattened ellipse of factor 0.85).

$$A_c = 0.85 \cdot b \cdot l$$

**Step 3:** The contact length $l$ is approximated from the deflection $\delta$ of the tyre. By geometry:

$$l \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot r_d \cdot \delta - \delta^2}$$

**Step 4 (final consolidated KTU form):**

$$\boxed{L \approx 0.85 \cdot p_i \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot r_d \cdot \delta - \delta^2}}$$

where $\delta$ is the tyre deflection (m), $r_d$ is the dynamic rolling radius (m).

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway:** Doubling inflation pressure nearly doubles load capacity, but quadruples soil compaction. *Less pressure = more footprint = less compaction*, provided the carcass is strong enough. This is the central trade-off in tractor ballasting.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Q [Model]:** A 35 kN tractor pulls a plough through soft field soil. The rolling resistance coefficient is 0.10, and the gross tractive force developed at the wheel is 18 kN. The wheel radius is 0.75 m, and the actual forward speed is 6.0 km/h. Find (i) the slip, (ii) the tractive efficiency, and (iii) the drawbar power in kW.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance:**

$$RR = \rho \cdot W = 0.10 \cdot 35\,000 = 3\,500 \text{ N} = 3.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Net traction:**

$$T_{net} = T_{gross} - RR = 18\,000 - 3\,500 = 14\,500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical speed at zero slip:**

$$V_{th} = r_d \cdot \omega$$

We are given the actual speed $V_a = 6.0$ km/h. We must find slip. We need another relation. Use the wheel power balance.

Power input to the wheel: $P_{wheel} = T_{gross} \cdot V_{th}$. This must equal tractive effort (input) and is lost partly to slip. For a 4WD farm tractor at typical 12% slip in soft soil:

$$s \approx 0.12 \quad \text{(assume soft soil, ASAE typical)}$$

$$V_{th} = \dfrac{V_a}{1 - s} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.88} = 6.818 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4 — Tractive efficiency (using the consolidated formula):**

$$\eta_t = (1 - s) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{RR}{T_{gross}}\right) = 0.88 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{3.5}{18}\right) = 0.88 \cdot 0.8056 = 0.7089$$

$$\eta_t \approx 70.9\%$$

**Step 5 — Drawbar power:**

$$P_{DBP} = T_{net} \cdot V_a = 14\,500 \cdot \dfrac{6.0 \cdot 1000}{3600} = 14\,500 \cdot 1.6667 = 24\,167 \text{ W} \approx 24.17 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:** Slip ≈ **12%**, Tractive efficiency ≈ **70.9%**, Drawbar power ≈ **24.2 kW**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tyre Selection by Field Operation

| Field Operation | Soil Type | Recommended Tyre | Inflation Pressure | Ballast |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ploughing (deep tillage)** | Soft, moist | R-1 wide-base, possibly dual wheels | **80 – 100 kPa** (low) | Heavy (water + cast iron) |
| **Disc harrowing** | Medium | R-1 standard | **100 – 120 kPa** | Medium |
| **Seeding / Spraying** | Firm, dry | R-1 narrow or R-3 turf | **120 – 150 kPa** | Light (dry ballast) |
| **Paddy (wetland)** | Submerged | R-2 deep-lug, or **half-track / full crawler** | **80 – 90 kPa** | Maximum water ballast |
| **Transport on road** | Hard road | R-1 (over-inflated) | **160 – 200 kPa** | Remove ballast, raise 3-point hitch |
| **Loader / Industrial** | Concrete | R-4 industrial lug | **200 – 250 kPa** | Heavy (cast iron) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU student error:** Running a tractor at *road inflation* in the field. This reduces the contact patch, increases slip to >25%, and burns fuel. Always **de-flate for fieldwork, re-inflate for road**.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Tyre Subsystem of a Wheeled Tractor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Torque] --> B[Transmission Gearbox]
    B --> C[Final Drive / Differential]
    C --> D[Axle Shaft to Rear Wheel Hub]
    D --> E[Rear Drive Tyre R-1]
    F[Steering Wheel Input] --> G[Steering Axle]
    G --> H[Front Steering Tyre F-2 or I-1]
    I[Air Inflation Pressure] --> E
    I --> H
    J[Liquid Water Ballast] --> E
    K[Cast Iron Weights on Rim] --> E
    L[Dynamic Load from Tractor Weight] --> E
    L --> H
    E --> M[Contact Patch on Soil]
    H --> N[Contact Patch Steering]
    M --> O[Gross Traction Force Tg]
    N --> P[Steering Reaction Force]
    O --> Q[Net Drawbar Pull Tnet]
    R[Rolling Resistance RR] --> Q
```

**Reading the diagram:** Engine torque is transformed step-by-step into tractive force. The three inputs that *modify* the rear drive tyre's behaviour are inflation pressure, ballast (liquid + dry), and dynamic load. The output is the *net drawbar pull* — the force actually delivered to the implement.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Crawler Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine] --> B[Transmission]
    B --> C[Bevel Gear Final Drive]
    C --> D[Drive Sprocket Rear]
    D --> E[Track Bushing Engages Sprocket Tooth]
    E --> F[Track Link Articulated Chain]
    F --> G[Track Shoe Grouser Plate]
    G --> H[Ground Contact Strip]
    I[Idler Wheel Front] --> F
    J[Track Rollers Bottom] --> F
    K[Carrier Rollers Top] --> F
    L[Track Tensioner Hydraulic] --> I
    H --> M[Specific Soil Pressure 30 to 50 kPa]
    M --> N[Drawbar Pull High on Soft Soil]
    O[Sealed Pin and Bushing Grease] --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power flows from the engine down the right side, terminates at the drive sprocket, which *engages the bushings* of the track. The track forms a closed loop carried by the rollers, idler, and sprocket. The *contact strip* between the lower span of the track and the ground generates the drawbar pull.

---

### 4.3 Cross-Section — Drive Tyre Component Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tread and Lug Pattern] --> B[Under-Tread Cushion Rubber]
    B --> C[Belt Plies Steel or Nylon]
    C --> D[Carcass Ply Layers Crossed at 30 to 45 deg Bias OR 90 deg Radial]
    D --> E[Inner Liner Halobutyl Rubber]
    E --> F[Bead Bundle Steel Wire]
    F --> G[Rim Flange Engagement]
    H[Sidewall Rubber Compound] --> D
    I[Chafer Strip] --> F
    J[Cap Ply Optional] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** From outside inward — Tread (ground contact) → Cushion (impact absorption) → Belt plies (stability) → Carcass (pressure containment) → Inner liner (air seal) → Bead (rim lock). Sidewall protects the carcass; chafer prevents bead-rim chafing.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024, Sem-2]**
> **List the functions of a pneumatic tractor tyre. Distinguish clearly between an R-1 and an R-2 tread pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Functions of a pneumatic tractor tyre (1½ marks):*
1. Transmits tractive effort from axle to soil.
2. Supports the static and dynamic weight of the tractor.
3. Provides steering control at the front axle.
4. Cushions the tractor against ground irregularities, reducing operator fatigue.
5. Minimises soil compaction by spreading load over a contact patch.

*R-1 vs R-2 tread distinction (1½ marks):*

| Feature | R-1 (Regular Ag.) | R-2 (Rice / Paddy) |
|---|---|---|
| Lug angle | 38° – 45° from centreline | 50° – 60° (steeper) |
| Lug height | Moderate (~50 mm) | Very high (~75 – 90 mm) |
| Application | General tillage, transport | Saturated paddy fields |
| Self-cleaning | Good in moist soil | Excellent in flooded mud |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023, Sem-1]**
> **What is ballast in a tractor? List any two types of ballast commonly used on drive tyres, and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Ballast is additional weight added to a tractor's drive wheels to increase weight on the driving axle, improve traction, and reduce wheel slip.

*Types (1 mark):*
- **Water / Calcium-chloride solution ballast** (filled in the tyre cavity through the valve stem).
- **Cast-iron wheel weights** (bolted to the rim).

*Advantage and disadvantage (1 mark):*
- Water ballast: *Advantage* — cheap and easy to add/remove. *Disadvantage* — adds only moderate weight (~25% fill) and corrodes the rim if plain water is used; hence CaCl₂ is preferred.
- Cast-iron weights: *Advantage* — adds the maximum usable ballast, balanced distribution. *Disadvantage* — slower to install, requires labour, may be stolen, adds unsprung mass.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A — Tyres of Wheeled Tractors**

**(a)** Classify tractor tyres by position and tread pattern. Explain the construction of a modern radial agricultural drive tyre with a neat sketch. Discuss how the radial construction improves tractive efficiency compared to a bias-ply tyre. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 40 kN tractor with 2WD operates on a tilled field. The rolling resistance coefficient is 0.08. The wheel radius is 0.78 m, the wheel speed corresponds to a theoretical forward speed of 7.2 km/h at zero slip, and the measured actual speed is 6.3 km/h. Compute the slip, the gross traction force, the net drawbar pull, the tractive efficiency, and the drawbar power in kW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Construction (7 marks):**

*Classification (2 marks):*

By position:
- **Steering (front) tyres:** F-2 rib, I-1 implement — small section, narrow, smooth rib.
- **Drive (rear) tyres:** R-1, R-2, R-3, R-4 — large section, deep lugs.

By construction:
- **Bias-ply (diagonal):** cords cross at 30°–45° to centreline.
- **Radial-ply:** cords run at 90°; stabilised by inextensible steel-belted hoops.

*Radial drive-tyre construction (3 marks):*

A radial agricultural drive tyre consists of:
1. **Tread** — thick lugged rubber compound (natural + synthetic blend) on the outer crown, moulded in R-1 pattern.
2. **Cap ply (optional)** — a thin belt of nylon covering the tread for high-speed stability.
3. **Steel-belted stabiliser rings** — 2 to 4 layers of crossed steel cords beneath the tread, holding the tread flat against the road/ground.
4. **Carcass ply** — multiple layers of rayon or polyester cords running *radially* (at 90°) from bead to bead. The radial orientation is the defining feature.
5. **Sidewall** — thin, flexible rubber on either flank; sidewall flexes freely in radial tyres, which is why they have a *bulging* appearance under load.
6. **Bead bundle** — high-tensile steel wire wrapped into a ring, anchoring the carcass to the rim.
7. **Inner liner** — halobutyl rubber replacing the inner tube in tubeless designs.

*Why radial improves tractive efficiency (2 marks):*

- **Larger contact patch:** The flexible sidewall allows the tread to flatten, increasing contact length by **25 – 30%**. More patch area = more soil engaged = higher tractive force.
- **Lower rolling resistance:** The radial carcass flexes more efficiently, dissipating less energy as heat in the sidewall, reducing ρ from ~0.10 to ~0.06 on hard surfaces.
- **Higher permissible slip without soil damage:** Because the contact patch is longer, the same drawbar pull is achieved at a *lower* slip, raising the term (1−s) in the ηₜ equation.
- **Longer tread life:** Even wear across the wider contact patch, plus higher mileage on road transport.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Slip:**

$$s = 1 - \frac{V_a}{V_{th}} = 1 - \frac{6.3}{7.2} = 1 - 0.875 = 0.125 = 12.5\%$$

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance:**

$$RR = \rho \cdot W = 0.08 \cdot 40\,000 = 3\,200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Tractive efficiency (resistance term):** Need the resistance term. We compute gross traction from a different relation. For 2WD, the weight on drive wheels is typically **70% of total** if front is light, but for this problem we assume the *effective weight on drive wheels* is the entire tractor weight (rear-mounted implements shift weight rear). We use the equation $T_{net} = \mu \cdot W_d$, but the cleaner route is to compute from the given data using $\eta_t$ directly. Re-reading: we need $T_{gross}$, which is not directly given. The standard KTU approach is to find $T_{gross}$ from the drawbar power input relation:

$$P_{wheel} = T_{gross} \cdot V_{th}$$

But we have no $P_{wheel}$ directly. Therefore we must assume a **coefficient of traction μ** for tilled field. Standard value: μ ≈ 0.45 for R-1 on tilled soil.

Then $T_{net} = \mu \cdot W_d$. Assuming the entire 40 kN is on drive wheels (worst-case 2WD assumption often used in KTU problems):

$$T_{net} = 0.45 \cdot 40\,000 = 18\,000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Gross traction:**

$$T_{gross} = T_{net} + RR = 18\,000 + 3\,200 = 21\,200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Tractive efficiency:**

$$\eta_t = (1 - s) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{RR}{T_{gross}}\right) = 0.875 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{3\,200}{21\,200}\right)$$

$$\eta_t = 0.875 \cdot (1 - 0.1509) = 0.875 \cdot 0.8491 = 0.7429$$

$$\eta_t \approx 74.3\%$$

**Step 6 — Drawbar power:**

$$P_{DBP} = T_{net} \cdot V_a = 18\,000 \cdot \frac{6.3 \cdot 1000}{3600} = 18\,000 \cdot 1.75 = 31\,500 \text{ W} = 31.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Slip | **12.5%** |
| Rolling Resistance | **3.2 kN** |
| Gross Traction | **21.2 kN** |
| Net Drawbar Pull | **18.0 kN** |
| Tractive Efficiency | **74.3%** |
| Drawbar Power | **31.5 kW** |

---

### **Question B — Crawler Tractor Accessories (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Describe the function of each of the following crawler tractor accessories: (i) drive sprocket, (ii) idler wheel, (iii) track roller, (iv) carrier roller, (v) track tensioner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 65 kN crawler tractor has two parallel tracks. Each track shoe is 0.45 m wide, and the ground-contact length of the lower track span is 1.8 m. Compute the specific soil pressure. Compare it with a 4-wheeled tractor of the same weight, each tyre having a contact patch of 0.07 m² (2 tyres on the drive axle carry 75% of the weight), and comment on the suitability of each for paddy-field operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functions of Crawler Accessories (7 marks — 1.4 each):**

(i) **Drive sprocket:** Located at the rear, driven by the final drive. Its teeth engage the *bushings* of the track links, converting the rotational torque of the engine into linear motion of the track — the fundamental propulsion element.

(ii) **Idler wheel:** Mounted at the front of the track frame. It (a) redirects the track from the bottom span back to the top span, (b) sets the effective wheelbase of the crawler, and (c) acts as the *anchor point* for the track-tensioning mechanism.

(iii) **Track roller (bottom roller):** Rolls along the inner face of the lower track span. Supports the entire weight of the tractor between sprocket and idler. Usually *sealed-for-life* and runs on a stationary axle.

(iv) **Carrier roller (top roller):** Mounted on the upper track frame. Holds up the *upper* span of the track so that it does not flap or sag, ensuring smooth high-speed running and reducing vibration.

(v) **Track tensioner:** A hydraulic cylinder (or grease-pump adjuster) acting on the idler axle. Maintains the correct *track sag* (typically 25 – 50 mm) so the track does not throw off the sprocket teeth. Auto-tensioners use a hydraulic accumulator with a nitrogen-charged pre-load.

**(b) Numerical Comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Crawler specific soil pressure:**

$$p_{crawler} = \frac{W}{2 \cdot b \cdot L_c} = \frac{65\,000}{2 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 1.8} = \frac{65\,000}{1.62} = 40\,123 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.1 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Wheeled tractor pressure on drive tyres:**

Weight on drive wheels: $W_d = 0.75 \cdot 65\,000 = 48\,750$ N
Total contact area of 2 drive tyres: $A = 2 \cdot 0.07 = 0.14$ m²

$$p_{wheel} = \frac{W_d}{A} = \frac{48\,750}{0.14} = 348\,214 \text{ Pa} \approx 348.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Crawler | Wheeled (4WD) |
|---|---|---|
| Specific soil pressure | **40.1 kPa** | **348.2 kPa** |
| Ratio (wheeled / crawler) | 1× | ~8.7× |

**Step 4 — Comment on paddy suitability (2 marks):**

- Paddy fields have a *bearing capacity* of typically **25 – 60 kPa** in the flooded, puddled state. A wheeled tractor at **348 kPa** far exceeds this and will sink, sometimes to the axles, especially during puddling or wet harvesting.
- The crawler at **40 kPa** stays within the safe bearing range and floats on the soft paddy mud, making it the *only* practical choice for wetland preparation, transplanting, and combine harvesting of rice.
- Hence **crawlers are mandatory for sustained paddy work**, while wheeled tractors dominate on dry-land farming.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** writing $RR$ in N but $T_{gross}$ in kN — the KTU evaluator deducts ½ to 1 mark. **Always convert to a single unit (N or kN) within one calculation chain.**
> 2. **Forgetting the (1−s) factor in tractive efficiency:** A common mistake is to write $\eta_t = 1 - (RR/T_{gross})$ only, omitting the slip term. KTU 2024 marks scheme expects the *full product form* — losing 1 mark if missing.
> 3. **Conflating R-1 and R-4 treads:** R-4 is *industrial* (bar-tread), not agricultural. Writing "R-4 for ploughing" is a ½-mark deduction.
> 4. **Calling the idler a "guide pulley" only:** The idler also *holds the track tensioner*. Mentioning only one function loses ½ mark.
> 5. **Drawing the crawler track with no sprocket teeth or with the rollers on the wrong side** of the track — a 1-mark deduction in the 7-mark construction question. Always show the **drive sprocket at the rear**, **idler at the front**, **track rollers below**, and **carrier rollers above** the track.
> 6. **Forgetting that "R" in size designation (e.g., 18.4 R 30) means *radial***, not "rim" — common confusion in Part A.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tyres & Crawler Accessories (Module 1):**

- **Tyre size decoding:** Width (in) × Rim (in), e.g., 18.4 – 30 means 18.4-inch width on a 30-inch rim; a literal "R" in between ⇒ **Radial**.
- **Ply Rating (PR)** is a *strength index*, not the literal number of cord layers in a radial tyre.
- **R-1 = standard agriculture**, R-2 = paddy (deepest lug), R-3 = turf/golf, R-4 = industrial. **Mnemonic: "1-Farm, 2-Flooded, 3-Field-grass, 4-Factory".**
- **Bias vs Radial:** Bias has stiffer sidewall, smaller contact patch, cheaper; Radial has flexible sidewall, larger contact patch, higher ηₜ, more expensive.
- **Tractive efficiency formula (must-memorise):**
$$\eta_t = (1 - s) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\rho \cdot W}{T_{gross}}\right)$$
- **Rolling resistance coefficient ρ** ≈ 0.04 – 0.10 on field soil; 0.02 on concrete; 0.10 – 0.15 on loose sand.
- **Slip range:** optimum is **8 – 15%** for field work; >20% wastes fuel and destroys soil structure.
- **Ballast rule:** Add ballast when slip > 15% in field; remove ballast when slip < 8% in soft soil. Use *water/CaCl₂* for light, *cast-iron weights* for heavy.
- **Inflation rule:** Low pressure (80 – 100 kPa) for fieldwork → larger contact patch, less compaction; high pressure (150 – 200 kPa) for road → lower RR, higher speed.
- **Crawler components memorise order:** Sprocket (rear, drives) → Idler (front, guides & tensions) → Track rollers (bottom, carry weight) → Carrier rollers (top, support upper span) → Tensioner (hydraulic, on idler).
- **Specific soil pressure:** Crawler 30 – 50 kPa; Wheeled tractor 100 – 350 kPa. Crawler is **mandatory** for paddy/wetland operations.
- **Sealed-and-lubricated track (SLT)** is a KTU-recognised improvement — life extended from ~3,000 hr to ~6,000 hr.
- **Drawbar power = Net traction × actual speed.** Net traction = Gross traction − Rolling resistance.
- **For 2 marks questions:** always state units explicitly (kPa, mm, kN, m/s²) — evaluators look for this in the first 10 seconds of reading your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Farm Equipment: Introduction to Harvesting, Principles and Types of Cutting Mechanisms, Study of Harvesting Operation and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Farm Equipment: Introduction to Harvesting, Principles and Types of Cutting Mechanisms, Study of Harvesting Operation and Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Farm Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Farm equipment refers to the entire spectrum of machinery, implements, and tools used in agricultural operations — from primary tillage to post-harvest processing. For Module 2, the focus is on **harvesting machinery**, which is broadly classified into crop-cutting machines (mowers, reapers), threshers, and combined harvester systems.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a farm as a giant outdoor factory. Just as a manufacturing plant has a conveyor belt that brings raw material to a series of stations that cut, shape, and package a product, a farm has equipment that **cuts** standing crops, **collects** them, **separates** the grain from the stalk, and **stores** the final yield. Harvesting is the very first "cutting station" of that factory line.

### 1.2 What is Harvesting?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Harvesting:**
> Harvesting is the agricultural operation of cutting, gathering, and removing the matured crop (grain, fodder, or fibre) from the field. It is a **time-critical** operation because delays cause grain shattering, quality degradation, and economic loss.

### 1.3 Classification of Harvesting Methods

| Category | Sub-Type | Description | Typical Crop |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual** | Hand Sickle / Scythe | Operator uses a hand-held curved blade | Paddy, wheat, grass |
| **Animal-Drawn** | Tractor-mounted Reaper | Tractor PTO powers a reciprocating cutter bar | Wheat, oats |
| **Mechanized** | Combine Harvester | Integrated cutting–threshing–winnowing machine | Paddy, soybean, maize |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the transition from manual to **mechanized harvesting** as a core topic because mechanization directly influences productivity (kg/man-hour) and post-harvest losses.

### 1.4 Definition of Cutting Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Cutting Mechanism:**
> A cutting mechanism is a mechanical assembly of blades, guards, and actuating members that severs the standing crop stem from its base through the combined action of **shear**, **impact**, and **friction**.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Imagine using a pair of office scissors on a thick piece of rope. The two blades slide past each other in a *shearing* action and sever the rope. A mower's cutter bar does exactly the same thing — one blade is stationary (the guard), the other is moving (the knife), and the crop stem is trapped between them until it snaps.

### 1.5 Importance of Cutting in Harvesting

- **First operation** in the harvesting sequence.
- Determines **field capacity** of the entire harvester.
- Poor cutting → uneven stubble, clogging, and grain loss.
- Cutting consumes roughly **15–25%** of total harvester power.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Cutting Mechanisms

Cutting is fundamentally a **mechanical failure of the stem** under combined stresses. Three core principles operate either alone or in combination:

#### Principle 1 — Shearing Action
- Two opposing edges (one fixed guard + one moving knife) pass each other with a small clearance.
- The stem is trapped between them and is **cut by shear stress**.
- Used in: **Reciprocating mowers, combine harvester cutter bars.**
- Equation basis: $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$, where the stem fails when shear stress exceeds its **ultimate shear strength**.

#### Principle 2 — Impact Cutting
- A high-speed blade strikes the stem with kinetic energy.
- The stem fractures due to bending/tensile stress at the point of impact.
- Used in: **Rotary mowers, flail mowers, disc mowers.**
- Governing equation: $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$.

#### Principle 3 — Sawing / Abrasive Cutting
- A serrated edge slides across the stem, abrading fibres.
- Used in: **Chaff cutters, certain forage harvesters.**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Shear Cutting (Most Important)

1. The crop stem is guided into a **slot between a guard and a knife section**.
2. The knife section moves with velocity $v_k$ (m/s).
3. The stem is held stationary (or moves at much lower velocity) by the crop mat.
4. The relative velocity $v_{rel}$ between knife and stem creates a **shear strain rate**.
5. Stem material yields when shear stress $\tau$ exceeds stem shear strength $\tau_u$ (typically **3–8 MPa** for paddy/wheat).
6. The cut stem is delivered to the conveying system.

### 2.3 Types of Cutting Mechanisms

| Type | Working Principle | Speed | Crops | Power/Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating Cutter Bar** | Shearing (linear oscillation) | 1.5 – 3.0 m/s | Paddy, wheat, oats | Low |
| **Rotary (Disc) Cutter** | Impact + Shear | 50 – 90 m/s | Heavy grass, sugarcane | Medium |
| **Flail Mower** | Impact (free-swinging blades) | 40 – 60 m/s | Pasture, brush | High |
| **Reel-Mower Combination** | Shear + Reel assist | 1.5 – 3.0 m/s | Tall crops | Medium |
| **Sickle Bar (Hand)** | Manual shearing | 0.5 – 1.5 m/s | All grass crops | Zero (manual) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Reciprocating cutter bars (used in combine harvesters) appear in nearly every previous-year question paper. Memorize the kinematic equations for stroke length, crank radius, and knife speed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Knife (average) Speed | $v_k = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$ | m/s | $L_c$ = crank radius, $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Maximum Knife Speed | $v_{max} = \omega \cdot L_c$ | m/s | At mid-stroke |
| 3 | Stroke Length | $S = 2 L_c$ | m | — |
| 4 | Cutting Energy | $E_c = F_c \cdot s$ | J | $F_c$ = cutting force, $s$ = displacement |
| 5 | Power for Cutting | $P_c = F_c \cdot v_k$ | W | — |
| 6 | Theoretical Field Capacity | $C_t = \dfrac{W \cdot v_f}{10}$ | ha/h | $W$ = width (m), $v_f$ = forward speed (km/h) |
| 7 | Effective Field Capacity | $C_e = C_t \cdot \eta_f$ | ha/h | $\eta_f$ = field efficiency (0.7 – 0.85) |
| 8 | Stem Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F_c}{A_s}$ | Pa | $A_s$ = stem cross-sectional area |
| 9 | Specific Cutting Energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_c}{A_s}$ | J/m² | Indicates crop "cuttability" |
| 10 | Reel Index | $K = \dfrac{v_r}{v_f}$ | — | $v_r$ = reel tip speed; optimum $K = 1.25$ to $1.5$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Encoding Rule:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above are written as `\vert` or by rephrasing, to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Combine harvester design** depends on cutter bar kinematics.
- **Forage harvester** design uses rotary disc dynamics.
- **Tractor PTO selection** is dictated by peak cutting power.
- **Crop-lost minimisation** programs worldwide (FAO) target improved cutting uniformity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average and Maximum Velocity of a Reciprocating Knife

The reciprocating knife is driven by a crank-slider mechanism. The displacement of the knife from the centre of stroke is:

$$
x(t) = L_c \cos(\omega t)
$$

Differentiating with respect to time gives the instantaneous velocity:

$$
v(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = -\omega L_c \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 1 — Maximum velocity** occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = \pm 1$, i.e., at the mid-stroke position:

$$
v_{max} = \omega L_c = \dfrac{2 \pi N L_c}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Average velocity** is computed over one complete stroke (forward + return):

$$
v_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{Stroke length}}{\text{Time per stroke}} = \dfrac{2 L_c}{T/2} = \dfrac{4 L_c}{T}
$$

Since $T = \dfrac{60}{N}$:

$$
v_{avg} = \dfrac{4 L_c N}{60} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}
$$
