**Conversion logic:** We divide by 2 because within one revolution the knife travels $2 \times (\text{stroke length}) = 4 L_c$ in total, but the "effective" cutting happens only on the forward half; the average over the full revolution simplifies to $\dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Required by the Cutter Bar

Let the average cutting force on the entire cutter bar width be $F_c$ (N). The work done per knife stroke is:

$$
W_{stroke} = F_c \cdot S = F_c \cdot 2 L_c
$$

The number of strokes per minute equals the crank rpm $N$. Therefore cutting power:

$$
P_c = \dfrac{W_{stroke} \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot 2 L_c \cdot N}{60}
$$

But $2 L_c = S$ and $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$, so we may write equivalently:

$$
P_c = F_c \cdot v_{avg}
$$

**Step 3 — Expressing in kW and adding PTO efficiency factor:**

$$
P_{PTO} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_{avg}}{\eta_{PTO}}
$$

where $\eta_{PTO} \approx 0.85$.

**Conversion logic:** The product of force (N) and velocity (m/s) gives watts; dividing by mechanical efficiency (0.85) gives the actual PTO power the tractor must deliver.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Field Capacity

Theoretical field capacity (ha/h) for a machine of width $W$ (m) moving at forward speed $v_f$ (km/h):

$$
C_t = \dfrac{W \cdot v_f}{10}
$$

**Why divide by 10?** Unit reconciliation: $W$ (m) × $v_f$ (km/h) → $W$ (m) × $1000 v_f$ (m/3600 s) → dividing by $10{,}000$ m²/ha yields the factor 10.

Effective field capacity accounts for turning, overlap, and stoppage time:

$$
C_e = C_t \cdot \eta_f
$$

**Conversion logic:** $\eta_f$ (field efficiency) typically ranges from **0.70 to 0.85** for combine harvesters, representing 15–30% time lost in headland turns, operator adjustments, and crop variability.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A combine harvester has a cutter bar width of **4.2 m** and operates at a forward speed of **5 km/h**. The crank of the knife drive rotates at **600 rpm** with a crank radius of **38 mm**. The average cutting force on the bar is **1200 N**. Find:
> (i) Average knife speed
> (ii) Power required for cutting
> (iii) Theoretical and effective field capacity (assume $\eta_f = 0.8$)

**Solution:**

Given: $W = 4.2$ m, $v_f = 5$ km/h, $N = 600$ rpm, $L_c = 38 \text{ mm} = 0.038$ m, $F_c = 1200$ N, $\eta_f = 0.8$.

**(i) Average knife speed:**

$$
v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.038 \times 600}{60}
$$

Step-by-step:
- Numerator: $2 \times 0.038 = 0.076$
- $0.076 \times 600 = 45.6$
- Divide by 60: $45.6 / 60 = 0.76$ m/s

$$
\boxed{v_{avg} = 0.76 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Power required:**

$$
P_c = F_c \cdot v_{avg} = 1200 \times 0.76 = 912 \text{ W} \approx 0.912 \text{ kW}
$$

With PTO efficiency:

$$
P_{PTO} = \dfrac{0.912}{0.85} \approx 1.073 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Field capacity:**

$$
C_t = \dfrac{W \cdot v_f}{10} = \dfrac{4.2 \times 5}{10} = \dfrac{21}{10} = 2.1 \text{ ha/h}
$$

$$
C_e = C_t \cdot \eta_f = 2.1 \times 0.8 = 1.68 \text{ ha/h}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stem Properties vs Cutting Energy

| Crop | Stem Diameter (mm) | Shear Strength (MPa) | Specific Cutting Energy (kJ/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paddy | 4 – 6 | 4 – 6 | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Wheat | 3 – 5 | 5 – 8 | 0.4 – 0.7 |
| Maize (stalk) | 20 – 30 | 6 – 10 | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Sugarcane | 25 – 35 | 8 – 12 | 2.0 – 3.0 |
| Lucerne (fodder) | 2 – 4 | 3 – 5 | 0.3 – 0.5 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Combine Harvester (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Standing Crop in Field] --> B[Reel Assembly]
    B --> C[Cutter Bar with Reciprocating Knife]
    C --> D[Conveyor / Auger]
    D --> E[Threshing Cylinder]
    E --> F[Straw Walkers and Sieves]
    F --> G[Cleaning Fan and Blower]
    G --> H[Clean Grain Elevator]
    H --> I[Grain Tank]
    F --> J[Straw Outlet]
    G --> K[Chaff Outlet]

    subgraph PowerTrain[Power Transmission Line]
        P[Tractor PTO Shaft] --> Q[Belt and Pulley Drive]
        Q --> C
        Q --> E
        Q --> G
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The crop travels left-to-right through sequential stations. The Power Train subgraph shows how a single PTO input powers the cutter, thresher, and fan through a belt-and-pulley distribution.

### 4.2 Cutting Mechanism Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cutting Mechanisms] --> B[Linear / Reciprocating]
    A --> C[Rotary]
    A --> D[Manual]

    B --> B1[Sickle Bar Mower]
    B --> B2[Combine Cutter Bar]
    B --> B3[Reaper Binder]

    C --> C1[Disc Mower]
    C --> C2[Drum Mower]
    C --> C3[Flail Mower]
    C --> C4[Rotary Reaper]

    D --> D1[Hand Sickle]
    D --> D2[Scythe]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Reciprocating Cutter Bar Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine / PTO] --> B[V-Belt Pulley]
    B --> C[Crank Shaft]
    C --> D[Connecting Rod]
    D --> E[Pitman Arm]
    E --> F[Knife Head]
    F --> G[Reciprocating Knife with Sections]
    G --> H[Stationary Guards with Slots]
    G --> I[Cut Crop Delivered to Auger]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Shear Action Between Knife and Guard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stationary Guard - upper edge] --> B[Gap Slot]
    B --> C[Moving Knife Section - lower edge]
    C --> D[Crop Stem - vertically oriented]
    D --> E[Shear Plane - thin line of failure]
    E --> F[Cut Stem - falls onto platform]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The clearance between the knife section and the guard (typically **0.3 – 0.5 mm**) is critical. Excessive clearance causes stem bending rather than clean cutting, raising the specific cutting energy and clogging the bar.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

Define harvesting. List any three mechanized harvesting methods used in Indian agriculture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Harvesting is the agricultural operation of cutting and collecting the mature crop from the field for further processing (1 mark). Three mechanized harvesting methods are:

1. **Self-propelled combine harvester** – performs cutting, threshing, and cleaning in a single pass (1 mark).
2. **Tractor-mounted reaper** – cuts and windrows the crop, leaving it for separate threshing (0.5 mark).
3. **Self-propelled or tractor-driven forage harvester** – chops green fodder into small pieces for silage (0.5 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adaptation]**

State the three basic principles of cutting crop stems. Name the cutting mechanism that uses each principle.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Principle | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Shearing** (1 mark) | Reciprocating cutter bar (0.5 mark) |
| **Impact** (1 mark) | Rotary disc mower / flail mower (0.5 mark) |

(For full 3 marks, mention sawing/abrasive and chaff cutter as the third — 0.5 mark adjustment.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Adaptation]**

(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **reciprocating cutter bar** as used in a combine harvester. (7 marks)

(b) A combine harvester is fitted with a cutter bar of width **3.6 m**, operated at a forward speed of **4.5 km/h**. The crank radius of the knife drive is **40 mm** and it rotates at **550 rpm**. If the average cutting resistance per metre width of the bar is **300 N/m**, calculate the **(i)** average knife speed, **(ii)** cutting power required, and **(iii)** theoretical and effective field capacity. Assume field efficiency $\eta_f = 0.8$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

- **Cutter bar (knife + guards):** The bar is a long horizontal assembly made up of triangular knife sections riveted to a knife back (1 mark). Alternating stationary guards are bolted to the bar with sharpened upper edges and slot openings through which the knife sections slide (1 mark).
- **Drive mechanism:** The tractor PTO drives a V-belt pulley, which rotates a crank shaft. A pitman arm (connecting rod) converts rotary motion into reciprocating motion of the knife (1 mark).
- **Reel assembly:** A bat-reel with curved tines rotates above the cutter bar, pushing the standing crop into the knife slots and gently laying the cut crop on the platform (1 mark).
- **Working sequence:** As the machine moves forward, the reel bends the stalks over the cutter bar; the reciprocating knife and stationary guard perform a scissor-like shearing action; the cut crop falls on the platform and is swept inward by an auger (1 mark).
- **Working principle:** Stem shearing — the stem is held by the guard lip while the knife slides past with velocity $v_k$ at clearance 0.3–0.5 mm, creating shear stress $\tau = F/A$ that exceeds the stem's ultimate shear strength (1 mark).
- **Diagram:** (neat sketch of cutter bar with crank–pitman–knife–guard–reel — 1 mark).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $W = 3.6$ m, $v_f = 4.5$ km/h, $L_c = 0.040$ m, $N = 550$ rpm, $q = 300$ N/m, $\eta_f = 0.8$.

Total cutting force:
$$
F_c = q \cdot W = 300 \times 3.6 = 1080 \text{ N}
$$

**(i) Average knife speed:**

$$
v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.040 \times 550}{60} = \dfrac{44}{60} = 0.733 \text{ m/s}
$$

(2 marks for formula + 0.5 mark for answer)

**(ii) Cutting power:**

$$
P_c = F_c \cdot v_{avg} = 1080 \times 0.733 = 791.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.792 \text{ kW}
$$

(2 marks for formula + 0.5 mark for answer)

**(iii) Field capacity:**

$$
C_t = \dfrac{W \cdot v_f}{10} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 4.5}{10} = 1.62 \text{ ha/h}
$$

$$
C_e = C_t \cdot \eta_f = 1.62 \times 0.8 = 1.296 \text{ ha/h} \approx 1.30 \text{ ha/h}
$$

(1.5 marks for both formulas + 0.5 mark for final answer)

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

(a) Explain the **principle of shearing** in cutting crop stems. Derive the expression for **average knife speed** of a reciprocating cutter bar in terms of crank radius and rpm. (7 marks)

(b) Compare **reciprocating cutter bar**, **rotary disc mower**, and **flail mower** on the basis of working principle, suitable crops, power requirement, and cutting efficiency. (7 marks)

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(a) Principle of shearing and derivation (7 marks):**

- Shearing definition: two edges (guard + knife) pass each other in close clearance, producing shear stress (2 marks).
- Displacement equation: $x = L_c \cos \omega t$ (1 mark).
- Velocity: $v = -\omega L_c \sin \omega t$ (1 mark).
- $v_{max} = \omega L_c$ at mid-stroke (1 mark).
- Average over full revolution: $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$ (2 marks).

**(b) Comparison (7 marks — 1.75 marks per column):**

| Parameter | Reciprocating Bar | Rotary Disc | Flail Mower |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Shearing | Impact + Shear | Free-impact |
| Speed | 1.5 – 3.0 m/s | 50 – 90 m/s | 40 – 60 m/s |
| Crops | Paddy, wheat | Heavy grass, sugarcane | Pasture, brush |
| Power | Low | Medium | High |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups in $v_{avg}$ derivation:** Many students forget to convert rpm to rev/s (divide by 60) and end up with $v_{avg} = 2 L_c N$ instead of $\dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Field capacity formula misuse:** The formula $C_t = \dfrac{W \cdot v_f}{10}$ requires $v_f$ in **km/h** and $W$ in **metres**. Substituting $v_f$ in m/s gives an answer off by a factor of 3.6 — full-mark loss on that sub-part.
> 3. **PTO efficiency omission:** Forgetting to divide by $\eta_{PTO} \approx 0.85$ in power calculations is a frequent oversight.
> 4. **Confusing $v_{avg}$ with $v_{max}$:** $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$ is the **average** (used in power = $F \times v$); $v_{max} = \dfrac{2 \pi L_c N}{60}$ is the **peak** (used for inertia and stress analysis).
> 5. **Reel index forgotten:** When a reel is mentioned, examiners expect $K = v_r / v_f$ and the optimum range **1.25 – 1.5**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Harvesting** = cutting + collecting mature crop; first stage of post-production.
- **Three cutting principles:** shearing, impact, sawing/abrasive.
- **Reciprocating cutter bar** dominates combine harvester design — *most-asked topic*.
- **Crank-slider kinematics:** $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L_c N}{60}$ ; $v_{max} = \dfrac{2 \pi L_c N}{60}$.
- **Cutting power:** $P_c = F_c \cdot v_{avg}$ (use average, not maximum).
- **Field capacity:** $C_t = \dfrac{W v_f}{10}$ (units: $W$ in m, $v_f$ in km/h → ha/h).
- **Effective field capacity:** $C_e = C_t \cdot \eta_f$ with $\eta_f = 0.7 - 0.85$.
- **Knife–guard clearance:** 0.3 – 0.5 mm for clean shear; too wide → bending loss.
- **Reel index** optimum = **1.25 – 1.5** for proper crop delivery.
- **Specific cutting energy** is crop-dependent: paddy ≈ 0.5–0.8 kJ/m², sugarcane ≈ 2–3 kJ/m².
- **PTO efficiency** ≈ **0.85** — required in all power-output calculations.
- **Classification memory hook:** *Reciprocating = Shear; Rotary = Impact; Flail = Free-Impact; Manual = Shear-Hand*.
- Always show **units** and **conversion steps** in numerical answers to fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harvesting Terminology and Study of Mowers: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Harvesting Terminology and Study of Mowers: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Harvesting — Formal KTU Definition

**Harvesting** is defined as the operation of cutting, gathering, and collecting the mature crop from the field after it has attained physiological maturity, separating the useful portion (grain/fruit/fodder) from the non-useful portion (stubble/straw). In the context of agricultural engineering, harvesting is the **last mechanical operation** performed on a standing crop before post-harvest processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Harvesting is broadly classified into two major mechanical operations:
> 1. **Mowing / Cutting** — separation of standing crop from the root zone.
> 2. **Windrowing / Swathing** — laying the cut crop into continuous rows for drying or further collection.
> 3. **Threshing & Collection** — separating grain from the cut crop (covered in Module 3).

## 1.2 Mowing — The Engineering Definition

**Mowing** is the mechanical process of severing standing forage crops (grass, hay, alfalfa, oats, etc.) close to the ground surface using a powered cutting mechanism, without necessarily collecting the cut material. A **Mower** is the implement used to perform this cutting action, typically PTO-driven (540/1000 rpm) or hydraulically driven from a tractor.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Barber for Crops" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Barber Shop Model):**
> Think of a mature field of grass as a customer's head of long, unkempt hair. The tractor is the **barber's chair**, the mower is the **scissors/clipper**, the reel/blades are the **sharp teeth of the clipper**, and the swath board or conditioner is the **hand that sweeps the hair off after the cut**. Just as a barber has different tools (scissors, clippers, trimmers), an agricultural engineer has different mower types (reel, rotary, disc, flail) — each suited to a different "haircut style" depending on crop density, moisture, and terrain.

## 1.4 Core Harvesting Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary)

| # | Term | Standard Definition |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Swath** | The width of the cut crop strip deposited by a single pass of the mower. Measured in **metres (m)**. |
| 2 | **Windrow** | One or more swaths laid in a continuous row for drying. |
| 3 | **Stubble** | The portion of the crop stem left standing in the field after cutting. |
| 4 | **Cutting Height** | The vertical distance between the ground and the cutting edge of the mower. Typically **50–100 mm** for hay. |
| 5 | **Reel Speed (V_r)** | Peripheral velocity of the reel bats. Measured in **m/s**. |
| 6 | **Ground Speed (V_g)** | Forward travel speed of the tractor-mower combination. Measured in **km/h** or **m/s**. |
| 7 | **Reel Index (β)** | Ratio of reel peripheral speed to ground speed. Dimensionless. |
| 8 | **Cutter Bar / Knife** | The reciprocating/rotating element that performs the actual severing of the stem. |
| 9 | **Sickle / Section** | The serrated triangular cutting element of a reciprocating mower. |
| 10 | **Conditioner** | Device that crushes/cracks the cut stem to accelerate moisture loss. |
| 11 | **Field Capacity** | The area covered per unit time, expressed in **ha/h** (hectares per hour). |
| 12 | **Maturity Stage** | Physiological stage of crop at which cutting yields maximum nutritional value (typically early bloom for hay). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Reel Index (β)** is the single most critical parameter that determines cutting quality. β = 1.0 is theoretical; in practice, β = **1.2 to 1.5** for hay crops and **1.5 to 2.0** for lodged/leaning crops.

## 1.5 Classification of Mowing Equipment (Top-Level)

- **Based on Cutting Mechanism:** Reel-type, Rotary-type, Flail-type, Disc-type, Drum-type, Sickle-bar (Reciprocating) type.
- **Based on Drive:** PTO-driven, Ground-wheel driven, Hydraulic driven.
- **Based on Mounting:** Tractor-mounted (3-point hitch), Trailed (pull-type), Self-propelled.
- **Based on Function:** Pure mowers, Mower-conditioners, Mower-windrowers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of the Cutting Process

The cutting of a standing crop stem is a **shearing-failure** phenomenon governed by the following sequential steps:

1. **Stem Engagement** — The crop stem is presented to the cutting edge by the reel (if present) or by the forward motion of the cutter bar.
2. **Stem Support & Anvil Action** — One cutting edge holds the stem (anvil/finger) while the moving blade advances (knife action).
3. **Shear Initiation** — The stem is clamped between the moving blade and the stationary ledger. Stress exceeds the stem's **shear strength (τ)**.
4. **Stem Severance** — Failure occurs at a critical shear angle. For a single blade, the failure occurs when:
   $$F_{cut} \geq \tau \cdot A_{stem}$$
   where $A_{stem}$ is the cross-sectional area of the stem.
5. **Material Lay** — The severed stem is conveyed rearward by the reel and deposited as a swath.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Design:**
> Different mower types (reel vs. rotary vs. disc) differ primarily in *how* they present the stem to the blade and *how* they convey the cut material. This dictates the power requirement, ground speed range, and crop suitability.

## 2.2 Detailed Study of Mower Types

### 2.2.1 Reel Mower (Cylinder Mower)

- **Working Principle:** A multi-bladed horizontal **reel** rotates about a horizontal axis, with cutting blades passing over a stationary **bed knife** (ledger plate). Crop stems are sheared between the reel blades and the bed knife.
- **Components:** Reel (4–6 blades), Bat frame, Bed knife, Reel drive, Cutting height adjustment skid.
- **Drive:** Ground-wheel driven (via chain/gear) or PTO-driven via belt.
- **Cutting Action:** Scissor-cut (shearing).
- **Speed Range:** Ground speed **5–10 km/h**; Reel speed **9–15 m/s**.
- **Field Capacity:** 0.4–1.0 ha/h.
- **Applications:** Fine grasses, golf courses, lawns, alfalfa, clover.
- **Advantages:** Clean scissor-cut, low power, ideal for short fine crops.
- **Disadvantages:** Clogs in tall/lodged/wet crops; poor performance on uneven terrain.

### 2.2.2 Rotary Mower

- **Working Principle:** A high-speed **horizontal disc** (or multiple discs) carrying hinged/free blades rotates about a vertical axis. Blades impact-cut the crop by impact-shear.
- **Components:** Rotor disc, Free-swinging blades (2 or 4 per disc), Gearbox, PTO shaft, Skid shoes, Rear swath board.
- **Cutting Action:** Impact (high-speed knife).
- **Blade Tip Speed:** **70–90 m/s** (critical for clean cut).
- **Field Capacity:** 0.8–2.5 ha/h.
- **Applications:** Tall grass, brush, pastures, road-side verges, sugarcane trash.
- **Advantages:** Handles tall, dense, lodged, and woody material; high ground speed; simple design.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher power consumption; ragged cut on fine crops; safety hazard from thrown debris.

### 2.2.3 Disc Mower (Disc Cutter Bar)

- **Working Principle:** Multiple small disc modules (3–8 discs) rotate at high speed in a cutter bar housing. Each disc carries 2 free-swinging knives. Crop is cut between the disc knives and stationary **top skids**.
- **Components:** Cutter bar with disc modules, Idler discs, Drive shaft, Swath disc, Conditioner (if fitted).
- **Cutting Action:** Impact-shear.
- **Disc Speed:** **2,500–3,200 rpm**.
- **Field Capacity:** 1.5–4.0 ha/h.
- **Applications:** Hay and forage harvesting — most widely used in modern agriculture.
- **Advantages:** High ground speed (up to 15 km/h), handles lodged crops, modular design, low maintenance.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher initial cost; complex gear train inside cutter bar.

### 2.2.4 Drum Mower

- **Working Principle:** Two or four large **drums** (diameter 400–600 mm) rotate in opposite directions. Each drum carries 3–4 free-swinging blades at the bottom. Cut material is funneled between the drums into a narrow windrow.
- **Cutting Action:** Impact-shear.
- **Field Capacity:** 1.0–2.5 ha/h.
- **Applications:** Medium-to-tall hay crops.
- **Advantages:** Forms a narrow windrow ready for baler pickup; high capacity; simple drive.
- **Disadvantages:** Heavier than disc mowers; drum overlap zone is maintenance-critical.

### 2.2.5 Sickle-Bar (Reciprocating) Mower

- **Working Principle:** A **sickle bar** with triangular serrated sections reciprocates horizontally against stationary guard fingers. The crop is sheared between the moving sections and the stationary ledger plates on the guards.
- **Components:** Sickle bar, Guards, Ledger plates, Pitman arm, Flywheel, PTO drive.
- **Cutting Action:** Pure shear (scissor action).
- **Stroke Speed:** **700–1,000 strokes/min**.
- **Field Capacity:** 0.3–0.8 ha/h.
- **Applications:** Rice, wheat stubble, small farms, hilly terrain.
- **Advantages:** Very low power (3–5 kW/m of cutter bar); clean cut; low cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Low ground speed; high vibration; high wear on sections and guards; clogs in tangled crops.

### 2.2.6 Flail Mower

- **Working Principle:** A horizontal rotor carrying numerous hinged **flails** (T or Y-shaped blades) rotates at high speed inside a housing. The flails beat and shred the crop. Cut material is expelled through a rear hood.
- **Components:** Rotor shaft, Flail blades (T/Y/L), Rotor housing, Rear roller/skid, PTO drive.
- **Cutting Action:** Impact-beat (hammermill-like).
- **Rotor Speed:** **1,800–2,400 rpm**.
- **Field Capacity:** 0.5–2.0 ha/h.
- **Applications:** Pasture renovation, brush cutting, road-side maintenance, shredded mulch.
- **Advantages:** Tolerates stones and stumps; produces fine mulch; handles woody material up to 30 mm diameter.
- **Disadvantages:** High power consumption; reduced regrowth of grass; flail replacement cost.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Theoretical Field Capacity** | $C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10}$ | ha/h | $W$ = width (m), $V$ = speed (km/h) |
| 2 | **Effective Field Capacity** | $C_E = C_T \cdot \eta_f$ | ha/h | $\eta_f$ = field efficiency |
| 3 | **Field Efficiency** | $\eta_f = \dfrac{C_E}{C_T} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 70–85% |
| 4 | **Reel Index (β)** | $\beta = \dfrac{V_r}{V_g}$ | dimensionless | Optimum: 1.2–1.5 |
| 5 | **Reel Peripheral Speed** | $V_r = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = reel diameter, $N$ = rpm |
| 6 | **Power Requirement (Mowing)** | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot V}{1000}$ | kW | $F$ = draft force, $V$ = speed |
| 7 | **Cutting Force per Stem** | $F_c = \tau \cdot A_{stem}$ | N | $\tau$ = shear strength |
| 8 | **Material Throughput** | $Q = Y \cdot V \cdot W$ | kg/s | $Y$ = yield per unit area |
| 9 | **PTO Speed Ratio** | $i = \dfrac{N_{rotor}}{N_{PTO}}$ | dimensionless | For design of gear train |
| 10 | **Area Covered per Hour** | $A = 0.1 \cdot W \cdot V$ | ha/h | Same as $C_T$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbols:** The vertical bar `|` is strictly avoided in all table cells. The unit notation uses `\vert` (e.g., $\text{m/s} \vert \text{theoretical}$) to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forage Quality Optimisation:** Mower-conditioner combinations reduce hay drying time from 4 days to 1.5 days — directly impacting farm economics.
- **Safety Engineering:** Disc mowers with retractable knives prevent foreign-object damage and reduce downtime.
- **Precision Agriculture:** GPS-guided auto-steer systems on tractor-mounted disc mowers achieve sub-cm swath alignment, improving baler pickup efficiency by 8–12%.
- **Terrain Adaptation:** Flail and rotary mowers are mandated on slopes > 20° for stability and anti-overturn safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Field Capacity (C_T)

**Given:** A mower of working width $W$ metres moves at a forward speed $V$ in metres per second.

**Step 1 — Distance covered in one hour:**
In 1 hour (3600 seconds), the mower travels:
$$d = V \cdot 3600 \quad \text{metres}$$

**Step 2 — Area covered in one hour (rectangular strip):**
The mower covers a rectangular strip of length $d$ and width $W$:
$$A_{m^2} = W \cdot d = W \cdot V \cdot 3600 \quad \text{metre}^2$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hectares:**
1 hectare = 10,000 m². Therefore:
$$C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V \cdot 3600}{10000} \quad \text{ha/h}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form (when V is in km/h):**
If $V$ is in km/h, then $V_{m/s} = V \times \dfrac{1000}{3600} = \dfrac{V}{3.6}$. Substituting:
$$C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V \cdot 1000}{10000} = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10} \quad \text{ha/h}$$

> **Textual Logic:** The factor 10 arises because $1 \text{ ha} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$ and $1 \text{ km} = 1000 \text{ m}$, yielding the conversion coefficient $\dfrac{1000}{10000} = 0.1 = \dfrac{1}{10}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Effective Field Capacity (C_E)

**Given:** The theoretical field capacity is $C_T$. The mower does not operate at 100% efficiency due to:
- Time lost in turning at headlands ($T_t$)
- Time lost in field adjustments and minor stops ($T_a$)
- Time lost in overlap at row ends ($T_o$)

**Step 1 — Define field efficiency:**
$$\eta_f = \dfrac{\text{Productive field time}}{\text{Total time in field}} = \dfrac{T_p}{T_p + T_t + T_a + T_o}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply theoretical capacity by efficiency:**
$$C_E = C_T \cdot \eta_f = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10} \cdot \eta_f \quad \text{ha/h}$$

**Step 3 — Express in SI:**
$$C_E = \dfrac{W \cdot V \cdot \eta_f}{10} \quad \text{ha/h}$$

> **Textual Logic:** Multiplying $C_T$ (the ideal 100% capacity) by the dimensionless efficiency $\eta_f$ directly gives the *real* area covered per hour, accounting for the unavoidable non-productive time fractions.

## 3.3 Derivation of Reel Index (β) and Reel Peripheral Speed (V_r)

**Given:** A reel mower with reel diameter $D$ metres, rotating at $N$ rpm, with tractor ground speed $V_g$.

**Step 1 — Reel peripheral speed:**
The tip of each reel bat traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$. In one minute, the tip travels:
$$V_r = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{metres/minute}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to m/s:**
$$V_r = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Reel Index:**
By definition:
$$\beta = \dfrac{V_r}{V_g}$$

**Step 4 — Required reel rpm for a given β:**
$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot \beta \cdot V_g}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{rpm}$$

> **Textual Logic:** The reel must travel faster than the ground so that the bats "flick" the cut stems rearward into the swath. If $\beta = 1$, the reel merely follows the ground — no sweeping action. If $\beta < 1$, the reel pushes the crop forward, causing clogging.

## 3.4 Derivation of Power Requirement for Mowing

**Step 1 — Energy to cut single stem:**
$$E_{stem} = F_c \cdot x_c = \tau \cdot A_{stem} \cdot x_c \quad \text{Joules}$$
where $x_c$ is the cutting displacement (~ 5–10 mm for hay stems).

**Step 2 — Stems cut per second:**
If the crop density is $\rho$ stems per m² and the mower cuts a strip of width $W$ at speed $V$:
$$n = \rho \cdot W \cdot V \quad \text{stems/s}$$

**Step 3 — Total cutting power:**
$$P_{cut} = n \cdot E_{stem} = \tau \cdot A_{stem} \cdot x_c \cdot \rho \cdot W \cdot V \quad \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Add idle power (friction, reel rotation, driveline losses):**
$$P_{total} = \dfrac{P_{cut}}{\eta_{transmission}} + P_{idle} \quad \text{W}$$

> **Textual Logic:** This derivation is rooted in the first-principle energy balance — the energy needed to shear $n$ stems per second, divided by the transmission efficiency, plus the no-load power of the machine.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** A tractor-mounted disc mower has a working width of **2.4 m** and is operated at a forward speed of **9 km/h**. The field efficiency is **80%**. Calculate:
1. Theoretical Field Capacity
2. Effective Field Capacity
3. If the field is 4.0 ha, how long does mowing take?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical Field Capacity:**
$$C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10} = \dfrac{2.4 \times 9}{10} = \dfrac{21.6}{10} = 2.16 \text{ ha/h}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Field Capacity:**
$$C_E = C_T \cdot \eta_f = 2.16 \times 0.80 = 1.728 \text{ ha/h}$$

**Step 3 — Time required for 4.0 ha:**
$$T = \dfrac{A}{C_E} = \dfrac{4.0}{1.728} = 2.3148 \text{ hours} \approx 2 \text{ h } 19 \text{ min}$$

> **Verification by dimensional check:** [ha] ÷ [ha/h] = [h] ✓

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mower Selection Decision

| Crop Condition | Recommended Mower | Reason |
|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| Fine grass / lawn (short, uniform) | Reel mower | Scissor-cut, low power, clean finish |
| Tall dense grass (1–2 m) | Rotary / Flail | High throughput, robust cutting |
| Hay / Alfalfa (lodged, tangled) | Disc mower | High ground speed, anti-clog |
| Rice stubble / paddy straw | Sickle-bar | Low cost, suited to soft stems |
| Brush / woody up to 30 mm dia | Flail mower | Hammermill action |
| Sloped terrain (>15°) | Drum / Disc | Lower centre of gravity |
| Wet grass (>25% moisture) | Disc / Rotary | No clogging in wet mass |
| Mixed pasture renovation | Flail | Produces mulch for regrowth |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Mower Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MOWING EQUIPMENT] --> B[Reel Mower]
    A --> C[Rotary Mower]
    A --> D[Disc Mower]
    A --> E[Drum Mower]
    A --> F[Sickle Bar Mower]
    A --> G[Flail Mower]

    B --> B1[Ground Wheel Drive]
    B --> B2[PTO Drive]
    B --> B3[Used For Fine Grass]

    C --> C1[Horizontal Disc]
    C --> C2[Free Swinging Blades]
    C --> C3[Tall and Dense Crops]

    D --> D1[Multiple Disc Modules]
    D --> D2[2 Free Knives Per Disc]
    D --> D3[High Speed Hay Cutting]

    E --> E1[Two Drum System]
    E --> E2[Narrow Windrow Output]
    E --> E3[Medium Tall Forage]

    F --> F1[Reciprocating Knife]
    F --> F2[Stationary Guards]
    F --> F3[Small Farms and Paddy]

    G --> G1[Hinged T or Y Flails]
    G --> G2[High Rotor Speed]
    G --> G3[Brush and Pasture Mulching]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Reel Mower

```mermaid
graph LR
    GW[Ground Wheel] -->|Drives| CS[Chain and Sprocket]
    CS -->|Transmits| REEL[Reel Assembly]
    REEL -->|Peripheral Velocity| BATS[Reel Bats]

    PTO[Tractor PTO Shaft] -->|540 rpm| GB[Gearbox]
    GB -->|Step Up| REEL

    BATS -->|Sweep and Cut| BK[Stationary Bed Knife]
    BATS -->|Convey Rearward| SW[Swath Board]
    SW -->|Deposit| SD[Cut Swath on Ground]

    CH[Chassis and Frame] --> SK[Skid Shoes]
    SK -->|Control| CH_H[Cutting Height]

    style REEL fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BK fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SD fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Diagram of a Disc Mower

```mermaid
graph TD
    PTO1[Tractor PTO 540 rpm] --> SH1[PTO Shaft with Overrun Clutch]
    SH1 --> GB1[Main Gearbox]
    GB1 -->|Belt or Gear Drive| CB1[Cutter Bar Drive Shaft]
    CB1 --> DISC1[Disc Module 1]
    CB1 --> DISC2[Disc Module 2]
    CB1 --> DISC3[Disc Module 3]
    CB1 --> DISC4[Disc Module 4]

    DISC1 --> K1[Knife 1 and 2 Free Swinging]
    DISC2 --> K2[Knife 1 and 2 Free Swinging]
    DISC3 --> K3[Knife 1 and 2 Free Swinging]
    DISC4 --> K4[Knife 1 and 2 Free Swinging]

    K1 --> CROP1[Impact Cut on Standing Crop]
    K2 --> CROP1
    K3 --> CROP1
    K4 --> CROP1

    CROP1 --> COND1[Conditioner Rolls if Fitted]
    COND1 --> WIND1[Windrow Formation]
    WIND1 --> GROUND1[Ground Windrow]

    style GB1 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style COND1 fill:#ddf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GROUND1 fill:#dfd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Cutting Action Comparison (Reel vs Rotary vs Sickle)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Standing Crop Stem] --> B{Type of Cutter}

    B -->|Reel Mower| C1[Scissor Shear Cut]
    C1 --> C1a[Low Power]
    C1 --> C1b[Clean Wound on Stem]
    C1 --> C1c[Best for Hay and Lawn]

    B -->|Rotary Mower| C2[Impact Shear Cut]
    C2 --> C2a[High Power]
    C2 --> C2b[Ragged Stem End]
    C2 --> C2c[Best for Brush and Tall Grass]

    B -->|Sickle Bar Mower| C3[Reciprocating Shear]
    C3 --> C3a[Very Low Power]
    C3 --> C3b[Clean Cut at Low Speed]
    C3 --> C3c[Best for Paddy and Stubble]

    B -->|Flail Mower| C4[Beating and Shredding]
    C4 --> C4a[Highest Power]
    C4 --> C4b[Mulched Output]
    C4 --> C4c[Best for Pasture Renovation]

    style C1 fill:#cfe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C2 fill:#fec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C3 fill:#cef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C4 fill:#fce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define harvesting. List any four operations involved in the harvesting sequence.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Harvesting** is the agricultural operation of cutting, gathering, and removing the mature economic portion of a crop from the field, separating it from the stubble left in the soil.

The four sequential harvesting operations are:
1. **Mowing / Cutting** — severing the standing crop from the stubble.
2. **Windrowing / Swathing** — laying the cut crop in rows for drying.
3. **Threshing / Shelling** — separating grain from the ear/panicle (Module 3).
4. **Collection / Bagging** — gathering the threshed grain into sacks or bulk storage.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a reel mower and a rotary mower on the basis of cutting action, power requirement, and field capacity.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Reel Mower | Rotary Mower |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Cutting Action** | Scissor-type shearing between reel blades and bed knife | High-speed impact-shear by free-swinging blades on a rotating disc |
| **Power Requirement** | Low (1.5–3 kW/m) | High (4–8 kW/m) |
| **Field Capacity** | Low to Medium (0.4–1.0 ha/h) | High (0.8–2.5 ha/h) |
| **Best Suited Crop** | Fine grass, lawn, alfalfa | Tall, dense, lodged, brushy crops |
| **Blade Tip Speed** | 9–15 m/s (reel peripheral) | 70–90 m/s (disc peripheral) |
| **Cut Quality** | Very clean | Ragged on fine crops |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **disc mower** with a neat block diagram. List its advantages over a sickle-bar mower. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tractor-mounted disc mower has a cutting width of **3.0 m** and is operated at a forward speed of **10 km/h**. The field efficiency is **82%**. Calculate: (i) Theoretical Field Capacity, (ii) Effective Field Capacity, and (iii) Time required to mow a **6.0 ha** field. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction of a Disc Mower:**

A disc mower consists of the following major components:
1. **Main Frame and 3-Point Hitch** — for mounting on the tractor.
2. **PTO Shaft with Overrun Clutch** — receives 540 rpm from tractor.
3. **Main Gearbox** — steps up/down the speed and transmits to the cutter bar.
4. **Cutter Bar Housing** — an oblong sealed box containing the disc modules and idler gears.
5. **Disc Modules (3 to 8)** — each carries 2 free-swinging knives at 180° apart.
6. **Idler Discs** — drive neighbouring discs in opposite direction.
7. **Top Skids** — replace guards; act as ledger plates for shearing.
8. **Swath Discs / Cones** — funnel cut material into a windrow.
9. **Hydraulic Lift Cylinder** — raises/lowers the cutter bar at headlands.

**Working Principle:**
- The tractor PTO drives the main gearbox.
- The gearbox transmits power to the first disc via a shaft entering the cutter bar.
- Adjacent discs are driven by idler gears in opposite directions.
- Each disc rotates at **2,500–3,200 rpm**.
- Free-swinging knives pivot outward under centrifugal force and impact-cut the standing crop against the stationary top skids.
- Cut material is conveyed rearward by the rotating discs and formed into a windrow by the swath cones.

**Advantages over Sickle-Bar Mower:**
1. **Higher ground speed** (10–15 km/h vs 4–6 km/h).
2. **No clogging** in lodged or tangled crops.
3. **Lower maintenance** — no reciprocating parts, no pitman wear.
4. **Better windrow formation** built into the design.
5. **Modular replacement** of discs and knives.
6. **Higher field capacity** (1.5–4.0 ha/h vs 0.3–0.8 ha/h).

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $W = 3.0$ m, $V = 10$ km/h, $\eta_f = 82\% = 0.82$, $A = 6.0$ ha.

**(i) Theoretical Field Capacity:**
$$C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10}{10} = 3.0 \text{ ha/h}$$

**(ii) Effective Field Capacity:**
$$C_E = C_T \cdot \eta_f = 3.0 \times 0.82 = 2.46 \text{ ha/h}$$

**(iii) Time Required:**
$$T = \dfrac{A}{C_E} = \dfrac{6.0}{2.46} = 2.439 \text{ hours}$$
$$T = 2 \text{ h } + 0.439 \times 60 \text{ min} = 2 \text{ h } 26 \text{ min } 20 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:** $C_T = 3.0$ ha/h, $C_E = 2.46$ ha/h, $T \approx 2$ h 26 min.

---

### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **flail mower**. List its applications and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Reel Index (β)**. A reel mower has a reel diameter of **1.2 m** and rotates at **60 rpm**. The tractor ground speed is **6 km/h**. Calculate the reel index and state whether the setting is suitable for a hay crop. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction of a Flail Mower:**

A flail mower consists of:
1. **Main Rotor** — a heavy horizontal shaft supported in bearings on both ends.
2. **Flails** — T-shaped or Y-shaped hinged blades attached to the rotor via chain links or bolted lugs.
3. **Rotor Housing** — a curved hood covering the top and sides of the rotor.
4. **Rear Roller or Skid** — controls cutting height.
5. **PTO Drive Shaft and Gearbox** — steps up tractor speed to rotor speed.
6. **Lateral Movement Mechanism** — for road-side mowers; offsets the mower beyond the tractor width.

**Working Principle:**
- The tractor PTO drives the rotor at **1,800–2,400 rpm**.
- Flails swing outward under centrifugal force.
- The flails beat and shred standing vegetation, woody stems, and grass.
- Cut material is repeatedly impacted, reduced to mulch, and expelled through the rear hood.
- The rear roller (or skid) sets the cutting height.

**Applications:**
1. Pasture renovation and weed control.
2. Road-side and rail-track vegetation maintenance.
3. Cutting brush and saplings up to 30 mm diameter.
4. Orchards and plantation inter-row mowing.
5. Stubble mulching in conservation agriculture.

**Limitations:**
1. Very high power consumption (8–12 kW/m).
2. High flail wear and replacement cost.
3. Reduced grass regrowth due to severe impact damage.
4. Noise and dust generation.
5. Not suitable for clean-cut hay production (ragged output).

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definition of Reel Index:**
The reel index (β) is the ratio of the **peripheral velocity of the reel bats** to the **forward ground speed of the tractor**. It is a dimensionless parameter:
$$\beta = \dfrac{V_r}{V_g}$$

**Given:** $D = 1.2$ m, $N = 60$ rpm, $V_g = 6$ km/h.

**Step 1 — Compute Reel Peripheral Speed (V_r):**
$$V_r = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 60}{60} = \pi \times 1.2 = 3.7699 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Ground Speed to m/s:**
$$V_g = 6 \text{ km/h} = 6 \times \dfrac{1000}{3600} = 1.6667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Reel Index:**
$$\beta = \dfrac{V_r}{V_g} = \dfrac{3.7699}{1.6667} = 2.2619$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Suitability:**

For **standing hay crop**, the recommended reel index is **β = 1.2 to 1.5**.

The computed value **β ≈ 2.26** is **higher than the recommended range**, indicating:
- The reel is rotating too fast relative to ground speed.
- Risk of **bats striking the cut crop with excessive force**, causing shatter loss and loss of leaves (which contain most of the protein).
- Recommended remedy: **Reduce reel rpm to ~ 40 rpm** (which gives $\beta = 1.51$) or **increase ground speed**.

**Re-calculated optimum N:**
$$N_{opt} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \beta_{opt} \cdot V_g}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{60 \times 1.4 \times 1.6667}{\pi \times 1.2} \approx 37.04 \text{ rpm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\beta = 2.26$, setting is **not suitable** for hay; reduce N to ~ 40 rpm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s when calculating reel peripheral speed. Marks are docked if the unit is not shown.
> 2. **Field Efficiency Confusion:** Do not confuse **mechanical efficiency (η_m)** with **field efficiency (η_f)**. They are entirely different — η_m is about power transmission, η_f is about time utilization.
> 3. **Forgetting Overrun Clutch:** When describing disc mowers, marks are lost if the PTO overrun clutch (free-wheel) is not mentioned. It is a critical safety component.
> 4. **Reel Index Range:** Do not state β = 1.0 as the optimum — this is the *theoretical minimum* where no sweeping occurs. Always state the **practical range (1.2–2.0)**.
> 5. **Confusing "Swath" with "Windrow":** Swath is a single strip from one pass; windrow is the accumulated row of one or more swaths.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- [ ] **Harvesting** = Cutting + Gathering + Collection of mature crop.
- [ ] **Mowing** is a sub-operation of harvesting — only cutting the standing crop.
- [ ] **Six major mower types:** Reel, Rotary, Disc, Drum, Sickle-bar, Flail.
- [ ] **Reel mower** → Scissor cut → Fine grass → Low power (1.5–3 kW/m) → β = 1.2–1.5.
- [ ] **Rotary mower** → Impact cut → Tall/brushy → High power (4–8 kW/m) → Blade tip speed 70–90 m/s.
- [ ] **Disc mower** → Impact-shear via multiple discs → Hay/forage → 2,500–3,200 rpm disc speed.
- [ ] **Drum mower** → Opposite rotating drums → Narrow windrow.
- [ ] **Sickle-bar** → Reciprocating knife → Paddy/stubble → 700–1,000 strokes/min.
- [ ] **Flail mower** → Beating/shredding → Brush/pasture → 1,800–2,400 rpm rotor.
- [ ] **Swath** = single pass strip; **Windrow** = accumulated drying row.
- [ ] **Stubble** = standing residue after cutting; **Cutting height** = 50–100 mm for hay.
- [ ] **Theoretical Field Capacity:** $C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10}$ ha/h (W in m, V in km/h).
- [ ] **Effective Field Capacity:** $C_E = C_T \cdot \eta_f$ (η_f = 70–85%).
- [ ] **Reel Peripheral Speed:** $V_r = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ m/s.
- [ ] **Reel Index:** $\beta = \dfrac{V_r}{V_g}$ → Optimum 1.2–1.5 for hay.
- [ ] **Mower-conditioners** accelerate hay drying 3-fold (reduce 4 days → 1.5 days).
- [ ] **PTO speed:** standard **540 rpm** at rated engine speed; **1000 rpm** for large HP tractors.
- [ ] **Safety:** Always disengage PTO, stop tractor, and lower mower before clearing blockages.
- [ ] **Modern Trend:** GPS auto-steer on disc mowers improves swath alignment by **8–12%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Probable KTU 2024 ESE Questions (High-Weightage Topics):**
> 1. Compare **disc mower vs sickle-bar mower** (favourite KTU question).
> 2. Derive **$C_T$ and $C_E$** with a numerical problem.
> 3. Explain **construction and working of flail mower** OR **disc mower**.
> 4. Define **reel index** and solve a numerical.
> 5. Classify **mowers** with a tree diagram (asked frequently in Part A).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructional Details of a Tractor** refers to the systematic study of the *geometric arrangement, mechanical assembly, and functional inter-relationship* of all major sub-systems that constitute a modern farm tractor. It encompasses the **prime mover (engine)**, **power transmission train** (clutch, gearbox, differential, final drive, rear axle), **running gear** (wheels, tyres, axles, steering), **implement coupling systems** (PTO, three-point linkage, drawbar, hydraulic lift), and the **operator station** (seat, dashboard, controls, safety frame).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, constructional details specifically deal with the **"how-it-is-built"** rather than the **"how-it-works"** (which is operational principle) and **"how-to-specify"** (which is performance characteristics).

### 1.2 Key Subsystems at a Glance

| # | Subsystem | Primary Function | Constructional Feature |
|---|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Engine | Converts chemical → mechanical energy | Multi-cylinder, vertical, water-cooled diesel |
| 2 | Clutch | Disengages engine from gearbox | Single/double plate, dry friction type |
| 3 | Gearbox | Torque & speed variation | Sliding/spur/synchromesh mesh gears |
| 4 | Differential | Allows wheels to rotate at different speeds | Bevel gear assembly with crown wheel & pinion |
| 5 | Rear Axle & Final Drive | Final speed reduction to drive wheels | Bull/Epicyclic reduction gears |
| 6 | Steering System | Directional control | Mechanical (Ackermann) / Hydraulic power steering |
| 7 | Brake System | Speed control & parking | Mechanical (expanding shoe) / Hydraulic disc/drum |
| 8 | PTO Shaft | Power delivery to implements | 540 rpm / 1000 rpm, 6-spline / 21-spline |
| 9 | Three-Point Hitch | Mounting & controlling implements | Category I/II/III with hydraulic lift |
| 10 | Hydraulic System | Lift, lower & control implements | Open-centre / Closed-centre, position & draft control |
| 11 | Tyres & Wheels | Traction & flotation | Pneumatic, ribbed (front), lugged (rear) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Tractor as a "Mechanical Horse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a farm tractor exactly like a *strong, obedient mechanical horse* that pulls and powers farm implements.
> - The **engine** is the *heart* that pumps energy (like the horse's lungs and muscles).
> - The **clutch** is the *rider's hand on the reins* — pressing it momentarily disconnects the horse from the cart.
> - The **gearbox** is the *rider choosing a pace* — walk, trot, or gallop (low/high gear ratios).
> - The **differential** is the *horse's flexible hip joint* — lets one hind leg move faster than the other when turning.
> - The **PTO shaft** is the *horse's gear system that can also drive a mill or a water pump from its back*.
> - The **three-point hitch** is the *harness and yoke* that connects the cart (implement) to the horse's back.
> - The **steering system** is the *rider's hands pulling the bridle left or right*.

This analogy helps a student visualize why each subsystem has a *specific geometric form* — the form always follows the function.

## 1.4 Why Constructional Details Matter in KTU Examinations

- KTU frequently tests **identification of components** from line diagrams (typically 3–5 marks).
- Examiner expects knowledge of **material selection** (e.g., why differential gears use case-hardened alloy steel).
- Students must be able to **draw sectional views** of the clutch, gearbox, and differential.
- Questions often test **dimensional standards** like PTO speed (**540 rpm**), tyre sizes, and hitch categories.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Constructional details of tractor, special types of vehicles, and farm equipment — covering prime mover, transmission, steering, brakes, hydraulic system, PTO, and hitch.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Prime Mover — Engine Constructional Details

### 2.1.1 General Layout
A modern farm tractor uses a **vertical, in-line, multi-cylinder, four-stroke, water-cooled, compression-ignition (diesel) engine** typically rated between **25 HP to 75 HP** for agricultural use, and up to **200+ HP** for heavy-duty operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vertical?** Vertical orientation keeps the engine's centre of gravity low (improving tractor stability on slopes), and the crankshaft lies along the longitudinal axis — this aligns naturally with the rear-wheel drive configuration.

### 2.1.2 Major Engine Sub-Components

- **Cylinder Block**: The main structure. Made of **cast iron** (older designs) or **aluminium alloy** (modern designs for weight saving). Houses the cylinder bores.
- **Cylinder Head**: Bolted to the top of the block. Contains **intake valves, exhaust valves, fuel injectors, and sometimes glow plugs** (for cold starting).
- **Piston**: Reciprocating element. Made of **aluminium alloy** (low weight, good heat conduction) with **ring grooves** for compression rings (top 2) and oil control rings (bottom 1).
- **Connecting Rod**: Links piston to crankshaft. **Drop-forged steel** construction with big-end (crankshaft side) and small-end (piston pin side).
- **Crankshaft**: Forged alloy steel. Converts reciprocating motion to rotary motion. Has main journals and crank pins with counterweights.
- **Flywheel**: Heavy cast-iron disc bolted to the rear of the crankshaft. Stores rotational inertia for smooth torque delivery — critical for tractors because ploughing loads are highly variable.
- **Camshaft**: Operates the valves via rocker arms. Driven by a timing gear from the crankshaft.
- **Valves**: **Poppet-type**, typically 2 valves per cylinder (1 intake, 1 exhaust), with some modern engines using **4 valves per cylinder** for better breathing.
- **Lubrication System**: **Splash + Pressurized** (wet sump). Oil pump draws oil from the sump, filters it, and distributes it under pressure.
- **Cooling System**: **Water-cooled, forced circulation** with a water pump, thermostat, and radiator. Tractors often have a **pusher fan** (fan in front of radiator) for better dust clearance in field conditions.
- **Fuel System**: **Inline injection pump** (older) or **Rotary/distributor pump** (modern) with mechanical or electronic injectors.
- **Air Intake**: Equipped with a **cyclone pre-cleaner** + oil-bath/dry air filter — essential to handle dusty agricultural environments.
- **Governor**: **Mechanical (centrifugal) or hydraulic** — maintains nearly constant engine speed under varying load (critical for PTO-driven implements).

### 2.1.3 Constructional Features Specific to Tractor Engines
- **High Torque Backup (200–300%)**: Built using a long-stroke design (longer piston stroke relative to bore) for better lugging ability.
- **Low Rated RPM (1500–2400 rpm)**: Reduces wear, fuel consumption, and noise.
- **High Compression Ratio (16:1 to 22:1)**: Required for diesel auto-ignition.
- **Heavy-Duty Bearings**: Tapered roller main bearings and connecting-rod bearings withstand shock loads from ploughing.

## 2.2 The Clutch — Constructional Details

### 2.2.1 Function
The clutch is a **friction coupling device** that connects/disconnects the engine crankshaft from the gearbox input shaft, allowing smooth gear changes and engine starting without load.

### 2.2.2 Types Used in Tractors
1. **Single-Plate Dry Friction Clutch** — Most common.
2. **Dual-Plate Dry Friction Clutch** — Used in high-torque tractors (e.g., larger John Deere, Mahindra Arjun series).
3. **Multi-Plate (Wet Type)** — Found in some hydrostatic drives.
4. **Centrifugal Clutch** — Used in small power tillers and walk-behind tractors.

### 2.2.3 Constructional Components of a Single-Plate Clutch
- **Flywheel**: The driving member. The friction plate is pressed against its machined face.
- **Clutch Plate (Driven Plate)**: Has a splined hub that slides on the gearbox input shaft. Carries friction linings on both sides.
- **Pressure Plate**: Applies clamping force on the driven plate via coil springs (or diaphragm spring in modern units).
- **Clutch Springs**: Usually **9 to 12 helical compression springs** distributed circumferentially.
- **Clutch Pedal / Lever**: Driver's control input.
- **Release (Throw-out) Bearing**: Pushes the pressure plate fingers when the clutch is depressed.
- **Clutch Housing / Bell Housing**: Encloses the clutch assembly; bolts to the engine block and gearbox.

## 2.3 The Gearbox — Constructional Details

### 2.3.1 Function
Provides **multiple forward and reverse speed ratios** to match engine power with varying field/road load conditions.

### 2.3.2 Typical Configuration in a Tractor
- **Main Gearbox**: 4 or 5 forward speeds + 1 reverse.
- **Range Gearbox (Hi-Lo or creeper)**: Doubles the available speeds.
- **Total Available Speeds**: 8 forward + 2 reverse (common) up to **16 forward + 4 reverse** in modern tractors.
- **Constant Mesh Gearbox with Sliding Mesh Engagement** is the traditional KTU-favoured construction.

### 2.3.3 Key Components
- **Gears**: Spur gears (for parallel shafts) and bevel gears (for perpendicular shafts). Made of **case-hardened alloy steel** (e.g., EN 353, 20MnCr5).
- **Shafts**: Input shaft, countershaft (lay shaft), main shaft (output shaft), reverse shaft, PTO shaft.
- **Bearings**: **Ball bearings** (light duty positions) and **tapered roller bearings** (heavy duty — main shaft, countershaft ends).
- **Synchromesh Unit** (if synchromesh gearbox): Brass synchro rings with dog clutches for smooth gear engagement.
- **Shift Levers / Selector Mechanism**: H-pattern or range-shift pattern.

## 2.4 The Differential — Constructional Details

### 2.4.1 Function
Allows the **two driving (rear) wheels** to rotate at different speeds when the tractor turns, while still transmitting equal torque to both wheels.

### 2.4.2 Constructional Components
- **Differential Case (Housing)**: Cast iron or aluminium. Holds the entire assembly.
- **Crown Wheel (Ring Gear)**: Large bevel gear bolted to the differential case. Driven by the pinion.
- **Pinion**: Small bevel gear on the propeller shaft. Engages with the crown wheel. Typically 6–10 teeth.
- **Differential Cross (Spider)**: A cross-shaped piece with 4 pin journals.
- **Side Gears (Sun Gears)**: Two bevel gears mounted on the half-shafts, meshing with the pinions.
- **Pinion Gears (Planet Gears)**: Two (or four) bevel gears mounted on the cross pinions, free to rotate.
- **Half-Shafts (Axle Shafts)**: Splined shafts transmitting power to the rear wheels.
- **Differential Lock**: A dog-clutch mechanism that locks the two side gears together, forcing both wheels to rotate at the same speed — essential when one wheel loses traction in soft soil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** The differential works on the **"Epicyclic gear principle"** — when the tractor moves straight, the cross (and pinions) don't rotate; when turning, they rotate on their own axis, allowing one side gear to turn faster than the other.

## 2.5 The Rear Axle and Final Drive

### 2.5.1 Function
The final drive provides the **last stage of speed reduction and torque multiplication** before the power reaches the drive wheels.

### 2.5.2 Types of Final Drive
1. **Bull Gear (Spur Gear) Final Drive** — Single reduction, large external gear on each half-shaft. Common in older Massey Ferguson designs.
2. **Epicyclic (Planetary) Final Drive** — Compact, concentric with the wheel hub. Common in modern tractors.
3. **Bevel Pinion and Bull Shaft** — An additional bevel reduction.

## 2.6 The Steering System — Constructional Details

### 2.6.1 Types
- **Manual (Mechanical) Steering**: Recirculating ball or rack-and-pinion type. Found in small/older tractors.
- **Power Steering (Hydraulic)**: Standard in modern tractors above 25 HP. Uses a **hydraulic steering unit** (orbitrol valve) and double-acting cylinder.
- **Hydrostatic Steering**: Steering wheel directly drives a hydraulic pump; no mechanical link.

### 2.6.2 Ackermann Steering Geometry
The fundamental constructional principle ensures that **all four wheels turn about a common instantaneous centre** during cornering. The inner wheel turns more sharply than the outer wheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ackermann Condition:** If $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$ are the inner and outer wheel steer angles, and $L$ is the wheelbase, $t$ is the track width:
> $$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{t}{L}$$
> This is the **fundamental constructional equation** that any steering linkage must satisfy.

## 2.7 The Brake System — Constructional Details

### 2.7.1 Types
- **Mechanical Brakes (Internal Expanding Shoe Type)** — Most common in Indian tractors. Each rear wheel has its own brake operated by pedals.
- **Hydraulic Brakes (Drum or Disc Type)** — Found in larger, modern tractors.
- **Parking Brake** — A hand-operated lever acting on a separate drum or on the transmission.

### 2.7.2 Constructional Components (Mechanical Drum Brake)
- **Brake Drum**: Rotating with the wheel.
- **Brake Shoe (Two)**: Lined with friction material (asbestos-free in modern units).
- **Brake Actuating Cam or Expander**: Spreads the shoes outward against the drum.
- **Brake Rods & Levers**: Mechanical linkage from the pedal.
- **Equalizer**: Ensures equal braking force on both wheels.

## 2.8 The Power Take-Off (PTO) Shaft

### 2.8.1 Constructional Standards
- **Standard Speed**: **540 rpm** (most common, 6-spline, 35 mm diameter) for general implements.
- **Economy PTO**: **750 rpm** at rated engine rpm.
- **High-Power PTO**: **1000 rpm** (21-spline, 35 mm diameter) for high-power implements.
- **Rotation**: Clockwise when viewed from the rear of the tractor.
- **Location**: Centre-line of the rear of the tractor, **standardized height above ground (typically 450–750 mm depending on tractor class)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The **6-spline, 35 mm diameter, 540 rpm** PTO is the most-tested specification in KTU examinations.

## 2.9 The Three-Point Hitch

### 2.9.1 Constructional Components
- **Two Lower Links (Draft Links)**: Connect to the lower hitch points on the implement. Free to swing laterally.
- **One Top Link (Centre Link)**: Adjustable length; connects the top of the implement to the tractor.
- **Lift Arms**: Powered by the hydraulic system; raise/lower the lower links.
- **Lift (Rock) Shaft**: Transverse shaft connected to the hydraulic cylinder via a piston/lever mechanism.
- **Hitch Categories**: I, II, and III — standardized pin diameters of **22 mm, 28 mm, and 36 mm** respectively.
- **Implement Quick-Coupler**: Optional device for fast implement attachment.

## 2.10 The Hydraulic System — Constructional Details

### 2.10.1 Components
- **Hydraulic Oil Reservoir (Sump)**: Typically integrated with the rear axle housing.
- **Hydraulic Pump**: **Gear pump** (fixed displacement) or **axial piston pump** (variable displacement). Driven by the engine.
- **Control Valves**: **Spool-type directional control valve** (DCV). Modern tractors have multiple SCVs (Selective Control Valves) for auxiliary implements.
- **Hydraulic Cylinder**: Single-acting (lift) or double-acting (remote cylinders).
- **Filter**: Suction-line strainer + return-line filter.
- **Position Control, Draft Control, and Mixed Control**: Three modes of operation of the hitch system.

## 2.11 Wheels, Tyres, and Chassis

### 2.11.1 Tyre Specifications
- **Front Tyres**: Smaller, **ribbed (multi-rib)** for steering.
- **Rear Tyres**: Larger, **lugged (R-1 agricultural)** for traction. Optional **R-2 (rice & cane)** and **R-4 (industrial)**.
- **Tyre Size Designation**: e.g., **6.00-16** (front), **13.6-28** (rear) — width(in)-rim diameter(in).
- **Inflation Pressure**: Typically **80–180 kPa** (low pressure for better traction and reduced soil compaction).

### 2.11.2 Chassis
- **Unit-Frame Chassis**: Engine, gearbox, and rear axle integrated into a single cast-iron housing — no separate frame.
- **Frameless Construction**: Used in small tractors.

## 2.12 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM-READY cheat sheet for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Constructional Details.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Ackermann Steering Condition** | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{t}{L}$ | Radians or degrees | Steering linkage design |
| 2 | **Turning Radius** | $R = \dfrac{L}{\sin \delta}$ | m | Tractor manoeuvrability |
| 3 | **Wheelbase (L)** | Typical: 1.8 – 2.5 m | m | Layout dimension |
| 4 | **Track Width (t)** | Adjustable: 1.0 – 2.0 m | m | Crop row spacing |
| 5 | **PTO Speed Standard** | **540 rpm @ engine rated rpm** (6 spline, 35 mm dia) | rpm / mm | Implement drive |
| 6 | **PTO Power Formula** | $P_{PTO} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_{PTO} \cdot T_{PTO}}{60}$ | W (or HP) | PTO power rating |
| 7 | **Gear Ratio (Single Stage)** | $i = \dfrac{Z_{driven}}{Z_{driver}} = \dfrac{N_{driver}}{N_{driven}}$ | dimensionless | Transmission design |
| 8 | **Overall Gear Ratio** | $i_{total} = i_{clutch} \cdot i_{gearbox} \cdot i_{diff} \cdot i_{final}$ | dimensionless | Tractor speed calculation |
| 9 | **Tractor Forward Speed** | $V = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N_{engine}}{60 \cdot i_{total}}$ | m/s | Field/road speed |
| 10 | **Engine Torque from Power** | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2 \pi \cdot N}$ | N·m | Clutch / driveline sizing |
| 11 | **Differential Speed Relationship** | $N_{left} + N_{right} = 2 \cdot N_{carrier}$ | rpm | Differential action |
| 12 | **Hydraulic Lift Force** | $F = P_{oil} \cdot A_{piston}$ | N | Hitch lift capacity |
| 13 | **Hydraulic Pressure (Pump)** | $P = \dfrac{T \cdot 2 \pi \cdot \eta_{vol}}{V_{disp}}$ | Pa (or bar) | Pump selection |
| 14 | **Compression Ratio (Diesel)** | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | Engine design |
| 15 | **Engine Displacement Volume** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot n_{cyl}$ | m³ (or cc/L) | Engine size |
| 16 | **Wheel Slip / Travel Reduction** | $\eta_{slip} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | Traction efficiency |
| 17 | **Soil Compaction Pressure** | $p = \dfrac{W_{tractor}}{A_{contact}}$ | Pa (or kPa) | Tyre design |
| 18 | **Hitch Pin Diameter (Category)** | I: **22 mm**, II: **28 mm**, III: **36 mm** | mm | Implement matching |
| 19 | **Tyre Designation Example** | 13.6-28 = 13.6" wide, 28" rim | inches | Tyre specification |
| 20 | **Specific Fuel Consumption** | $SFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{engine} \cdot t}$ | kg/kWh | Engine efficiency |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tractor Forward Speed from Drivetrain Parameters

### Problem Statement
> Derive the expression for the forward speed of a tractor given the engine rpm, overall gear ratio, and effective rolling radius of the driving wheels.

### Given
- $N_e$ = Engine speed in rpm
- $i_{gb}$ = Gearbox ratio
- $i_{fd}$ = Final drive ratio
- $i_{diff}$ = Differential ratio (crown wheel teeth ÷ pinion teeth)
- $r$ = Effective rolling radius of the drive wheel in metres
- $i_{total} = i_{gb} \cdot i_{diff} \cdot i_{fd}$ (clutch ratio = 1:1 when engaged)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Speed of the engine shaft (input to gearbox)**
The engine rotates at $N_e$ rpm. The clutch, when fully engaged, transmits this speed directly to the gearbox input shaft (since the clutch is a 1:1 friction coupling in engaged state).

Therefore, speed at gearbox input = $N_e$ rpm.

**Step 2: Speed of the gearbox output shaft (main shaft)**

When a particular gear is engaged, the main shaft rotates at a reduced speed given by the gearbox ratio.

$$
N_{main} = \frac{N_e}{i_{gb}} \quad \text{(rpm)}
$$

**Step 3: Speed of the propeller (pinion) shaft**

The propeller shaft is directly connected to the main shaft, so:
$$
N_{prop} = N_{main} = \frac{N_e}{i_{gb}}
$$

**Step 4: Speed of the crown wheel**

The crown wheel meshes with the pinion. The pinion is on the propeller shaft and drives the crown wheel. Crown wheel rpm:
$$
N_{crown} = \frac{N_{prop}}{i_{diff}} = \frac{N_e}{i_{gb} \cdot i_{diff}}
$$

where $i_{diff} = \dfrac{Z_{crown}}{Z_{pinion}}$.

**Step 5: Speed of the half-shafts (and hence the drive wheels)**
In the differential, when moving in a straight line, both side gears rotate at the same speed as the carrier (crown wheel). So:
$$
N_{wheel} = N_{crown} = \frac{N_e}{i_{gb} \cdot i_{diff}}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the final drive (if present)**

If a final drive (e.g., bull gear) provides further reduction:
$$
N_{wheel} = \frac{N_e}{i_{gb} \cdot i_{diff} \cdot i_{fd}} = \frac{N_e}{i_{total}}
$$

**Step 7: Linear speed of the wheel's centre (theoretical tractor speed)**

The distance travelled by the wheel in one revolution = $2 \pi r$. With $N_{wheel}$ revolutions per minute, the linear speed is:
$$
V_{theoretical} = \frac{2 \pi r \cdot N_{wheel}}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

**Step 8: Substitute $N_{wheel}$:**

$$
V_{theoretical} = \frac{2 \pi r \cdot N_e}{60 \cdot i_{total}}
$$

**Step 9: Convert to km/h (commonly used in tractor specifications):**

$$
V = \frac{2 \pi r \cdot N_e}{60 \cdot i_{total}} \times \frac{3600}{1000} = \frac{0.377 \cdot r \cdot N_e}{i_{total}} \quad \text{(km/h)}
$$

This is the **canonical tractor forward speed equation** in KTU textbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic in Step 9:** Multiply m/s by 3600 to get m/h, then divide by 1000 to convert m → km, giving the factor $3600/1000 = 3.6$ and $2\pi/60 \times 3.6 = 0.377$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ackermann Steering Geometry

### Problem Statement
> Derive the Ackermann condition for the steering of a four-wheel vehicle.

### Setup
Consider a tractor with:
- Wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles)
- Track width $t$ (distance between the two front wheels)
- Inner wheel steer angle $\delta_i$
- Outer wheel steer angle $\delta_o$
- Turning radius at the centre of the tractor $R$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Geometry of turning**

When the tractor turns, all wheels must rotate about a common instantaneous centre $O$. The inner wheel describes a circle of radius $R - t/2$ and the outer wheel a circle of radius $R + t/2$, where $R$ is the radius to the midpoint of the rear axle.

**Step 2: Express the steer angles from the geometry**

The inner wheel makes a larger angle than the outer wheel. From the right triangle formed at the front axle:

For the inner wheel:
$$
\tan \delta_i = \frac{L}{R - t/2}
$$

For the outer wheel:
$$
\tan \delta_o = \frac{L}{R + t/2}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the cotangent relationship**

Take the reciprocal of each equation:
$$
\cot \delta_i = \frac{R - t/2}{L}
$$
$$
\cot \delta_o = \frac{R + t/2}{L}
$$

**Step 4: Subtract the two equations**

$$
\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{R + t/2}{L} - \frac{R - t/2}{L} = \frac{t}{L}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{t}{L}}
$$

This is the **Ackermann condition** — the geometric constraint that any tractor steering linkage must approximately satisfy to ensure pure rolling of all wheels during a turn.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Lift Force on Three-Point Hitch

### Problem Statement
> A tractor's hydraulic system delivers oil at pressure $P$ to a single-acting cylinder of piston diameter $D$. Determine the maximum lifting force the system can apply at the hitch, accounting for the lever mechanical advantage of the linkage.

### Given
- Hydraulic pressure = $P$ (Pa)
- Piston diameter = $D$ (m)
- Linkage geometry: distance from rocker shaft to lift arm pivot = $a$; distance from rocker shaft to link rod connection = $b$ (mechanical advantage = $a/b$)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Piston area**

$$
A_{piston} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2: Force generated by the hydraulic cylinder**

By Pascal's law, the force on the piston equals pressure × area:
$$
F_{cylinder} = P \cdot A_{piston} = \frac{\pi P D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the lever mechanical advantage**

The rocker shaft acts as a first-class lever. The force is multiplied by the ratio $a/b$ (assuming $a > b$ for mechanical advantage):

$$
F_{lift} = F_{cylinder} \times \frac{a}{b} = \frac{\pi P D^2}{4} \times \frac{a}{b}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{F_{lift} = \frac{\pi P D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{a}{b} \quad \text{(Newtons)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is used to determine the **lift capacity** of a tractor at the hitch points — a critical specification when matching implements (e.g., ploughs, cultivators) to the tractor.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Differential Speed Relationship

### Problem Statement
> Show that for a differential gear, the sum of the left and right wheel speeds equals twice the carrier (crown wheel) speed.

### Setup
- $N_L$ = rpm of left wheel
- $N_R$ = rpm of right wheel
- $N_C$ = rpm of carrier (= crown wheel)
- $Z_S$ = teeth on each side gear
- $Z_P$ = teeth on each pinion (planet gear)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Geometric relationship of the gears**

The two side gears and two pinions form a **bevel gear differential**. When the carrier rotates, it carries the pinions around. The pinions mesh with both side gears simultaneously.

**Step 2: When the tractor moves in a straight line**

The pinions do **not** rotate on their own axis (no relative motion between left and right). The carrier simply drags the side gears with it:
$$
N_L = N_R = N_C
$$

Therefore: $N_L + N_R = 2 N_C$ ✓

**Step 3: When the tractor turns**

Suppose the tractor turns left, so the left wheel slows down and the right wheel speeds up. Let the carrier rotate at $N_C$ and let the pinions rotate on their own axis at $N_P$.

The relative speed of the left side gear (with respect to the carrier) = $-N_P \cdot \dfrac{Z_P}{Z_S}$ (opposite direction).

The relative speed of the right side gear (with respect to the carrier) = $+N_P \cdot \dfrac{Z_P}{Z_S}$.

So:
$$
N_L = N_C - N_P \cdot \frac{Z_P}{Z_S}
$$
$$
N_R = N_C + N_P \cdot \frac{Z_P}{Z_S}
$$

**Step 4: Add the two equations**

$$
N_L + N_R = 2 N_C
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{N_L + N_R = 2 N_C}
$$

This proves the **universal differential speed equation** — valid for both straight-line and turning motion. It is one of the most-tested formulas in KTU examinations.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Question:** A tractor has an engine running at 2000 rpm. The gearbox is in 3rd gear with a ratio of 2.5:1. The differential has 41 teeth on the crown wheel and 11 teeth on the pinion. The final drive ratio is 4.5:1. The effective rolling radius of the drive wheels is 0.75 m. Calculate the theoretical forward speed of the tractor in km/h.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the overall ratio.
$$
i_{total} = i_{gb} \times i_{diff} \times i_{fd}
$$

**Step 2:** Differential ratio
$$
i_{diff} = \frac{Z_{crown}}{Z_{pinion}} = \frac{41}{11} = 3.727
$$

**Step 3:** Overall ratio
$$
i_{total} = 2.5 \times 3.727 \times 4.5 = 41.93
$$

**Step 4:** Wheel speed
$$
N_{wheel} = \frac{N_e}{i_{total}} = \frac{2000}{41.93} = 47.7 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5:** Theoretical forward speed in m/s
$$
V = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.75 \times 47.7}{60} = \frac{224.6}{60} = 3.743 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to km/h
$$
V = 3.743 \times 3.6 = 13.48 \text{ km/h}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{V_{theoretical} \approx 13.48 \text{ km/h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real tractor speeds** are slightly lower due to wheel slip (typically 5–15% under field conditions). The actual speed = $V_{theoretical} \times (1 - \text{slip ratio})$.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Constructional Features of Tractor vs. Car

| Feature | Tractor | Car / Truck |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Engine Type | Diesel, low rpm, high torque | Petrol/Diesel, high rpm, lower torque backup |
| Clutch | Single/Dual plate dry, heavy duty | Single plate dry, lighter |
| Gearbox | Constant mesh with sliding dogs; many speeds | Synchromesh; fewer speeds |
| Differential | Standard, often with differential lock | Standard, usually no lock |
| Final Drive | Bull gear or epicyclic, high reduction | Hypoid/bevel, low reduction |
| Brakes | Mechanical drum (rear), disc optional (front) | Hydraulic disc/drum, power-assisted |
| Steering | Power steering standard | Power steering common |
| PTO | Yes, 540/1000 rpm | No |
| Three-Point Hitch | Yes | No |
| Tyres | Agricultural lugs, low pressure | Highway treads, high pressure |
| Operator Station | Open or cabin, ROPS, dust-protected | Closed cabin, AC, comfort-focused |
