# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

> **List and briefly explain the constructional features of a typical farm tractor engine. Why is a diesel engine preferred over a petrol engine for tractors?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Mark Distribution Indicated)

**Constructional Features of a Farm Tractor Engine (2 Marks):**
1. **Vertical, in-line, multi-cylinder configuration** — low centre of gravity, easy alignment with drivetrain.
2. **Four-stroke, compression-ignition (diesel) cycle** with **high compression ratio (16:1 to 22:1)**.
3. **Water-cooled with forced circulation** — radiator + water pump + thermostat.
4. **Long-stroke design** — provides high torque at low rpm (lugging ability essential for ploughing).
5. **Heavy-duty bearings** — tapered roller main and big-end bearings to withstand shock loads.
6. **Cyclone pre-cleaner + dry/oil-bath air filter** — handles dusty field environments.
7. **Mechanical/hydraulic governor** — maintains nearly constant speed under varying load.
8. **Heavy flywheel** — stores inertia, smooths out torque fluctuations during intermittent loads.

**Why Diesel is Preferred (1 Mark):**
- Higher thermal efficiency (35–40% vs 25–30% for petrol).
- Higher torque backup (200–300%) for heavy draft operations.
- Lower specific fuel consumption → cheaper to run for long field hours.
- Diesel fuel is safer (less flammable) and more available in rural areas.
- Longer engine life due to slower rpm operation and robust construction.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

> **What is the function of a differential in a tractor? Draw a labelled block diagram of a differential unit and state the Ackermann steering condition.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Function (1 Mark):**
The differential allows the **two driving (rear) wheels of the tractor to rotate at different speeds** when the tractor turns a corner, while still transmitting equal torque to both wheels. Without it, the inner wheel would skid and the tractor would be very difficult to turn.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**
A labelled diagram should include: Propeller shaft → Bevel Pinion → Crown Wheel → Differential Case (carrying cross/spider and planet pinions) → Two side gears on half-shafts → Two driving wheels. The differential lock mechanism should be shown optionally.

**Ackermann Steering Condition (1 Mark):**
$$
\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{t}{L}
$$
where $\delta_i$ = inner wheel steer angle, $\delta_o$ = outer wheel steer angle, $t$ = track width, $L$ = wheelbase.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional details of a single-plate dry friction clutch** used in tractors. List the materials used for the friction lining and state the typical clamping force range. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **theoretical forward speed of a tractor** in terms of engine rpm, gear ratios, differential ratio, final drive ratio, and wheel rolling radius. A tractor has an engine running at **2200 rpm**, gearbox ratio **2.8:1**, differential ratio **3.6:1**, final drive ratio **4.2:1**, and wheel rolling radius **0.78 m**. Calculate the theoretical forward speed in **km/h**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Answer — Clutch Constructional Details (7 Marks)

**1. Function of Clutch (1 Mark):**
The clutch is a friction coupling device that connects/disconnects the engine from the gearbox. It allows smooth starting, gear changing, and idling without stalling the engine.

**2. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
The student must draw a clear labelled diagram showing:
- Flywheel (driving member, bolted to crankshaft)
- Clutch plate (driven member, with friction lining on both sides, splined hub)
- Pressure plate (applies clamping force)
- Clutch springs (9–12 coil springs)
- Release bearing (mounted on the clutch fork)
- Clutch fork and pedal
- Clutch housing (bell housing)

**3. Material of Friction Lining (1 Mark):**
- **Asbestos-based** (older designs) — now phased out due to health concerns.
- **Semi-metallic** (iron, copper, glass fibres in resin binder) — modern.
- **Ceramic** — high-performance applications.
- **Paper-based** — for wet clutches.

**4. Constructional Details (2 Marks):**
- **Single-plate** means one driven plate between the flywheel and pressure plate.
- **Dry** means the clutch operates without oil (unlike wet multi-plate clutches).
- **Friction** means torque is transmitted by friction between the lining and the flywheel face.
- The pressure plate is mounted on the flywheel cover using **retraction bolts** so that when released, it moves back uniformly.
- The release bearing is a **ball or roller thrust bearing** that pushes against the pressure plate fingers.

**5. Clamping Force (1 Mark):**
Typical clamping force for a tractor clutch: **3000 N to 8000 N** depending on tractor size. For 540 PTO tractors, around **4000–5000 N** is common.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Forward Speed Derivation and Calculation (7 Marks)

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
Following the steps in Section 3.1, the student must derive:

$$
V = \frac{0.377 \cdot r \cdot N_e}{i_{total}} \quad \text{(km/h)}
$$

The student should explicitly show:
- Step 1: $N_{wheel} = N_e / i_{total}$
- Step 2: Linear distance per minute = $2\pi r \cdot N_{wheel}$
- Step 3: Speed in m/s = $\dfrac{2\pi r \cdot N_{wheel}}{60}$
- Step 4: Speed in km/h = $\dfrac{2\pi r \cdot N_{wheel}}{60} \times 3.6 = \dfrac{0.377 \cdot r \cdot N_e}{i_{total}}$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given:
- $N_e = 2200$ rpm
- $i_{gb} = 2.8$
- $i_{diff} = 3.6$
- $i_{fd} = 4.2$
- $r = 0.78$ m

**Step 1:** Overall gear ratio:
$$
i_{total} = 2.8 \times 3.6 \times 4.2 = 42.336
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the formula:
$$
V = \frac{0.377 \times 0.78 \times 2200}{42.336} = \frac{646.97}{42.336} = 15.28 \text{ km/h}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{V_{theoretical} \approx 15.28 \text{ km/h}}
$$

---

### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** Describe the **constructional details of a three-point hitch system** in a modern farm tractor. Explain **position control, draft control, and mixed control** modes of operation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the **constructional details of a hydraulic system** used in tractors. State the **formula for the lift force** developed at the hitch and calculate the lift force if the pump pressure is **175 bar**, the piston diameter is **75 mm**, and the linkage mechanical advantage is **2.2**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Answer — Three-Point Hitch (7 Marks)

**1. Constructional Components (3 Marks):**
- **Two Lower Links (Draft Links)** — connect the tractor to the lower hitch points of the implement; can swing laterally.
- **One Top Link (Centre Link)** — connects the top of the implement; length is adjustable via a turnbuckle.
- **Lift Arms** — driven by the rocker shaft; raise/lower the lower links.
- **Rocker Shaft (Lift Shaft)** — transverse shaft operated by the hydraulic cylinder piston rod.
- **Implement Pins** — Category I (22 mm), II (28 mm), or III (36 mm).
- **Stabilizers / Sway Chains** — control lateral movement of the lower links.
- **Quick Coupler** (optional) — for fast implement attachment.

**2. Position Control (1.5 Marks):**
- The **position of the implement relative to the tractor** is controlled and held constant regardless of draft force.
- The driver sets the desired height/depth using a position control lever.
- Best suited for **light implements** like sprayers, mowers, and cultivators in transport.
- Does NOT automatically adjust for varying soil resistance.

**3. Draft Control (1.5 Marks):**
- The **draft (horizontal pull force on the implement)** is sensed via **top link strain / lower link force**.
- When the implement hits hard soil, draft increases → control valve sends oil to lift the implement slightly → draft decreases → implement is lowered again.
- Maintains **constant draft force** → uniform ploughing depth and reduced operator fatigue.
- Best suited for **heavy tillage implements** like mouldboard ploughs and disc ploughs.

**4. Mixed Control (1 Mark):**
- A combination of both position and draft control.
- The implement responds to both draft variations and pre-set position limits.
- Provides the best compromise for varied field conditions.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Hydraulic System (7 Marks)

**1. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
The student must draw a clear diagram showing:
- Oil reservoir (in rear axle housing) → Suction strainer → Gear pump → Relief valve → Control valve (spool type) → Lift cylinder → Rocker shaft → Lift arms → Lower links.
- Return path: Lift cylinder → Return filter → Reservoir.

**2. Components and Their Functions (2 Marks):**
- **Reservoir**: Stores hydraulic oil; integrated with rear axle housing.
- **Gear Pump**: Fixed displacement, engine-driven; provides flow.
- **Relief Valve**: Limits maximum system pressure (typically 175–200 bar).
- **Control Valve**: Spool type, directs oil to cylinder or back to tank.
- **Lift Cylinder**: Single-acting; pushes the rocker arm.
- **Filter**: Removes contaminants from the oil.

**3. Formula for Lift Force (1 Mark):**
$$
F_{lift} = \frac{\pi P D^2}{4} \times \frac{a}{b}
$$
where $P$ = pressure, $D$ = piston diameter, $a/b$ = linkage mechanical advantage.

**4. Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**
Given:
- $P = 175$ bar $= 175 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $D = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- $a/b = 2.2$

**Step 1:** Piston area
$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.005625}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Cylinder force
$$
F_{cyl} = P \times A = 175 \times 10^5 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} = 77,315 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply mechanical advantage
$$
F_{lift} = 77,315 \times 2.2 = 170,093 \text{ N} \approx 170 \text{ kN}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{F_{lift} \approx 170.1 \text{ kN} \approx 17.34 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most students forget to convert bar to Pa** in the lift-force calculation. 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa. Failing to convert = lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up diameter and radius** in the area formula is a common error. Always use $\pi D^2/4$ (diameter squared), NOT $\pi r^2$ where $r = D/2$ would give the same result — pick one and be consistent.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by the mechanical advantage** ($a/b$ ratio) — students often report only the cylinder force, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **In the clutch question**, students often list the parts but fail to mention the **retraction bolts** or the **release bearing mechanism** — both are important.
> 5. **In the forward-speed derivation**, skipping the conversion from m/s to km/h is a major deduction point.
> 6. **For the differential**, students often forget to state the **universal equation** $N_L + N_R = 2N_C$ — this is the most-tested formula.
> 7. **For draft vs. position control**, students frequently describe both as the same — they are fundamentally different (one is force-based, the other position-based). Marks are lost if this distinction is not clear.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Constructional Details**

### ✅ Engine Construction
- [ ] Vertical, in-line, multi-cylinder, water-cooled **diesel engine**.
- [ ] Compression ratio: **16:1 to 22:1**.
- [ ] Operating speed: **1500–2400 rpm** (low for high torque).
- [ ] Long-stroke design → high lugging ability.
- [ ] Components: Block, head, piston, con-rod, crankshaft, flywheel, camshaft, valves, governor.
- [ ] Air filter: **Cyclone pre-cleaner + dry/oil-bath filter**.
- [ ] Flywheel is **heavy** to absorb torque fluctuations.

### ✅ Clutch
- [ ] **Single-plate, dry friction type** is the most common in tractors.
- [ ] **9 to 12 coil springs** provide clamping force.
- [ ] **Release bearing** is a thrust bearing; pushed by clutch fork.
- [ ] Friction lining materials: **asbestos-free semi-metallic / ceramic / paper-based**.

### ✅ Gearbox
- [ ] **Constant mesh** with sliding dog engagement (traditional).
- [ ] Modern: **synchromesh** or **partial synchromesh**.
- [ ] Typical: **8 forward + 2 reverse** up to **16 forward + 4 reverse**.
- [ ] Gears: **case-hardened alloy steel** (e.g., 20MnCr5, EN 353).

### ✅ Differential
- [ ] Bevel gear type with **crown wheel, pinion, cross, side gears, planet pinions**.
- [ ] **Universal equation**: $N_L + N_R = 2N_C$.
- [ ] **Differential lock** uses a dog clutch to force both wheels to rotate together.
- [ ] Material: **Case-hardened alloy steel** gears.

### ✅ Final Drive
- [ ] **Bull gear** (external spur, simple) or **Epicyclic** (planetary, compact).
- [ ] Final reduction ratio typically **3:1 to 6:1**.

### ✅ Steering
- [ ] **Ackermann condition**: $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{t}{L}$.
- [ ] **Power steering** is standard in modern tractors.
- [ ] Steering geometry: drop arm → track rod → steering arms on knuckle spindles.

### ✅ Brakes
- [ ] **Internal expanding shoe type** on rear wheels (mechanical).
- [ ] **Disc brakes** increasingly common on front wheels.
- [ ] **Equalizer** ensures balanced braking.

### ✅ PTO
- [ ] **Standard**: **540 rpm @ engine rated rpm, 6 spline, 35 mm dia**.
- [ ] **High-power**: 1000 rpm, 21 spline, 35 mm dia.
- [ ] Rotation: **clockwise from the rear** of the tractor.

### ✅ Three-Point Hitch
- [ ] **Two lower links + one top link**.
- [ ] **Categories**: I (22 mm), II (28 mm), III (36 mm).
- [ ] **Position control** vs **draft control** vs **mixed control**.

### ✅ Hydraulic System
- [ ] **Gear pump**, **spool-type control valve**, **single-acting lift cylinder**.
- [ ] Pressure: **175–200 bar** typical.
- [ ] Lift force formula: $F_{lift} = \dfrac{\pi P D^2}{4} \times \dfrac{a}{b}$.

### ✅ Tyres
- [ ] Front: smaller, **ribbed** for steering.
- [ ] Rear: larger, **lugged (R-1)** for traction.
- [ ] Low pressure (**80–180 kPa**) for better grip and reduced soil compaction.

### ✅ Forward Speed Formula (THE MOST IMPORTANT)
- [ ] $V = \dfrac{0.377 \cdot r \cdot N_e}{i_{total}}$ **(km/h)**
- [ ] $i_{total} = i_{gb} \times i_{diff} \times i_{fd}$

### ✅ Differential Speed Formula (ALSO VERY IMPORTANT)
- [ ] $N_L + N_R = 2 N_C$ — **Always write this in your answer sheet.**

### ✅ Standard Specifications to Memorize
- [ ] PTO: **540 rpm, 6 spline, 35 mm dia** (most common)
- [ ] Hitch pin: **22 / 28 / 36 mm** (Category I/II/III)
- [ ] Differential ratio: $Z_{crown}/Z_{pinion} \approx 3$ to $5$ (typical)
- [ ] Engine rpm: **1500–2400 rpm**
- [ ] Compression ratio: **16:1 to 22:1**
- [ ] Hydraulic pressure: **175–200 bar**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Valuator's Perspective:** Draw clear, **labelled** block diagrams. Always state the **units**. Memorize the **forward speed formula** and the **differential speed equation** — together they account for nearly **30% of the marks** in any Module 2 question. Use **standard ISO terminology** (e.g., "draft link", "rocker shaft", "final drive") rather than colloquial terms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working and Introduction to Threshing Systems: Manual and Mechanical Systems, Types of Threshers - Tangential and Axial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working and Introduction to Threshing Systems: Manual and Mechanical Systems, Types of Threshers - Tangential and Axial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Threshing

**Threshing** is the post-harvest operation of mechanically separating the **grain (seeds)** from the **harvested crop ears, heads, or pods** by impact, rubbing, squeezing, or a combination of these actions. The separated grain is then cleaned and stored.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> Threshing is the process of detaching grains from the panicles, ears, or pods of the harvested crop by applying impact, rubbing, or combing forces, while causing minimum mechanical damage to the grain and the straw.

## 1.2 Classification of Threshing Systems

Threshing systems are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Description | Power Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Threshing Systems** | Traditional, human/animal powered | Human labor, animal power |
| **Mechanical Threshing Systems** | Engine/PTO-driven machines | Tractor PTO, electric motor, engine |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a **bunch of sunflower seeds stuck tightly to a dried flower head**. To get the seeds out, you can:
> 1. **Beat the head with a stick** (impact threshing) – this is what manual flailing does.
> 2. **Roll a heavy log over the heads** (rubbing/compression) – this is animal treading.
> 3. **Use a spinning drum with teeth that grabs and pulls the grain off** (mechanical threshing with rasp bars).
>
> In each case, the goal is the same: **detach grain from stalk with minimum breakage of the grain itself.**

## 1.4 Threshing Damage – A Key Performance Metric

> [!NOTE]
> The effectiveness of a thresher is judged by three parameters:
> - **Threshing Efficiency (ηₜ):** Percentage of grain actually separated from the crop.
> - **Grain Damage (%):** Percentage of broken/cracked grains (must be < **2–3%** for commercial grade).
> - **Scatter Loss (%):** Grain lost as un-threshed or scattered material.

The **target threshing efficiency** for a well-designed mechanical thresher is **≥ 99%** with **grain damage below 2%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manual Threshing Systems – Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Methods of Manual Threshing

**A. Beating / Flailing with Sticks**
- Workers hold bundles of harvested crop and beat them against a hard surface or against each other.
- Crop is held over a **threshing floor** (clean, hard-packed earth, often smeared with cow dung).
- Grain falls on the floor; straw is removed and used as fodder or fuel.
- **Capacity:** ~50–80 kg grain/day per worker.
- **Damage:** Moderate, high labor cost.

**B. Treading by Animals (Cattle Trampling)**
- Sheaves of harvested crop are spread on the threshing floor.
- A team of **bullocks, oxen, or buffaloes** is driven in circles over the crop.
- Animal hooves provide the rubbing/impact force.
- **Capacity:** ~250–400 kg grain/hour (with 6–8 animals).
- **Damage:** Low, but unhygienic due to dung/urine contamination.

**C. Rubbing by Hand**
- Crop bundles are rubbed against a **rough stone, wooden plank, or bamboo mat**.
- Used for **small quantities** of delicate crops (e.g., pulses, vegetables).
- Very low capacity, but minimal grain damage.

**D. Log Rolling / Stone Rolling**
- A heavy log or stone is pulled over spread-out sheaves by animals.
- Continuous compression detaches grains.
- Ancient method, still used in remote parts of India.

### 2.1.2 Disadvantages of Manual Threshing

- **Very low output** (1–2 quintals per day per person).
- **High labor requirement** and drudgery.
- **Weather-dependent** (grain must be dry).
- **Contamination** from dust, animal droppings, stones.
- **High post-harvest losses** (5–10% typical).
- Not suitable for **large-scale, time-bound harvest operations**.

## 2.2 Mechanical Threshing Systems – Detailed Analysis

A **mechanical thresher** uses a powered **rotating cylinder** with threshing elements (rasp bars, spikes, pegs, or wire loops) that work against a stationary **concave** (a curved grating/screen below the cylinder).

### 2.2.1 Main Components of a Power Thresher

1. **Feeding tray / Hopper:** Crop is fed uniformly.
2. **Threshing cylinder:** Rotating drum with threshing elements.
3. **Concave:** Stationary curved screen below the cylinder; gap adjustable.
4. **Blower / Fan:** Generates airflow for cleaning.
5. **Sieves (Top & Bottom):** Reciprocating screens for grain-cleaning.
6. **Grain outlet & straw outlet.**
7. **Power transmission system** (belts, pulleys, gear box).
8. **Frame and main body.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for this topic.

| Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Threshing Efficiency (ηₜ) | $\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100$ | $W_g$ = threshed grain (kg); $W_u$ = unthreshed grain (kg) |
| Grain Damage (%) | $D = \dfrac{W_b}{W_t} \times 100$ | $W_b$ = broken grain (kg); $W_t$ = total grain (kg) |
| Scatter Loss (%) | $L_s = \dfrac{W_s}{W_t} \times 100$ | $W_s$ = scattered grain (kg) |
| Cleaning Efficiency (ηc) | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_{gc}}{W_{gc} + W_{uc}} \times 100$ | $W_{gc}$ = clean grain; $W_{uc}$ = unclean grain |
| Cylinder Peripheral Speed (v) | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | $D$ = cylinder diameter (m); $N$ = rpm |
| Throughput Capacity (Q) | $Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | $\rho$ = feed density; $v$ = peripheral speed; $A$ = threshing area |
| Concave Clearance | $c$ (mm) – adjustable | Gap between cylinder and concave |
| Specific Power Requirement | $P_s = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | kW per tonne/hour |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Tangential threshers** dominate **cereal crops** (wheat, paddy, sorghum, maize) in Indian agriculture due to low cost and simplicity.
- **Axial flow threshers** are preferred for **multi-crop** operations and **higher capacity commercial threshing**, especially in custom-hire service centers.
- The choice between tangential and axial affects **grain quality, threshing loss, and operating cost** – a key decision parameter in agricultural mechanization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cylinder Peripheral Speed

The peripheral (linear) speed of the threshing cylinder at the rasp bar tip is a critical design parameter. It directly controls the **impact velocity** on the crop.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The cylinder makes $N$ revolutions in one minute. Therefore, the angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s is:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

**Step 2:** The peripheral speed $v$ of a point on the cylinder surface (at radius $r = D/2$) is given by the kinematic relation $v = \omega \cdot r$:

$$
v = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying the expression:

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

**Step 4:** This peripheral speed must be maintained within an **optimum range** for different crops:

| Crop | Peripheral Speed (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Paddy | **14 – 18** |
| Wheat | **15 – 22** |
| Maize | **10 – 15** |
| Sorghum | **12 – 18** |
| Soybean | **8 – 12** |

> [!NOTE]
> If $v$ is too low → incomplete threshing (un-threshed grain loss rises).  
> If $v$ is too high → grain breakage and straw pulverization rise.

## 3.2 Derivation: Threshing Efficiency

**Step 1:** Define the grain collected at the main grain outlet as $W_g$ (kg).  
**Step 2:** Define the grain remaining in the straw and chaff that escaped un-threshed as $W_u$ (kg).  
**Step 3:** Total grain input to the thresher is:

$$
W_{total} = W_g + W_u
$$

**Step 4:** Threshing efficiency is the ratio of grain actually separated to total grain:

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Substituting:**

$$
\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Tangential vs Axial Flow Thresher

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield KTU Comparison Table – memorize this for 14-mark questions.**

| Parameter | Tangential Flow Thresher | Axial Flow Thresher |
|---|---|---|
| **Crop Movement** | Tangential (perpendicular to cylinder axis) | Axial (parallel to cylinder axis) |
| **Number of Passes through Threshing Zone** | One (single pass) or Two (re-threshing) | Multiple (crop spirals 2–3 times around cylinder) |
| **Threshing Cylinder Type** | Rasp bar, spike tooth, peg tooth, wire loop | Rasp bars arranged helically on rotor |
| **Cylinder Diameter** | 400 – 600 mm | 400 – 700 mm |
| **Cylinder Length** | 600 – 1200 mm | 1200 – 2400 mm |
| **Cylinder Speed** | 800 – 1600 rpm | 500 – 1000 rpm |
| **Peripheral Speed** | 15 – 30 m/s | 10 – 25 m/s |
| **Threshing Efficiency** | 95 – 98 % | **99 – 99.5 %** |
| **Grain Damage** | 2 – 5 % | **< 1 – 2 %** |
| **Straw Breakage** | More (chopped) | Less (long straw preserved) |
| **Capacity** | 500 – 1500 kg/h | 2000 – 5000 kg/h |
| **Power Required** | 5 – 15 kW | 15 – 35 kW |
| **Weight & Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Suitability** | Single crop, small/marginal farms | Multi-crop, commercial / custom-hire |
| **Complexity** | Simple design, easy maintenance | Complex helical arrangement |
| **Examples in India** | Common in Punjab, Haryana for wheat/paddy | Used by custom-hiring centers, FPOs |

## 3.4 Working Principle – Tangential Flow Thresher (Detailed Step Logic)

1. **Crop bundle feeding** → Sheaves are fed from the feed tray into the gap between the rotating cylinder and the concave.
2. **Initial impact** → Rasp bars strike the crop ears, applying a sudden impact force that loosens grains from the stalks.
3. **Rubbing action** → As crop is dragged along the concave, the rubbing between cylinder and concave surface shears off remaining grains.
4. **Tangential discharge** → Straw, now free of most grains, is **tangentially ejected** out of the threshing zone through the straw outlet.
5. **Grain passage** → Most threshed grain (75–85%) falls through the concave openings onto the cleaning sieves.
6. **Re-threshing (optional)** → In **double-cylinder** designs, straw passes through a second threshing zone for residual grain recovery.
7. **Cleaning** → The grain mixture on the sieves is cleaned by **air blast from the blower**, separating light chaff from heavier grain.
8. **Bagging** → Clean grain is discharged into a bag; chaff and dust exit via the blower.

## 3.5 Working Principle – Axial Flow Thresher (Detailed Step Logic)

1. **Crop feeding** → Crop is fed axially into one end of the cylinder through the feed opening.
2. **Helical motion** → Helically arranged rasp bars on the rotating cylinder **propel the crop along the cylinder axis** while simultaneously **rotating it around the cylinder**.
3. **Multi-pass threshing** → The crop makes **2–3 complete spirals** around the cylinder, exposing every ear/grain head to multiple impact-rubbing cycles.
4. **Gentler action** → Lower peripheral speed but more prolonged exposure → **higher threshing efficiency with less grain damage**.
5. **Straw ejection** → At the end of the cylinder, long straw is axially discharged, often with **blower assistance**.
6. **Grain separation** → Grain falls through concave perforations and is conveyed to the cleaning unit.
7. **Cleaning & bagging** → Standard cleaning, grading, and bagging operations follow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Threshing Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Threshing Systems] --> B[Manual Threshing]
    A --> C[Mechanical Threshing]
    B --> B1[Beating with Sticks]
    B --> B2[Animal Treading]
    B --> B3[Hand Rubbing]
    B --> B4[Log Rolling]
    C --> C1[Tangential Flow Thresher]
    C --> C2[Axial Flow Thresher]
    C1 --> C1A[Single Cylinder]
    C1 --> C1B[Double Cylinder]
    C2 --> C2A[Single Axial Rotor]
    C2 --> C2B[Twin Axial Rotor]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Power Thresher

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Power Source - Tractor PTO or Electric Motor] --> PT[Power Transmission - Belt Pulley and Gearbox]
    PT --> CY[Threshing Cylinder - Rotating Rotor]
    PT --> BL[Blower Fan]
    PT --> SV[Reciprocating Sieves]
    FT[Feeding Tray] --> CY
    CY --> CO[Concave - Stationary Screen]
    CO --> GS[Grain Stream to Sieves]
    CY --> SO[Straw Outlet]
    GS --> SV
    BL --> SV
    SV --> GO[Clean Grain Outlet]
    SV --> CO2[Chaff Outlet]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Crop Flow in Tangential vs Axial Thresher

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TANGENTIAL
    T1[Feed In] --> T2[Impact Zone Cylinder + Concave]
    T2 --> T3[Straw Out Tangential]
    T2 --> T4[Grain through Concave]
    end

    subgraph AXIAL
    A1[Feed In Axial End] --> A2[Helical Spiral Path]
    A2 --> A3[Straw Out Axial End]
    A2 --> A4[Grain through Concave]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Threshing Cylinder Element Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Rasp Bar Cylinder] --> R1[Most Common - Wheat Paddy]
    S[Spike Tooth Cylinder] --> S1[For Paddy and Soybean]
    P[Peg Tooth Cylinder] --> P1[For Maize]
    W[Wire Loop Cylinder] --> W1[For Sunflower and Pulses]
    H[Helical Rasp Bar] --> H1[Axial Flow Thresher Only]
```

## 4.5 Schematic: Layout of a Multi-Crop Thresher

```mermaid
graph TD
    FEED[Feeding Tray] --> CYL[Threshing Cylinder with Concave]
    CYL --> GRAIN[Grain Auger to Sieves]
    CYL --> STRAW[Straw Blower or Thrower]
    GRAIN --> TOP[Top Sieve - Oscillating]
    GRAIN --> BOT[Bottom Sieve - Oscillating]
    TOP --> FAN[Cleaning Blower]
    BOT --> FAN
    FAN --> BAG[Clean Grain Outlet - Bagging]
    FAN --> CHAFF[Chaff and Dust Outlet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of a thresher", the cleanest answer is: **Feed Tray → Cylinder + Concave → Blower + Sieves → Clean Grain Outlet / Straw Outlet / Chaff Outlet** with the power source on the side.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Threshing. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
Threshing is the post-harvest operation of separating grains from the harvested crop ears, heads, or pods by applying impact, rubbing, or combing forces through a threshing cylinder and concave arrangement, while ensuring minimum grain damage. It is performed immediately after harvesting and before winnowing/cleaning.

---

### Question 2: List any four types of manual threshing methods. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The four types of manual threshing methods are:
1. **Beating/Flailing** – striking the crop bundles with sticks.
2. **Animal Treading** – driving cattle/oxen over spread-out sheaves.
3. **Hand Rubbing** – rubbing the ears against a rough surface.
4. **Log Rolling** – dragging a heavy log or stone over the crop.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - When explaining **working of threshers**, students often forget to mention the **role of the concave** and **blower** – this costs 2–3 marks.
> - Students confuse **tangential** and **axial** flow directions. Always specify the **axis of rotation vs crop flow direction**.
> - Drawing the **block diagram** is mandatory for full marks. Without it, expect 2–4 mark deduction.
> - Always state **threshing efficiency values** with units (%) – examiners award marks for quantified answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Tangential Flow Thresher** with a neat block diagram. List its main components and state its typical threshing efficiency and grain damage values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **manual threshing methods** practiced in India. State their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – (a) Tangential Flow Thresher

**Working Principle:**
In a tangential flow thresher, the crop is fed perpendicular (tangentially) to the axis of the rotating threshing cylinder. The high-speed rotating cylinder fitted with rasp bars strikes the crop ears, dislodging the grains by impact and rubbing against the concave screen below.

**Step-by-Step Working:**
1. The operator feeds the harvested crop sheaves uniformly through the **feeding tray/trough** into the threshing zone.
2. The **threshing cylinder**, rotating at 800–1600 rpm, carries the crop between itself and the **concave**, where impact and rubbing detach the grains.
3. About 75–85% of threshed grain falls through the **concave perforations** onto the cleaning sieves.
4. The remaining straw is **tangentially ejected** out of the threshing zone.
5. A **blower fan** blows air across the sieves, separating lighter chaff from heavier grain.
6. The **reciprocating sieves** grade the grain, and clean grain is discharged via the **grain outlet** for bagging.
7. Chaff and dust exit through the chaff outlet.

**Key Specifications:**
- Threshing efficiency: **95 – 98%**
- Grain damage: **2 – 5%**
- Cylinder speed: **800 – 1600 rpm**
- Peripheral speed: **15 – 30 m/s**

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**
Feeding Tray → Threshing Cylinder + Concave → Sieves + Blower → Clean Grain Outlet / Straw Outlet / Chaff Outlet

#### Model Solution – (b) Manual Threshing Methods

**1. Beating / Flailing Method:**
- Crop is held over a threshing floor and beaten with sticks.
- Capacity: 50–80 kg/person/day.
- Disadvantage: High labor, drudgery.

**2. Animal Treading:**
- Sheaves spread on floor; bullocks/oxen driven in circles.
- Capacity: 250–400 kg/hour with 6–8 animals.
- Disadvantage: Contamination by dung, unhygienic.

**3. Hand Rubbing:**
- Ears rubbed against rough stone, wooden plank or bamboo mat.
- Used for pulses and small quantities.
- Disadvantage: Very low capacity.

**4. Log / Stone Rolling:**
- A heavy log is pulled by animals over the spread sheaves.
- Compression detaches the grain.
- Disadvantage: Slow, animal-dependent.

**Advantages of Manual Threshing:**
- Low capital cost.
- Suitable for small/marginal farmers.
- Minimal grain damage if done carefully.
- No fuel/electricity needed.

**Limitations:**
- Very low throughput.
- High post-harvest losses (5–10%).
- Highly weather-dependent.
- Not viable for large-scale commercial farming.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of an **Axial Flow Thresher** with a suitable block diagram. State its advantages over the tangential flow thresher. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Tangential Flow** and **Axial Flow** threshers on the basis of: crop movement, threshing efficiency, grain damage, capacity, power requirement, and straw quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – (a) Axial Flow Thresher

**Construction:**
The axial flow thresher consists of:
- A **horizontal cylindrical rotor** carrying **rasp bars arranged helically** along its length.
- A **concave screen** (full circular, surrounding the rotor) with perforations.
- A **feeding hopper** at one axial end.
- A **straw outlet** at the opposite axial end.
- A **blower**, **oscillating sieves**, and **grain collection auger**.

**Working:**
1. Crop is fed axially at one end of the cylinder via the feed hopper.
2. The rotating rotor's **helical rasp bars** push the crop along the cylinder axis while simultaneously tumbling it around the cylinder.
3. The crop follows a **spiral helical path** around the rotor, making **2–3 complete passes** through the threshing zone.
4. Each pass subjects the crop to fresh impact and rubbing, ensuring **complete grain detachment**.
5. Threshed grain passes through the concave into the cleaning section.
6. Long, intact straw is discharged axially at the opposite end.
7. Cleaning is performed by blower + sieves, and clean grain is bagged.

**Advantages over Tangential Flow Thresher:**
- Higher threshing efficiency (**99–99.5%**).
- Lower grain damage (**< 2%**).
- Higher capacity (up to 5000 kg/h).
- Multi-crop versatility.
- Better straw quality (long straw, not chopped).

#### Model Solution – (b) Comparison Table

| Parameter | Tangential Flow | Axial Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Crop movement | Tangential to cylinder axis | Axial (parallel) to cylinder axis |
| Threshing efficiency | 95 – 98% | 99 – 99.5% |
| Grain damage | 2 – 5% | < 2% |
| Capacity | 500 – 1500 kg/h | 2000 – 5000 kg/h |
| Power requirement | 5 – 15 kW | 15 – 35 kW |
| Straw quality | Chopped / broken | Long, intact straw |
| Multi-crop suitability | Limited | Excellent |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Threshing** = separating grain from crop ears/heads by **impact + rubbing**.
- **Manual methods** = Beating, Treading, Hand Rubbing, Log Rolling. Low capacity, high loss.
- **Mechanical threshers** use a **rotating cylinder + concave** for threshing.
- **Tangential Flow Thresher:** Crop enters **tangentially**; single pass; efficiency **95–98%**; grain damage **2–5%**; lower cost, simpler design.
- **Axial Flow Thresher:** Crop moves **axially** (parallel to cylinder); makes **2–3 helical passes**; efficiency **99–99.5%**; grain damage **< 2%**; higher capacity, multi-crop.
- **Cylinder peripheral speed formula:** $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ m/s – must be in the **optimum range** for the crop.
- **Threshing efficiency formula:** $\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100$ %.
- **Main components** of any power thresher: Feed tray, Threshing cylinder, Concave, Blower, Oscillating sieves, Grain outlet, Straw outlet, Chaff outlet.
- **Rasp bar cylinder** = most common; for **wheat, paddy**. **Spike tooth** for soybean/paddy. **Peg tooth** for maize. **Wire loop** for sunflower/pulses. **Helical rasp bar** is exclusive to **axial flow threshers**.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in long-answer questions – 2–4 marks depend on it.
- **Mention the concave role** in every working explanation – most-missed item in valuation.
- Use **quantified values** (efficiency, speed, capacity) for full marks on 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Their Constructional Details and Cleaning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Their Constructional Details and Cleaning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Constructional Details of a Tractor Engine – Formal Definition

The **constructional details of a tractor engine** refer to the structural design, geometry, material composition, and assembly arrangement of the prime-mover components that convert the chemical energy of diesel fuel into mechanical rotational energy. For tractor applications, the engine is invariably a **multi-cylinder, vertical, in-line, water-cooled, 4-stroke Compression Ignition (CI) diesel engine**, owing to its high torque reserve, fuel economy, and durability under varying agricultural loads.

The principal constructional sub-assemblies include:

- **Cylinder Block & Cylinder Head (with Wet/Dry Liners)**
- **Piston, Piston Rings & Gudgeon Pin**
- **Connecting Rod & Crankshaft**
- **Valve Train Mechanism (Camshaft, Tappets, Push Rods, Rocker Arms, Valves, Springs)**
- **Flywheel, Manifolds (Intake & Exhaust)**
- **Crankcase, Oil Pan (Sump) and Mountings**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** A tractor engine is **NOT** an automobile engine. It operates at lower RPM (typically **1500–2200 rpm**), with a **high torque backup of 20–30%**, and uses **governor-based speed regulation** rather than throttle-based control.

## 1.2 Cleaning Systems – Formal Definition

A **cleaning system** in a tractor is a series of engineered filtration and separation devices designed to remove solid particulates, liquid contaminants, and abrasive matter from the working fluids (air, fuel, and lubricating oil) before they enter critical functional zones of the engine. The three principal cleaning systems are:

| Cleaning System | Target Contaminant | Consequence of Failure |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Cleaner** | Dust, chaff, pollen, sand | Cylinder liner scoring, piston ring wear, valve seat pitting |
| **Fuel Filter** | Water, scale, rust, paraffin wax | Injector nozzle seizure, pump plunger seizure |
| **Lubricating Oil Filter** | Carbon, metallic wear debris, soot | Bearing failure, camshaft lobe wear |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Lungs, Arteries, and Blood" Analogy

Imagine a tractor engine as a **human body working in a sandstorm**:

- The **Air Cleaner** is the **nose + lungs filter** – it must clean the dusty air before it reaches the "alveoli" (combustion chamber). Tractors work in fields where dust load can exceed **0.5 g/m³** (compared to 0.05 g/m³ in city air), so the air cleaner must be 10× more efficient.
- The **Fuel Filter** is the **kidney** – it purifies the "food" (diesel) before it reaches the "stomach" (combustion chamber), removing water and grit.
- The **Oil Filter** is the **liver** – it continuously cleans the "blood" (lubricating oil) that circulates to the "organs" (bearings, camshaft, pistons).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three cleaning stages?** A tractor engine typically has a service life of **6000–8000 hours** (versus ~3000 hours for a car). This longevity is impossible without an effective multi-stage cleaning system, because abrasive wear is the primary failure mode in off-road, dusty environments.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Terminology)

- **Bore (D)** = 75–125 mm (tractor class)
- **Stroke (L)** = 80–140 mm
- **Compression Ratio (r)** = **14:1 to 18:1** (for CI engines)
- **Swept Volume (V_s)** = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times n$ (where $n$ = number of cylinders)
- **Air-Fuel Ratio** = **18:1 to 25:1** (CI diesel, stoichiometric = 14.5:1, but always lean)
- **Dust capacity of oil-bath air cleaner** = **0.5–1.5 kg** before servicing
- **Filtration Efficiency (η)** = $\frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$, where $C$ = dust concentration in mg/m³
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details – Component-Wise Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Cylinder Block
- **Material**: Close-grained **cast iron** (Grade FG 200/250) or **aluminium alloy** for lightweight models.
- **Function**: Houses cylinders, crankshaft, camshaft, and forms the basic structural skeleton.
- **Construction**: Vertically split; in tractors, it is **rigid** (no cross-bolting) to handle high combustion pressures (**60–80 bar** peak).
- **Liners**: 
  - **Wet liners** (surrounded by coolant) – most common in tractors for easy replacement.
  - **Dry liners** (pressed into block) – used in compact tractors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tractor-Specific Feature**: Tractor cylinder blocks have **deep-skirt construction** (block extends well below the crankshaft centerline by 1.5× the stroke length) to resist torsional vibration from variable load operations like plowing.

### 2.1.2 Piston & Piston Rings
- **Material**: **Aluminium alloy (Al-Si eutectic, 12–18% Si)** for high thermal conductivity and low weight.
- **Crown shape**: For indirect injection (IDI) – **re-entrant (shallow bowl)**; for direct injection (DI) – **deep toroidal bowl** (Ricardo Comet V design).
- **Rings**: 3 rings typical:
  - **Top compression ring** (chrome-plated, barrel-faced)
  - **Second compression ring** (taper-faced, scraper)
  - **Oil control ring** (multi-piece, with expander spring)

> **Why chrome plating on the top ring?** Because in a tractor, the top ring experiences temperatures of **250–300°C** and bears the brunt of the combustion pressure; chromium resists scuffing and wear.

### 2.1.3 Connecting Rod
- **Material**: **Drop-forged steel** (e.g., EN8, EN15, or 40Cr alloy steel), I-section for high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Big end**: Split type, held by two **cap bolts** torqued to ~80–120 N·m.
- **Small end**: Bronze-bushed, oscillates on the **gudgeon (wrist) pin**.

### 2.1.4 Crankshaft
- **Material**: **Forged steel** (C45, 40Cr) or ductile iron; induction-hardened bearing journals.
- **Number of bearings**: Main bearings = Number of cylinders + 1 (e.g., 4 main bearings for a 3-cylinder engine).
- **Tractor-specific design**: Larger crankpin diameter to handle high firing pressures; counterweights sized for low-speed operation.

### 2.1.5 Valve Train
- **Valve clearance (cold)**: Intake = **0.20–0.30 mm**, Exhaust = **0.30–0.40 mm** (must be checked every **250 hours** of operation).
- **Valve timing**:
  - **IVO (Intake Valve Opens)** = 8°–12° **BTDC** (Before Top Dead Center)
  - **IVC (Intake Valve Closes)** = 35°–55° **ABDC**
  - **EVO (Exhaust Valve Opens)** = 35°–50° **BBDC**
  - **EVC (Exhaust Valve Closes)** = 8°–15° **ATDC**
- **Valve material**: Intake – **Silicon-chrome steel (EN52)**; Exhaust – **Austenitic steel (21-4N)** for heat resistance up to 800°C.

### 2.1.6 Flywheel
- **Function**: Stores rotational kinetic energy to take the piston through the non-firing strokes.
- **Tractor-specific**: Flywheel is **heavier** (5–8% of engine mass) than automotive flywheels to maintain momentum during sudden load variations (e.g., hitting a rock while plowing).

## 2.2 Cleaning Systems – Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Air Cleaning System (Most Critical in Tractors)

The air cleaning system in a tractor is a **two/three-stage system** because tractor dust concentration is **10–100 times higher** than ambient road dust.

**Stage 1 – Centrifugal Pre-Cleaner (Cyclonic Type):**
- Inlet air enters tangentially → spins at **2000–3000 rpm** → centrifugal force (F_c = m·v²/r) throws 70–80% of dust particles against the wall.
- Dust falls into a **dust cup** by gravity.
- **Efficiency**: Removes 70–85% of dust particles > 20 μm.

**Stage 2 – Oil Bath Air Cleaner (Primary Filter):**
- Air reverses direction, dips into an **oil bath** (SAE 30 oil, ~25 mm deep).
- Heavier particles stick to oil, lighter particles impinge on oil-coated mesh.
- Air then passes through **wire mesh/foam element** wetted with oil.
- **Efficiency**: 95–99% for particles > 5 μm.

**Stage 3 – Safety/Securing Element (Dry Paper Element):**
- A dry, replaceable **pleated paper element** that catches the remaining fine particles.
- Acts as a back-up if the oil-bath element is over-filled or damaged.
- **Efficiency**: 99.5%+ for particles > 1 μm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favorite Question Topic**: "Why is a centrifugal pre-cleaner used in tractor air cleaners?" – The answer must mention **(a) extending the life of the main filter, (b) reducing service frequency in dusty conditions, and (c) maintaining engine power by preventing air restriction**.

### 2.2.2 Fuel Cleaning System

A **multi-stage filtration** setup:
1. **Fuel Strainer** (mesh, ~100 μm) in the fuel tank – removes large debris.
2. **Primary Fuel Filter** (sediment bowl + replaceable element, ~10 μm) – removes water and sediment.
3. **Secondary Fuel Filter** (fine paper element, ~2–5 μm) – protects the injection pump.

> **Key Design Point**: Diesel fuel filters have a **water-separating element** because water causes **injector nozzle seizure** and **pitting of the injection pump plunger**.

### 2.2.3 Lubricating Oil Cleaning System

Two types:
- **Full-Flow Filtration** (all oil passes through filter) – most common in tractors.
- **By-Pass Filtration** (only 5–10% oil passes, deeper cleaning) – used in heavy-duty applications.

Filter types: **Mechanical (pleated paper), Centrifugal (bypass centrifuge), Magnetic (captures ferrous particles)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept Volume per cylinder | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | cm³ or litres |
| 2 | Total Displacement Volume | $V_d = V_s \times n$ | litres |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | 14:1 to 18:1 |
| 4 | Air-Fuel Ratio (CI) | $A/F = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | 18:1 to 25:1 |
| 5 | Centrifugal Force (Pre-cleaner) | $F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r}$ | Newtons |
| 6 | Pressure Drop across filter | $\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | kPa (max 5 kPa clean) |
| 7 | Filtration Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Dust Holding Capacity | $DHC = m_{dust\,collected}$ | grams |
| 9 | Air Velocity in Pre-cleaner | $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | m/s (15–25 m/s) |
| 10 | Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m = \frac{W_{cycle}}{V_d}$ | bar (7–9 bar BMEP) |
| 11 | Engine Torque | $T = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N}$ | N·m |
| 12 | Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) | $bsfc = \frac{m_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kW·h (220–250) |

> **KTU Examiner's Note**: When using $F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r}$, you MUST convert $v$ to m/s and $r$ to metres; otherwise, you will lose a mark for "unit inconsistency".
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force Acting on a Dust Particle in the Pre-Cleaner

The pre-cleaner uses **centrifugal separation**. We will derive the velocity needed to throw a dust particle of mass $m$ and diameter $d_p$ outward to the wall.

### Given Data (Typical Tractor Pre-Cleaner)
- Mean tangential velocity of air swirl: $v = 20 \text{ m/s}$
- Radius of pre-cleaner chamber: $r = 0.04 \text{ m}$ (4 cm)
- Dust particle density: $\rho_p = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (silica/quartz)
- Dust particle diameter: $d_p = 30 \text{ μm} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Volume of a spherical dust particle**

$$
V_p = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left( \frac{d_p}{2} \right)^3 = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of the dust particle**

$$
m = \rho_p \cdot V_p = \rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
m = 2500 \times \frac{\pi \times (30 \times 10^{-6})^3}{6}
$$

**Step 3: Expand the cubic term**

$$
(30 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 27000 \times 10^{-18} = 2.7 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the mass equation**

$$
m = 2500 \times \frac{\pi \times 2.7 \times 10^{-14}}{6} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the numerical numerator**

$$
2500 \times \pi \times 2.7 \times 10^{-14} = 2500 \times 8.482 \times 10^{-14} = 2.1205 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 6: Divide by 6 to obtain mass**

$$
m = \frac{2.1205 \times 10^{-10}}{6} \text{ kg} = 3.534 \times 10^{-11} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the centrifugal force using the formula**

$$
F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
F_c = \frac{3.534 \times 10^{-11} \times (20)^2}{0.04}
$$

**Step 8: Square the velocity**

$$
v^2 = 400 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 9: Multiply numerator terms**

$$
3.534 \times 10^{-11} \times 400 = 1.4136 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Step 10: Divide by radius to get force in Newtons**

$$
F_c = \frac{1.4136 \times 10^{-8}}{0.04} = 3.534 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{F_c \approx 3.53 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N per particle}}
$$

### Real-World Engineering Insight
This tiny force, applied to billions of particles per second, is what removes **~80% of dust** before it even touches the main filter. This extends the oil-bath element's life from **100 hours** (without pre-cleaner) to **400+ hours** in dusty agricultural operations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Filtration Efficiency of a Two-Stage Air Cleaner

Consider a two-stage system: **Pre-cleaner (Stage 1)** with efficiency $\eta_1 = 80\%$, followed by **Oil-bath element (Stage 2)** with efficiency $\eta_2 = 95\%$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Initial dust concentration entering Stage 1**

$$
C_{in} = 100 \text{ mg/m}^3 \text{ (assumed, for normalization)}
$$

**Step 2: Dust removed by Stage 1**

$$
C_{removed,1} = \eta_1 \times C_{in} = 0.80 \times 100 = 80 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Dust concentration entering Stage 2**

$$
C_{in,2} = C_{in} - C_{removed,1} = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Dust removed by Stage 2**

$$
C_{removed,2} = \eta_2 \times C_{in,2} = 0.95 \times 20 = 19 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Dust concentration leaving the system**

$$
C_{out} = C_{in,2} - C_{removed,2} = 20 - 19 = 1 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 6: Overall system efficiency**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{100 - 1}{100} \times 100
$$

**Step 7: Final result**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 99\%}
$$

### KTU Key Point
**Overall efficiency is NOT the sum of individual efficiencies.** The combined efficiency is calculated using multiplication of "pass-through fractions":

$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)
$$

**Verification**:
$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - 0.80)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - (0.20 \times 0.05) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\% \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Constructional Material Comparison

| Component | Primary Material | Alternative Material | Reason for Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder Block | Cast Iron (FG 250) | Aluminium Alloy (AlSi9Cu3) | CI = wear resistance; Al = weight saving |
| Piston | Al-Si Alloy (18% Si) | Forged Steel (older designs) | Al = low inertia, high conductivity |
| Cylinder Liner | Centrifugal Cast Iron | Chrome-plated steel | Wear resistance + oil retention |
| Connecting Rod | Forged Steel (40Cr) | Sintered Powder Metal (PM) | Strength + fatigue resistance |
| Crankshaft | Forged Steel (C45N) | Ductile Iron (SG Iron) | Toughness + balance |
| Camshaft | Chilled Cast Iron | Steel (carburized) | Surface hardness on lobes |
| Flywheel | Cast Iron | Dual-mass flywheel (DFM) | Mass moment of inertia for torque smoothing |
| Valve (Intake) | EN52 Steel | Titanium (high-end) | Heat resistance, cost |
| Valve (Exhaust) | 21-4N Austenitic | Inconel (racing) | Sustains 800°C exhaust temps |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cleaning System Comparison

| Parameter | Oil-Bath Air Cleaner | Dry-Type Air Cleaner | Cyclonic Pre-Cleaner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Filtration Efficiency | 95–99% | 99.5%+ | 70–85% |
| Maintenance Interval | 50–100 hrs | 250–500 hrs | Self-cleaning (dust cup) |
| Service Difficulty | Easy (oil change) | Moderate (replace element) | Easy (empty dust cup) |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | Low |
| Used in (Tractor class) | Older / economy | Modern / premium | Almost all (as Stage 1) |
| Sensitivity to Oil Level | **HIGH** (over-fill causes engine seizure) | None | None |
| Water Resistance | Moderate | Poor (paper damaged) | Excellent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tractor Engine & Cleaning Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AtmAtm["ATMOSPHERIC DUSTY AIR"]:::intake
    PC1["STAGE 1 CENTRIFUGAL PRE CLEANER"]:::stage
    DustCup["DUST COLLECTION CUP"]:::waste
    OB1["STAGE 2 OIL BATH ELEMENT"]:::stage
    SE1["STAGE 3 SAFETY DRY ELEMENT"]:::stage
    IntakeMan["INTAKE MANIFOLD"]:::flow
    CylHead["CYLINDER HEAD WITH VALVES"]:::engine
    Piston1["PISTON IN CYLINDER LINER"]:::engine
    Crank1["CRANKSHAFT AND FLYWHEEL"]:::engine
    ExhaustV["EXHAUST VALVE AND MANIFOLD"]:::exhaust
    ExhOut["EXHAUST TO MUFFLER"]:::exhaust

    AtmAtm --> PC1
    PC1 --> DustCup
    PC1 --> OB1
    OB1 --> SE1
    SE1 --> IntakeMan
    IntakeMan --> CylHead
    CylHead --> Piston1
    Piston1 --> Crank1
    CylHead --> ExhaustV
    ExhaustV --> ExhOut

    classDef intake fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef stage fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef waste fill:#f5e1e1,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
    classDef flow fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300
    classDef engine fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef exhaust fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Constructional Hierarchy of a Tractor Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TractorEng["TRACTOR DIESEL ENGINE ASSEMBLY"]:::root
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Oil-Bath Air Cleaner (Cross-Sectional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AirIn["DIRTY AIR INLET FROM PRE CLEANER"]:::intake
    GuideV["GUIDE VANE SWIRL CHAMBER"]:::stage
    OilCup["OIL BATH CUP SAE 30 OIL 25 MM DEEP"]:::oil
    MeshEl["OIL WETTED WIRE MESH ELEMENT"]:::stage
    SafetyEl["SAFETY PAPER ELEMENT DRY BACKUP"]:::stage
    CleanOut["CLEAN AIR TO INTAKE MANIFOLD"]:::flow
    SettledDust["SETTLED DUST TO BOTTOM"]:::waste
    OilReturn["OIL RETURN TO BATH"]:::oil

    AirIn --> GuideV
    GuideV --> MeshEl
    GuideV --> SettledDust
    SettledDust --> OilCup
    OilCup --> MeshEl
    MeshEl --> SafetyEl
    SafetyEl --> CleanOut
    OilCup --> OilReturn
    OilReturn --> MeshEl
```
