The bottom of the ladder is held at the desired digging depth by a **hydraulic cylinder** on the lift linkage. A **discharge chute** at the top directs the dumped soil sideways into a spoil bank or into a trailing hopper.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
1. The tractor moves slowly forward at a controlled creep speed (typically 0.2 – 1.5 km/h).
2. The PTO drives the chain, which drags the buckets through the soil at the bottom of the trench. Each bucket cuts a slice of soil equal to its width × depth.
3. The loaded bucket travels up the inclined ladder, retaining the soil by gravity.
4. At the top sprocket, the bucket inverts, dumping its contents onto the discharge chute.
5. The empty bucket returns down the back of the ladder to repeat the cycle.
6. Depth is regulated by the hydraulic cylinder, and the spoil is deposited in a continuous windrow alongside the trench.

*Sketch points to include in exam:* tractor, 3-point hitch, inclined ladder, top sprocket (driven), bottom sprocket (idler), chain with 5–6 buckets, dig zone at bottom, discharge chute at top, spoil bank on side, hydraulic depth-control cylinder.

---

**(b)** A tractor-mounted chain-bucket trench digger has the following specifications: bucket capacity $q = 25$ L, bucket spacing $L = 0.35$ m, chain speed $v = 1.2$ m/s, fill factor $k_f = 0.90$, loosening factor $k_l = 1.20$, and time efficiency $\eta_t = 0.75$. Calculate the **actual hourly production in m³/h** and the **number of buckets discharged per minute**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert bucket capacity to m³:**
$$q = 25 \; \text{L} = 0.025 \; \text{m}^3 \quad \text{(0.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical production rate (uncorrected):**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{3600 \cdot v \cdot q}{L} = \frac{3600 \times 1.2 \times 0.025}{0.35}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{108}{0.35} = 308.57 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply correction factors (one by one, as required by KTU valuators):**
$$Q = Q_{th} \times k_f \times k_l \times \eta_t$$

$$Q = 308.57 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 \times 0.75$$

- After fill factor: $308.57 \times 0.90 = 277.71$
- After loosening factor: $277.71 \times 1.20 = 333.26$
- After time efficiency: $333.26 \times 0.75 = 249.94$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 250 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Buckets discharged per minute:**
$$n = \frac{60 \cdot v}{L} = \frac{60 \times 1.2}{0.35} = \frac{72}{0.35}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 205.7 \;\text{buckets/min}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

#### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **bucket-wheel excavator**. Compare its productivity and application with a chain-bucket trencher. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A bucket-wheel excavator has a **large rotating wheel (the bucket wheel)** mounted on a **boom** at the end of a long **counterweight jib**. The wheel carries 6–20 buckets bolted radially around its rim. The wheel is driven by **electric or hydraulic motors** through a reduction gearbox. A **counter-rotating discharge chute** is positioned at the top of the wheel to deflect the dumped soil onto a **boom conveyor belt** that carries it to a discharge hopper or spreader. The entire upper structure sits on a **slewing ring** (carousel) and is mounted on **crawlers** for mobility.

**Working (2 marks):**
As the wheel rotates, the buckets on the lower quadrant dig into the soil face. The loaded buckets travel up the side of the wheel, and at the top they invert over the discharge chute, dumping the soil. The slewing motion allows the machine to sweep through a wide cutting arc, while the crawlers (or rail) provide feed-in. The digging depth is set by the boom angle and the undercarriage level.

**Comparison with chain-bucket trencher (2 marks):**

| Feature | Bucket-Wheel | Chain-Bucket |
|---|---|---|
| Production rate | Very high (500 – 5000 m³/h) | Moderate (100 – 500 m³/h) |
| Trench width | Wide (5 – 50 m cuts) | Narrow (0.3 – 1.5 m) |
| Application | Open-pit mining, large farms | Drainage, pipe-laying trenches |
| Mobility | Crawler/rail mounted | Tractor 3-point hitch |

---

**(b)** The digging resistance of a soil is given by the empirical relation $F_s = 18 + 6v + 2v^2$ kN/m, where $v$ is the wheel peripheral speed in m/s. If the cutting width of the bucket wheel is 4 m, calculate: (i) the total digging force, and (ii) the digging power required, when the wheel runs at $v = 1.0$ m/s. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$F_s = 18 + 6v + 2v^2 \quad \text{kN/m}, \quad b = 4 \; \text{m}, \quad v = 1.0 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate specific digging resistance at $v = 1.0$ m/s:**
$$F_s = 18 + 6(1.0) + 2(1.0)^2 = 18 + 6 + 2 = 26 \; \text{kN/m} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Total digging force:**
$$F_d = F_s \cdot b = 26 \times 4 = 104 \; \text{kN} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Digging power required:**
$$P_d = \frac{F_d \cdot v}{1000} = \frac{104 \times 1.0}{1000} = 0.104 \; \text{kW} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}$$

$$\boxed{F_d = 104 \; \text{kN}, \quad P_d = 0.104 \; \text{kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** A *very common* mistake is to forget dividing by 1000 when converting from kN·m/s to kW. Many students write $P_d = 104$ kW and lose 2 marks. Always check units: Power (kW) = Force (kN) × Speed (m/s) / **1000**.
> 2. **Skipping correction factors:** In production-rate problems, students often compute $Q_{th}$ and stop. KTU explicitly tests $Q$ (the *actual* rate), so failing to multiply by $k_f \times k_l \times \eta_t$ costs the full 3 marks allocated to that step.
> 3. **Confusing $q$ (bucket capacity) and $V_{discharge}$ (volume per discharge):** A bucket is rarely 100% full — that is what $k_f$ captures. Writing $V = n \cdot q$ without $k_f$ is a half-mark deduction at best.
> 4. **Mixing chain and wheel formulas:** A bucket-wheel has *discrete discharges per revolution*, while a chain-bucket has *discharges per minute = 60v/L*. Using the wrong one in a sketch-question's calculation is a frequent error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **multi-bucket excavator** uses a *closed loop* of buckets for **continuous** digging, lifting, and discharge.
- ✅ Two principal types: **Ladder/Chain-Bucket** (narrow-deep trenches) and **Bucket-Wheel** (wide-shallow high-volume cuts).
- ✅ **Rotary diggers** are a sub-class with a *single wheel* carrying 4–8 peripheral buckets, ideal for post-holes and small trenches.
- ✅ Master the master formula: $Q = \dfrac{3600 \cdot v \cdot q}{L} \cdot k_f \cdot k_l \cdot \eta_t$ — this is the **single most important equation** in the module.
- ✅ Number of discharges per minute on a chain: $n = \dfrac{60v}{L}$.
- ✅ Specific digging resistance is *empirical* and follows $F_s = k_1 + k_2 v + k_3 v^2$; the three terms represent static, viscous, and inertial contributions.
- ✅ **Power = Force × Velocity / 1000** — always check the unit conversion.
- ✅ Three independent correction factors in any production calculation: $k_f$ (fill), $k_l$ (loosening), $\eta_t$ (time). Do *not* collapse them into one combined factor.
- ✅ Rotary tillers share the kinematic principle (rotating soil-engaging element driven by PTO) but use L/C blades, not buckets — covered in Module 3 for completeness.
- ✅ Real-world exam context: trenching for farm drainage, post-hole boring in plantations, and bulk earth-moving for land levelling are the three classic application scenarios.
- ✅ **Most-marked KTU concepts** (from past 5 years): (i) derivation of production rate, (ii) the role of each $k$ factor, (iii) sketch + working of chain-bucket trencher, and (iv) the empirical $F_s$ polynomial.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second revision mantra:** *Continuous loop → buckets do all three jobs at once → production = 3600 v q / L corrected by three k-factors → power = F_d × v / 1000 → done.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scrapers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scrapers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scraper (Earthmoving Machine):** A scraper is a self-propelled or tractor-pulled piece of heavy equipment used in earthmoving operations that combines the functions of **excavating (scraping), loading, hauling, and spreading** loose material (soil, sand, gravel) in a single, continuous operation.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMUT523 - Tractors, Farm Equipments and Special Types of Vehicles), a scraper is classified as a versatile piece of **earthmoving and land-development machinery** primarily used in:

- Farm pond construction and large-area land leveling
- Farm road construction
- Canal and irrigation channel excavation
- Land reclamation and terracing projects

The defining feature of a scraper is the **integrated cutting edge (blade) combined with a moving bowl (hopper)** that simultaneously slices the earth and stores it for transport — a hybrid of a bulldozer, loader, and haul truck.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cookie Cutter on Wheels"**
> 
> Imagine a giant **cookie cutter (the cutting edge)** mounted on a **stainless-steel mixing bowl (the scraper bowl)**, being pulled across a soft chocolate cake. As the cookie cutter slices into the cake, the chocolate shavings automatically fall into the bowl being dragged right behind it. When the bowl is full, the bowl is closed like a clamshell, lifted slightly off the ground, and **towed to a different location** to deposit the load. That is exactly how a scraper works on a farm field.

| Component | Analogy | Real Function |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| Cutting edge | Cookie cutter | Slices soil from the ground |
| Bowl / Apron | Mixing bowl | Collects and stores the cut soil |
| Tractor (prime mover) | Person dragging the device | Provides traction and motion |
| Ejector (rear blade) | Cake spatula | Pushes the soil out at the dumping site |

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics for Scrapers (Industry Standards):**
> - **Heaped Capacity:** 8 m³ to 35 m³ (common farm range: 8–15 m³)
> - **Operating Speed (Hauling):** **15 to 25 km/h**
> - **Operating Speed (Returning):** **30 to 50 km/h**
> - **Cutting Width:** 2.4 m to 3.7 m
> - **Maximum Depth of Cut:** 200 mm to 500 mm
> - **Engine Power (Self-Propelled):** 200 kW to 600 kW

These metrics are **bold** in the KTU syllabus because they directly influence machine selection for a given farm project.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Scraper Works

The working cycle of a scraper consists of **four sequential phases**, each contributing to the machine's overall productivity:

### Phase 1: Loading (Scraping Phase)
- The scraper bowl is lowered so that the **cutting edge** bites into the soil.
- The prime mover (tractor or self-propelled engine) pulls/pushes the scraper forward.
- The **apron** (front gate) is in the **raised/open** position to allow soil to enter the bowl.
- Soil is sliced and lifted by the cutting edge, falling into the bowl under gravity.
- Loading is assisted by **loading aids** in larger scrapers: either a **powered elevator** (chain-flight or auger) or a **pusher tractor** (push-loading).
- The loading phase typically lasts **30 to 90 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loading Aids — A KTU Favourite Question:**
> - **Elevating-type scraper:** Self-loading using a flight conveyor or auger. No pusher needed.
> - **Conventional (Open-bowl) scraper:** Requires a **pusher tractor** (or push-pull arrangement) to force the bowl into the soil at full cutting depth. The pusher engages the rear bumper of the scraper during loading only.

### Phase 2: Hauling (Transport Phase)
- The bowl is **raised** off the ground (to reduce rolling resistance).
- The **apron (front gate)** is hydraulically **closed** to seal the load.
- The scraper travels at high speed (15–25 km/h) to the dump site.
- Engine power and gear selection are critical here for climbing grades.

### Phase 3: Spreading (Dumping Phase)
- The scraper arrives at the fill area and moves forward at a controlled low speed.
- The **ejector blade** (rear wall) moves forward hydraulically, **pushing the soil out** of the bowl.
- The thickness of the spread layer is controlled by adjusting the **bowl height and ejector speed**.
- Spreading typically takes **15 to 30 seconds**.

### Phase 4: Returning
- The empty scraper returns to the cut area at maximum speed (30–50 km/h).
- The cycle repeats.

## 2.2 Types of Scrapers

### 2.2.1 Based on Power Source

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Tractor-Pulled (Towed) Scraper** | Pulled by a farm tractor; usually 4–10 m³ capacity | Small/medium farms, leveling |
| **Self-Propelled Scraper** | Has its own engine; higher speeds; 15–35 m³ | Large construction, commercial farms |
| **Push-Pull Scraper** | Two scrapers work in tandem; each pushes the other during loading | Reduces need for separate pusher |

### 2.2.2 Based on Loading Mechanism

| Type | Mechanism | Key Feature |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Open-Bowl (Conventional) Scraper** | Relies on a pusher tractor | Simple, low cost, high capacity |
| **Elevating Scraper** | Has powered elevator/flights inside bowl | Self-loading, no pusher required |
| **Auger Scraper** | Uses rotating auger for loading | Self-loading, good for cohesive soils |

## 2.3 Main Components of a Scraper (Detailed)

1. **Bowl (Hopper):** The large container that holds the soil. Made of high-strength steel with a curved bottom for easy ejection.
2. **Cutting Edge:** Replaceable hardened-steel blade bolted to the bottom of the bowl. This is the **primary wear item**.
3. **Apron (Front Gate):** A hydraulically controlled vertical wall at the front that opens during loading and closes during hauling.
4. **Ejector:** A movable rear wall pushed by a hydraulic cylinder that **forces the material out** at the dump site.
5. **Draft Frame / Hitch:** Connects the scraper to the tractor (for towed units) or supports the rear axle.
6. **Tailgate (Optional):** Some scrapers have a rear gate to help force material out and aid spreading.
7. **Wheels/Tires:** Large off-road tires; dual rear tires common to distribute load.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams (Scrapers)**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Scraper Production (Volume Basis)** | $P_v = \dfrac{V \times 3600 \times E \times f}{T_c}$ | m³/h | V = heaped bowl capacity |
| 2 | **Scraper Production (Weight Basis)** | $P_w = \dfrac{V \times \rho_b \times 3600 \times E \times f}{T_c}$ | tonnes/h | $\rho_b$ = bank density of soil |
| 3 | **Total Cycle Time** | $T_c = T_l + T_h + T_d + T_r$ | seconds | l=load, h=haul, d=dump, r=return |
| 4 | **Haul Time** | $T_h = \dfrac{D_h}{S_h} \times 3600$ | seconds | $D_h$ = haul distance (km), $S_h$ = haul speed (km/h) |
| 5 | **Return Time** | $T_r = \dfrac{D_r}{S_r} \times 3600$ | seconds | $D_r$ = return distance (km) |
| 6 | **Load Growth Factor** | $f = \dfrac{\text{Bank volume}}{\text{Loose volume in bowl}}$ | dimensionless | $f \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.0$ |
| 7 | **Rolling Resistance Force** | $F_{rr} = W \times R$ | kN | R = rolling resistance factor |
| 8 | **Total Haul Resistance** | $R_t = W(\sin\theta + R)$ | kN | $\theta$ = grade angle |
| 9 | **Effective Grade (Combined)** | $R_{eff} = R + G$ | % or N/kg | R = rolling resistance, G = grade |
| 10 | **Swell Factor** | $S = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_l}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_l$ = loose density |
| 11 | **Bowl Capacity (Bank Volume Equivalent)** | $V_b = V_{heaped} \times \dfrac{1}{S}$ | m³ | Convert loose to bank measure |
| 12 | **Job Efficiency** | $E$ (typically 0.75 to 0.85) | dimensionless | Accounts for operator + delays |

> **Critical Boundary Conditions:**
> - $0 < E \leq 1$ (efficiency cannot exceed 100%)
> - $0 < f \leq 1$ (load factor is always ≤ 1 in bank volume terms)
> - Swell factor $S \geq 1$ always (loose density is always less than bank density)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Scrapers are the **backbone of bulk earthmoving** because they eliminate the need for separate loading equipment (like a front-end loader) and haul trucks. On a farm, a single tractor-scraper combination can:
- Construct a farm pond of 1 acre in a few days
- Level 5–10 hectares of uneven land
- Build farm roads at 200–500 m/day

> **Engineering Insight:** For KTU exams, students must understand that a scraper's productivity is dominated by **haul distance** — beyond **300 m haul distance**, scraper productivity drops dramatically, and trucks become more economical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Scraper Production Rate

The **production rate of a scraper** is the most important calculation in KTU earthmoving problems. We will derive it step-by-step from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Starting from Fundamental Definition

By definition, production rate is the **volume of material moved per unit time**, measured in bank cubic metres (BCM) or loose cubic metres (LCM) per hour.

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Volume of one load (bank)}}{\text{Cycle time per load}} \times \text{Number of cycles per hour} \times \text{Efficiency}$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1:** Express the volume of one load in **bank measure**.
The bowl's nominal (rated) capacity is given in **loose (heaped) measure**. To convert to bank measure, we divide by the swell factor $S$.

$$V_b = \dfrac{V_l}{S} = V_l \times f$$

where $f$ is the **load factor** (the reciprocal of the swell factor). This represents the actual in-ground volume of material the bowl's heaped loose load corresponds to.

**Step 2:** Express the number of cycles per hour.

A single cycle takes $T_c$ seconds. So in 3600 seconds (1 hour), the number of cycles is:

$$N = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by job efficiency $E$.

Job efficiency accounts for operator skill, minor delays, and idle time. The actual productive cycles per hour are:

$$N_{actual} = \dfrac{3600 \times E}{T_c}$$

**Step 4:** Combine all terms.

The volume-based production rate becomes:

$$P_v = \dfrac{V_l \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{h, bank measure}]$$

For weight-based production, multiply by the bank density $\rho_b$:

$$P_w = \dfrac{V_l \times f \times \rho_b \times 3600 \times E}{T_c} \quad [\text{tonnes}/\text{h}]$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A tractor-pulled scraper has a heaped bowl capacity of **10 m³**. The soil has a bank density of **1700 kg/m³** and a swell factor of **1.25**. The cycle consists of: Load = 60 s, Haul 200 m at 15 km/h, Dump = 30 s, Return 200 m at 40 km/h. The job efficiency is **0.80** (50-min hour). Calculate the production rate in m³/h (bank) and tonnes/h.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute Haul Time**

$$T_h = \dfrac{D_h}{S_h} \times 3600 = \dfrac{0.2 \text{ km}}{15 \text{ km/h}} \times 3600 = 48 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Return Time**

$$T_r = \dfrac{D_r}{S_r} \times 3600 = \dfrac{0.2 \text{ km}}{40 \text{ km/h}} \times 3600 = 18 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Total Cycle Time**

$$T_c = T_l + T_h + T_d + T_r = 60 + 48 + 30 + 18 = 156 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compute Load Factor**

$$f = \dfrac{1}{S} = \dfrac{1}{1.25} = 0.80$$

**Step 5: Production Rate (Volume, Bank Measure)**

$$P_v = \dfrac{V_l \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.80 \times 3600 \times 0.80}{156}$$

$$P_v = \dfrac{23040}{156} \approx 147.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h (bank measure)}$$

**Step 6: Production Rate (Weight)**

$$P_w = P_v \times \rho_b = 147.7 \times 1.7 = 251.1 \text{ tonnes}/\text{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Production Rate = **147.7 m³/h (bank)** = **251.1 tonnes/h**

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Haul Resistance

When a loaded scraper moves up a slope, two opposing forces act on it:
- **Rolling resistance** (due to tire deformation and soil deformation): $F_{rr} = W \cdot R$
- **Grade resistance** (due to gravitational pull along the slope): $F_{grade} = W \cdot \sin\theta$

For small angles (typical farm road grades < 10%), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = G$ (grade in % / 100). The **total resistance** is:

$$R_t = F_{rr} + F_{grade} = W \cdot R + W \cdot \sin\theta = W(R + \sin\theta)$$

If we express grade as a percentage and combine with rolling resistance:

$$R_t = W(R + G)$$

where $G$ is the grade in decimal form (e.g., 5% grade → $G = 0.05$).

**Example:** A 20-tonne loaded scraper on a 4% grade with rolling resistance 4%:

$$R_t = 200 \text{ kN} \times (0.04 + 0.04) = 16 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Tractor-Pulled Scraper | Self-Propelled Scraper | Elevating Scraper |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Capacity (m³) | 4–10 | 15–35 | 8–25 |
| Loading Method | Pusher required | Pusher required | Self-loading |
| Haul Speed (km/h) | 8–15 | 20–30 | 20–30 |
| Return Speed (km/h) | 15–25 | 35–50 | 35–50 |
| Cost (₹/machine) | Low | Very high | High |
| Best Haul Distance | < 200 m | 200–1500 m | 200–1000 m |
| Operator Skill | Low | High | High |
| Suitable for Farm? | **Excellent** | Marginal | Good for medium farms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Scraper System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prime Mover - Tractor or Self-Propelled Engine] --> B[Hydraulic System]
    A --> C[Draft Frame / Hitch]
    C --> D[Scraper Bowl Assembly]
    D --> E[Cutting Edge - Replaceable]
    D --> F[Apron - Front Gate]
    D --> G[Main Bowl - Storage]
    D --> H[Ejector - Rear Wall]
    B --> F
    B --> H
    B --> I[Bowl Lift Cylinder]
    I --> G
    D --> J[Rear Wheels - Tires]
    A --> K[Operator Cabin / Station]
    K --> A
    L[Soil Surface] --> E
    E --> M[Sliced Soil Flake]
    M --> G
    H --> N[Spreading at Fill Site]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Prime Mover** (tractor engine) supplies mechanical and hydraulic power.
- Hydraulic power operates the **Apron (open/close)**, **Ejector (push forward)**, and **Bowl Lift (raise/lower)**.
- The **Cutting Edge** slices soil → falls into the **Bowl** → carried to the dump site → pushed out by the **Ejector**.

## 4.2 Scraper Working Cycle — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Loading
    Loading: Loading - Bowl down, Apron open, Cutting edge in soil
    Loading --> Hauling: Bowl full, Apron closed
    Hauling: Hauling - Bowl raised, Travel to dump site
    Hauling --> Spreading: Arrive at fill area
    Spreading: Spreading - Ejector pushes soil out at controlled thickness
    Spreading --> Returning: Bowl empty, Ejector retracted
    Returning: Returning - Travel back to cut area at high speed
    Returning --> Loading: Re-enter cut zone
    Loading --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Pusher Loading vs Elevator Self-Loading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SL[Self-Loading Elevator Scraper]
        S1[Cutting Edge slices soil] --> S2[Soil falls onto flight conveyor]
        S2 --> S3[Flight conveyor lifts soil into bowl]
        S3 --> S4[No pusher required]
    end
    subgraph PL[Pusher-Loaded Open-Bowl Scraper]
        P1[Scraper alone cannot fully load] --> P2[Pusher tractor engages rear bumper]
        P2 --> P3[Combined push force drives cutting edge deeper]
        P3 --> P4[Soil flows into open bowl by gravity]
    end
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The elevating scraper (left) uses an internal mechanism, making it self-sufficient. The open-bowl scraper (right) **requires a separate pusher tractor** to achieve full loading within a short time.

## 4.4 Scraper Component Layout — Cross-Sectional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopView[Top View of Scraper Assembly]
        direction LR
        T1[Tractor] --- T2[Hitch / Draft Frame]
        T2 --- T3[Front of Bowl - Apron]
        T3 --- T4[Main Bowl Interior]
        T4 --- T5[Rear of Bowl - Ejector]
        T5 --- T6[Rear Wheels - Tires]
    end
    subgraph SideView[Side View Functions]
        direction TB
        SV1[Apron - Vertical sliding wall, controls bowl opening]
        SV2[Cutting Edge - Bottom front, slices soil]
        SV3[Bowl - Curved steel container, holds material]
        SV4[Ejector - Moves horizontally forward, expels material]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Q:** Define a scraper. List the four basic functions performed by a scraper in a single working cycle.

**Model Answer:**
> A scraper is a self-propelled or tractor-pulled earthmoving machine designed to **scrape, load, haul, and spread** loose material such as soil, gravel, or sand in a single integrated operation.
> 
> The four basic functions in one cycle are:
> 1. **Loading** — cutting and collecting soil using the cutting edge and bowl.
> 2. **Hauling** — transporting the loaded material to the disposal site.
> 3. **Spreading** — ejecting and distributing the material at the fill site.
> 4. **Returning** — moving the empty machine back to the cutting area for the next cycle.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Q:** Differentiate between an **open-bowl scraper** and an **elevating scraper** with respect to loading mechanism.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Bowl Scraper | Elevating Scraper |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Loading aid | Requires a **pusher tractor** | Has built-in **flight elevator or auger** |
| Self-loading | No | Yes |
| Pusher needed? | Yes (mandatory for full load) | No |
| Loading speed | Slower | Faster (self-powered) |
| Best for | Loose, granular soils | Wider range of soil types |

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a tractor-pulled scraper**. List its main components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A self-propelled scraper has the following specifications: Heaped capacity = **15 m³**, Swell factor = **1.20**, Loading time = **45 s**, Haul distance = **400 m** at **20 km/h**, Dump time = **25 s**, Return distance = **400 m** at **45 km/h**, Job efficiency = **0.83** (50-min hour). Calculate the **production rate in m³/h (bank measure) and tonnes/h** if the bank density of soil is **1800 kg/m³**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Working Principle:**
A tractor-pulled scraper operates on the principle of **simultaneous cutting and collecting** using a moving bowl. The tractor supplies both the draft (pulling) force and hydraulic power. The cycle is as follows:

1. **Loading:** The bowl is lowered; the cutting edge slices soil. The apron is open; soil accumulates in the bowl. A **pusher tractor** may assist by pushing from behind to increase cutting depth.

2. **Hauling:** The bowl is raised slightly; the apron is closed to seal the load. The tractor pulls the scraper to the dump site.

3. **Spreading:** At the fill area, the ejector blade moves forward, pushing the material out in a controlled layer.

4. **Returning:** The empty scraper returns to the cut zone at high speed.

**Main Components (Block Diagram Refer to Section 4.1):**
- Prime mover (tractor)
- Draft frame / hitch
- Bowl with cutting edge
- Apron (front gate)
- Ejector (rear wall)
- Hydraulic system
- Wheels / tires

---

#### Part (b) — Production Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Haul Time**

$$T_h = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ km}}{20 \text{ km/h}} \times 3600 = 72 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Return Time**

$$T_r = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ km}}{45 \text{ km/h}} \times 3600 = 32 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Total Cycle Time**

$$T_c = 45 + 72 + 25 + 32 = 174 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Load Factor**

$$f = \dfrac{1}{S} = \dfrac{1}{1.20} = 0.8333$$

**Step 5: Volume Production (Bank Measure)**

$$P_v = \dfrac{V_l \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.8333 \times 3600 \times 0.83}{174}$$

$$P_v = \dfrac{37369.26}{174} \approx 214.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 6: Weight Production**

$$P_w = P_v \times \rho_b = 214.8 \times 1.8 = 386.6 \text{ tonnes}/\text{h}$$

> **Final Answer:** $P_v \approx 214.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{h (bank)}$, $P_w \approx 386.6 \text{ tonnes}/\text{h}$

---

### ⭐ Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **types of scrapers** based on (i) power source and (ii) loading mechanism, with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the formula for **scraper production rate in bank measure**. A tractor-pulled scraper has a heaped capacity of **8 m³**. The soil swell is **25%**, and the load factor is **0.80**. If the cycle time is **120 s** and the job efficiency is **0.75**, determine the **hourly production** in m³/h. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Types of Scrapers (7 Marks)

**(i) Based on Power Source:**
1. **Tractor-pulled (Towed) scraper** — pulled by a farm tractor; small to medium capacity (4–10 m³); ideal for farms.
2. **Self-propelled scraper** — has its own diesel engine; high speed and capacity; used in commercial projects.
3. **Push-pull scraper** — pair of self-propelled scrapers that push each other during loading, then pull apart for hauling.

**(ii) Based on Loading Mechanism:**
1. **Open-bowl scraper** — needs a pusher tractor to achieve full load.
2. **Elevating scraper** — has an internal flight elevator; self-loading.
3. **Auger-type scraper** — uses a rotating auger inside the bowl for loading.

*(Refer to Mermaid block diagrams in Section 4.3 for visual representation.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Calculation (7 Marks)

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Heaped capacity $V_l$ is in **loose measure**. To find bank volume equivalent, divide by swell factor $S$:

$$V_b = \dfrac{V_l}{S} = V_l \cdot f$$

**Step 2:** Number of cycles per hour (with efficiency $E$):

$$N = \dfrac{3600 \times E}{T_c}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply:

$$\boxed{P_v = \dfrac{V_l \cdot f \cdot 3600 \cdot E}{T_c} \text{ m}^3/\text{h (bank)}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_l = 8$ m³, $S = 1.25$, $f = 0.80$, $T_c = 120$ s, $E = 0.75$.

$$P_v = \dfrac{8 \times 0.80 \times 3600 \times 0.75}{120}$$

$$P_v = \dfrac{17280}{120} = 144 \text{ m}^3/\text{h (bank measure)}$$

> **Final Answer:** Production = **144 m³/h (bank measure)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Scraper Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert hauler distance to km** — Students often plug metres into a formula expecting km/h, getting 3600× error. Always express $D$ in **km** when $S$ is in **km/h**.
> 2. **Mixing loose and bank volumes** — The heaped bowl capacity is **always loose**. You must divide by swell (or multiply by load factor) to get bank measure for true productivity reporting.
> 3. **Ignoring job efficiency** — A scraper does NOT work a full 60-minute hour. KTU expects the 50-min hour ($E = 0.83$) unless stated otherwise.
> 4. **Confusing `f` and `S`** — They are reciprocals. Writing $f = S$ in your answer will cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always write "m³/h" or "tonnes/h"; the unit is half the answer.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scraper** = integrated earthmover that **scrapes, loads, hauls, spreads** in one machine.
- **Two main types to remember:** Tractor-pulled (farm-friendly) and Self-propelled (commercial).
- **Open-bowl vs Elevating:** Open-bowl **needs pusher**; Elevating is **self-loading**.
- **Cutting edge** = primary wear part, made of hardened steel, replaceable.
- **Apron** = front gate; open during load, closed during haul.
- **Ejector** = rear wall; pushes material out at dump site.
- **Production formula (master this):**
  $$P_v = \dfrac{V_l \cdot f \cdot 3600 \cdot E}{T_c}$$
- **Cycle time = Load + Haul + Dump + Return** — memorize this addition.
- **Swell factor $S \geq 1$**, **Load factor $f \leq 1$**, **Efficiency $E \leq 1$** — these constraints appear in MCQs.
- **Haul distance threshold:** Scraper is economical only up to **~300 m** one-way; beyond that, trucks win.
- **Conversion rule:** Bowl capacity is always **loose (heaped)** → divide by **swell** to get **bank** volume.
- **KTU favourite terms:** Bowl, Apron, Ejector, Cutting edge, Draft frame, Elevator, Pusher tractor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elevating Graders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elevating Graders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ELEVATING GRADER (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> An **Elevating Grader** is a self-propelled (or tractor-drawn) earthmoving machine used primarily in road construction and maintenance, equipped with a **cutting moldboard (plough)** that dislodges soil or aggregate material, combined with an **integrated chain-and-flight elevator** that continuously lifts, conveys, and discharges the loosened material either to one or both sides as a **windrow**, into a transport vehicle, or back onto the road surface for uniform spreading.

The machine simultaneously performs **three operations**: scarifying/cutting, elevating, and spreading/mixing — making it a hybrid between a conventional motor grader and a belt-loader or elevating scraper.

**Primary Standard Metric (KTU 2024 Reference):** Production capacity is measured in **bank cubic meters per hour (bcm/hr)** or **loose cubic meters per hour (lcm/hr)**, with operating speeds typically ranging from **0.25 km/hr to 1.5 km/hr**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a "moving kitchen spatula + conveyor belt on wheels."**
>
> Imagine you are using a flat spatula to scrape burnt food off a frying pan. Now imagine that, as the spatula scrapes, a tiny conveyor belt (like the ones at grocery store checkouts) sits right next to it, scooping up everything the spatula loosens and dropping it neatly into a pile — or, if you want, spreading it evenly back across the pan.
>
> - The **spatula** = the **moldboard/plough**
> - The **conveyor belt** = the **elevator with cross flights**
> - The **frying pan** = the **ground/road surface**
>
> That's exactly what an elevating grader does, but in **continuous, industrial scale**, moving along a road at a slow, controlled pace.

---

### 1.3 Why It Was Invented — The Problem It Solves

| Conventional Motor Grader | Elevating Grader |
|---|---|
| Pushes material to the side (limited control) | **Lifts** material, can discharge in controlled windrow |
| Cannot work efficiently in wet, sticky material | Designed to handle **moist, cohesive soils** |
| Needs separate loader to load trucks | Can **load trucks** directly via conveyor |
| Multi-pass operation for mixing | **Single-pass scarifying + mixing** |
| Slower in deep cuts | Superior in **shallow, wide cuts** (gravel road maintenance) |

---

### 1.4 Key Components (KTU 2024 Naming Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Terminology — Memorize These Terms:**
>
> 1. **Moldboard (Plough)** — Curved cutting blade
> 2. **Elevator** — Inclined chain-and-flight conveyor
> 3. **Cross Conveyor / Discharge Chute** — Lateral material delivery system
> 4. **Tractor Unit** — Provides traction and power
> 5. **Operator's Platform** — Houses controls
> 6. **Wheel Lean Mechanism** — Tilts wheels for slope work
> 7. **Scarifier (Optional)** — Pre-loosens hard ground

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three-Stage Process

**Stage 1 — Scarification / Cutting:**
- The **moldboard** is hydraulically lowered and angled against the ground.
- A replaceable **cutting edge** (typically high-carbon steel) shears the material at a controlled depth.
- Cutting depth: **typically 25 mm to 150 mm**.
- The moldboard is set at a **cutting angle (α)** between **30° and 45°** to the direction of travel.

**Stage 2 — Elevation / Conveyance:**
- Material riding up the moldboard is intercepted by the **rotating elevator**.
- The elevator consists of two parallel chains carrying **steel flights (buckets)** mounted on rubber or steel cross-bars.
- The flights scrape material off the moldboard and carry it **upward and rearward**.
- Elevator speed: typically **0.5 m/s to 1.5 m/s** (variable via hydraulic drive).

**Stage 3 — Discharge / Spreading:**
- At the top of the elevator, material falls onto a **discharge chute or cross conveyor**.
- It can be:
  - **Windrowed** to one side (left or right)
  - **Loaded into a truck** moving alongside
  - **Re-spread** across the road surface for mixing/blending

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme Board-Exam-Relevant.** Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Production Capacity** | $$Q = \frac{V \cdot W \cdot D}{n}$$ | $Q$ = production (m³/hr), $V$ = forward speed (m/hr), $W$ = moldboard width (m), $D$ = cut depth (m), $n$ = number of passes |
| 2 | **Elevator Flight Capacity** | $$C_e = 60 \cdot v_e \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_b$$ | $C_e$ = elevator throughput (tonnes/hr), $v_e$ = elevator chain speed (m/min), $A_f$ = cross-section of flight (m²), $\rho_b$ = bulk density (tonnes/m³) |
| 3 | **Effective Field Production** | $$Q_{eff} = Q_{theo} \cdot E_t$$ | $E_t$ = job efficiency factor (**typically 0.50 to 0.83**) |
| 4 | **Material Swell Factor Relation** | $$\rho_b = \rho_{bank} \cdot (1 + s)$$ | $s$ = swell factor (**typically 0.20 to 0.40** for soil) |
| 5 | **Required Tractive Effort (Rimpull)** | $$F_t = W \cdot D \cdot R_r + G \cdot \sin\theta$$ | $R_r$ = specific resistance (kPa), $G$ = gross weight (kN), $\theta$ = grade angle |
| 6 | **Elevator Shaft Power** | $$P_e = \frac{C_e \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$$ | $P_e$ = power (kW), $H$ = vertical lift height (m), $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency |
| 7 | **Moldboard Cutting Force** | $$F_c = k \cdot W \cdot D$$ | $k$ = specific cutting resistance (**typically 30 to 100 kPa**) |
| 8 | **Forward Speed Limit (Cohesive Soil)** | $$V_{max} = \frac{v_e \cdot \tan\alpha}{1.5}$$ | To prevent material pile-up on moldboard |

---

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Rural Road Construction (Kerala Context):** Used in building village roads through laterite, clayey, and wet soils where conventional graders slip.
- **Gravel Road Maintenance:** Re-blends the wearing course by scarifying, mixing, and re-spreading in **one pass**.
- **Land Reclamation:** Lifts waterlogged soil and discharges it into trucks for transport.
- **Canal and Drain Work:** Cuts banks cleanly and windrows material away from the working face.
- **Forest Road Construction:** Operates in muddy, root-laden terrain where wheeled loaders get stuck.

---

### 2.4 Comparison: Elevating Grader vs. Other Earthmovers

| Parameter | Elevating Grader | Motor Grader | Elevating Scraper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Self-loading? | Yes (via elevator) | No (pushes only) | Yes (with elevator) |
| Hauls material? | No (short-range) | No | Yes (long-range) |
| Works in wet soil? | Excellent | Poor | Moderate |
| Typical use | Road maintenance | Finish grading | Bulk earthmoving |
| Truck loading | Yes (side discharge) | No | Yes (self) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Elevator Flight Capacity (Equation 2)

This is a high-weightage KTU derivation. We derive it **from first principles**.

**Given:**
- Elevator chain speed = $v_e$ (m/min)
- Cross-sectional area of each flight (rectangular bucket) = $A_f$ (m²)
- Bulk density of loose material = $\rho_b$ (tonnes/m³)

**Step 1 — Volume carried per minute by a single flight**

Each flight scoops material of volume $A_f$ as it moves one chain-pitch distance. The volume per minute moved by **one flight** is:

$$V_{1} = v_e \cdot A_f \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min per flight})$$

**Step 2 — Reason about the row of flights**

Since flights are mounted in **parallel pairs** (one on each chain) and the spacing between flights is fixed by the chain pitch, the *effective* volume transported is the sum of all flights passing a cross-section per minute. In the ideal model (no spillage, no slip), the throughput is the same as the single-flight equation:

$$V_{total} = v_e \cdot A_f \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min})$$

**Step 3 — Convert volumetric flow to mass flow (tonnes per hour)**

Multiply by 60 to convert minutes → hours, and by bulk density to convert volume → mass:

$$C_e = 60 \cdot v_e \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_b \quad (\text{tonnes/hr})$$

**Final Derived Equation (Boxed for KTU Exam):**

$$\boxed{C_e = 60 \, v_e \, A_f \, \rho_b \;\; [\text{tonnes/hr}]}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Production Capacity (Equation 1)

**Given:**
- A rectangular cut of width $W$ and depth $D$ over a travel distance $L$.

**Step 1 — Volume of one swath**

$$V_{swath} = W \cdot D \cdot L \quad (\text{m}^3)$$

**Step 2 — Time taken for one swath**

Forward speed $V$ is in m/hr, so:

$$t_{swath} = \frac{L}{V} \quad (\text{hr})$$

**Step 3 — Volume per hour per single pass**

$$Q_{1} = \frac{V_{swath}}{t_{swath}} = \frac{W \cdot D \cdot L}{L/V} = V \cdot W \cdot D \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{hr})$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for $n$ passes**

If the job requires $n$ passes over the same area:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{V \cdot W \cdot D}{n} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{hr}]}$$

**Step 5 — Apply job efficiency**

Since real machines have delays (turning, hydraulic response, operator breaks):

$$Q_{eff} = Q \cdot E_t \quad \text{where} \quad 0.50 \le E_t \le 0.83$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Typical Problem

> **Problem:**
> An elevating grader has a moldboard width of **3.0 m**, cuts at a depth of **75 mm**, and travels at **900 m/hr**. Each pass is the final pass, so $n = 1$. Job efficiency is **0.75**. Find the effective production in **m³/hr**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute into the production equation.

$$Q = \frac{V \cdot W \cdot D}{n} = \frac{900 \times 3.0 \times 0.075}{1}$$

$$Q = 202.5 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 2:** Apply efficiency.

$$Q_{eff} = 202.5 \times 0.75 = 151.875 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 3:** Final boxed answer.

$$\boxed{Q_{eff} \approx 151.88 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Remedies

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Symptom | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material pile-up on moldboard | Elevator speed too low vs. forward speed | Spillage forward of machine | Increase $v_e$ or reduce $V$ |
| Chain slippage | Wet clay adhering to flights | Elevator stalls | Add cleaning scrapers; use harder flight edges |
| Moldboard wear | Abrasive laterite (Kerala soil) | Rounded cutting edge | Use **reversible, replaceable cutting edges** |
| Sideways drift on slope | Insufficient wheel lean | Erratic cut | Engage **wheel lean mechanism** |
| Production loss in rain | Material sticks to chute | Stops discharge | Add **vibrator** on discharge chute |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Elevating Grader

The diagram below traces **material flow** and **power flow** through every major subsystem of the elevating grader. This is the **standard functional architecture** expected in KTU 2024 Scheme answer scripts for Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DIESEL ENGINE\nPRIME MOVER] --> B[TRANSMISSION\nGEARBOX]
    B --> C[REAR AXLE\nAND WHEELS]
    B --> D[HYDRAULIC\nPUMP]
    D --> E[MOLDBOARD\nLIFT CYLINDER]
    D --> F[WHEEL LEAN\nCYLINDER]
    D --> G[ELEVATOR\nHYDRAULIC MOTOR]
    H[MOLDBOARD\nCUTTING BLADE] --> I[MATERIAL\nLIFT STAGE]
    I --> J[CHAIN AND FLIGHT\nELEVATOR]
    J --> K[DISCHARGE\nCHUTE]
    K --> L1[WINDROW\nLEFT SIDE]
    K --> L2[TRUCK LOADING\nCROSS CONVEYOR]
    K --> L3[RE-SPREAD\nONTO ROAD]
    M[OPERATOR\nPLATFORM] --> A
    M --> B
    M --> D
    M --> G
    N[SCARIFIER\nOPTIONAL] --> O[GROUND]
    H --> O
    J --> P[ELEVATOR FRAME\nSTRUCTURE]
    C --> Q[GROUND\nTRACTION]
```

---

### 4.2 Component Interconnection Matrix

| Subsystem | Input | Output | Connects To |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diesel Engine | Diesel fuel | Mechanical power | Transmission, Hydraulic Pump |
| Transmission | Engine torque | Controlled torque + speed | Rear Axle, Elevator Motor |
| Hydraulic Pump | Engine rotation | Pressurized fluid | Lift Cylinders, Lean Cylinders, Elevator Motor |
| Moldboard Assembly | Hydraulic lift force | Cut material + force feedback | Ground, Elevator |
| Elevator (Chain + Flights) | Material from moldboard | Elevated material | Discharge Chute |
| Discharge Chute | Elevated material | Windrow or truck | Output zones L1/L2/L3 |
| Operator Platform | Human control inputs | Control signals | Engine, Transmission, Hydraulics |
| Wheel Lean Cylinders | Hydraulic pressure | Wheel tilt | Rear Axle |
| Scarifier (Optional) | Hydraulic down-force | Pre-loosened soil | Ground |

---

### 4.3 Material Flow Path — Engineering Trace

The path the material takes through the machine:

$$\text{Ground} \xrightarrow{\text{Scarifier (optional)}} \text{Pre-loosened soil} \xrightarrow{\text{Moldboard}} \text{Cut + lifted} \xrightarrow{\text{Elevator flights}} \text{Conveyed upward} \xrightarrow{\text{Discharge chute}} \text{Windrow/Truck/Spread}$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

> Define an **elevating grader**. List any **four advantages** it has over a conventional motor grader.

**Model Answer (3 marks — Board-Exam Standard):**

> An elevating grader is a self-propelled earthmoving machine that combines a **cutting moldboard** with a **chain-and-flight elevator** to cut, lift, convey, and discharge soil/aggregate in a single pass.
>
> **Four advantages over a motor grader:**
> 1. Can **load trucks directly** via cross conveyor (motor grader cannot).
> 2. Works efficiently in **wet, cohesive soils** (motor grader slips).
> 3. Performs **scarifying + mixing + spreading in one pass** (motor grader needs multiple passes).
> 4. Provides **uniform mixing** of the wearing course material in gravel road maintenance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

> State the **function of the elevator** in an elevating grader. What is the typical range of **elevator chain speed**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Function of the elevator:** It lifts material from the moldboard via **rotating steel flights** mounted on chains and discharges it through a chute into a windrow, truck, or back onto the road surface.
>
> **Typical elevator chain speed:** **0.5 m/s to 1.5 m/s** (or equivalently **30 m/min to 90 m/min**), adjustable via a hydraulic motor drive.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $E_t$** — Most students compute *theoretical* production but lose **2 marks** for not converting to *effective* production. The question almost always asks for **effective** production.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing mm with m without conversion. Always convert **mm to m** before substituting.
> 3. **Confusing bank density with loose density** — When a "swell factor" is given, apply $\rho_b = \rho_{bank}(1+s)$ before using elevator capacity formulas.
> 4. **Omitting the cross-conveyor / discharge chute explanation** — Examiners award 2–3 marks for **complete component description** in descriptive sub-parts.
> 5. **Skipping the scarifier's role** — A common descriptive-question trap worth 2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 3]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the working of an **elevating grader**. Describe the functions of the **moldboard, elevator, and discharge chute** in detail.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An elevating grader has a moldboard width of **2.5 m** and cuts at a depth of **100 mm**. The forward speed of the machine is **1.2 km/hr**. The job efficiency is **0.80**. **Calculate the effective production** in **m³/hr**. If the **swell factor is 0.30**, find the **elevator throughput in tonnes/hr** for a bulk density of 1.8 tonnes/m³ (loose).

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The elevating grader consists of the following major sub-systems (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):

1. **Moldboard (Plough):** A curved steel blade that is hydraulically lowered into the ground at a controlled depth. The cutting edge shears the soil and lifts it up the curved face. The moldboard is set at a cutting angle of **30° to 45°** from the direction of travel. It performs **cutting and partial lifting** of material.

2. **Elevator (Chain-and-Flight Conveyor):** Two parallel chains run over sprockets at the top and bottom of the elevator frame. Steel flights (buckets) are bolted to cross-bars between the chains. As material rides up the moldboard, the flights **scrape it off** and **carry it upward** to the discharge end. The elevator is driven by a **hydraulic motor** with variable speed control (typical range **0.5 to 1.5 m/s**). The elevator performs **lifting, conveying, and dosing** of the cut material.

3. **Discharge Chute / Cross Conveyor:** Located at the top of the elevator, this angled trough or belt redirects the falling material. It can be **swung left, swung right, or centered**. In windrowing mode, the chute deposits material in a long, even pile to one side. In truck-loading mode, it discharges into a **truck moving alongside**. In spreading mode, it drops material back onto the road for blending.

**Working:** The prime mover (diesel engine) drives the rear wheels through a transmission, providing traction. The same engine drives a hydraulic pump, which powers the moldboard lift, wheel lean, and elevator. As the machine advances, the lowered moldboard cuts the soil; the elevator continuously lifts and conveys it; and the chute discharges it to the desired location — all in a **single, continuous pass**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Moldboard width: $W = 2.5 \; \text{m}$
- Cut depth: $D = 100 \; \text{mm} = 0.10 \; \text{m}$
- Forward speed: $V = 1.2 \; \text{km/hr} = 1200 \; \text{m/hr}$
- Job efficiency: $E_t = 0.80$
- Number of passes: $n = 1$ (assumed final pass)
- Swell factor: $s = 0.30$
- Loose bulk density: $\rho_{loose} = 1.8 \; \text{tonnes/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Theoretical production:**

$$Q = \frac{V \cdot W \cdot D}{n} = \frac{1200 \times 2.5 \times 0.10}{1} = 300 \; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 2 — Effective production:**

$$Q_{eff} = Q \cdot E_t = 300 \times 0.80 = 240 \; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Elevator throughput (using loose density directly):**

$$C_e = Q_{eff} \cdot \rho_{loose} = 240 \times 1.8 = 432 \; \text{tonnes/hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{eff} = 240 \; \text{m}^3/\text{hr} \quad ; \quad C_e = 432 \; \text{tonnes/hr}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **elevating grader** with a **conventional motor grader** and an **elevating scraper** under the following heads: **(i) self-loading ability, (ii) material haulage range, (iii) suitability in wet soil, (iv) typical application, and (v) production mode.**
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working of the elevator mechanism** in an elevating grader with reference to **chain speed, flight design, and material discharge**. Derive the **elevator flight capacity equation** from first principles.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Head | Elevating Grader | Motor Grader | Elevating Scraper |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Self-loading | Yes (via elevator) | No (pushes only) | Yes (self-loader) |
| (ii) Material haulage | Short (deposits on adjacent ground) | None (only spreads) | Long (hauls in bowl) |
| (iii) Wet soil suitability | Excellent (designed for moisture) | Poor (wheels slip) | Moderate (bowl gets sticky) |
| (iv) Typical application | Gravel road maintenance, light road construction | Finish grading, snow removal | Bulk earthmoving in dam/road projects |
| (v) Production mode | Continuous, single-pass scarify-mix-spread | Multi-pass finishing | Single load-haul-dump cycle |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **elevator** is the heart of the elevating grader. It is an **inclined chain-and-flight conveyor** that lifts material from the moldboard and discharges it through the chute. Its key parameters are:

- **Chain Speed $v_e$:** Variable, typically **0.5 to 1.5 m/s**, controlled by a hydraulic motor. Faster speeds prevent material from piling up on the moldboard but increase wear and power demand.
- **Flight Design:** Flights are **steel buckets or paddles** bolted to cross-bars between two parallel roller chains. The leading edge is often serrated or tipped with tungsten carbide for **abrasion resistance** in lateritic soils.
- **Material Discharge:** At the top of the elevator, material slides off the flights due to gravity. The discharge chute then **deflects it** into a windrow, truck, or back onto the road. The chute angle is **adjustable from 0° to 45°** to control the landing point.

**Derivation of Elevator Flight Capacity:**

Consider one flight moving at chain speed $v_e$ (m/min). The volume of material it carries per unit length is its cross-sectional area $A_f$ (m²). In one minute, the flight traverses $v_e$ metres, so:

$$V_{1} = v_e \cdot A_f \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min per flight})$$

For the full elevator (with paired flights on two chains, all moving together), the throughput is identical in the **ideal no-slip, no-spillage model**:

$$V_{total} = v_e \cdot A_f \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min})$$

Converting to **tonnes per hour** (multiply by 60 for min→hr, and by $\rho_b$ for volume→mass):

$$\boxed{C_e = 60 \, v_e \, A_f \, \rho_b \;\; [\text{tonnes/hr}]}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Elevating Graders (KTU Module 3)**

- ☐ An elevating grader **cuts, lifts, conveys, and discharges** material in **one continuous pass**.
- ☐ Key components: **moldboard, elevator, discharge chute, scarifier (optional), wheel lean mechanism, operator's platform**.
- ☐ Cutting angle of moldboard: **30° to 45°**; cut depth: **25 mm to 150 mm**.
- ☐ Elevator chain speed range: **0.5 to 1.5 m/s** (≈ **30 to 90 m/min**).
- ☐ **Theoretical Production:** $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot W \cdot D}{n}$
- ☐ **Effective Production:** $Q_{eff} = Q \cdot E_t$ with $E_t = 0.50$ to $0.83$
- ☐ **Elevator Capacity:** $C_e = 60 \, v_e \, A_f \, \rho_b$ in **tonnes/hr**
- ☐ Always apply **efficiency factor** — most common KTU mistake is skipping it.
- ☐ **Convert mm to m** before substituting into formulas.
- ☐ Use **loose bulk density** for elevator throughput; convert from bank density using swell factor: $\rho_{loose} = \rho_{bank}(1+s)$.
- ☐ Elevating grader is **superior to motor grader** in **wet, cohesive soils** (very relevant to Kerala laterite).
- ☐ **Wheel lean** is used for **slope grading**; scarifier is used for **hard, compacted surfaces**.
- ☐ Discharge chute can be set for **windrowing, truck loading, or re-spreading**.
- ☐ KTU 2024 expects: definition, **neat block diagram**, **labeled components**, **at least one numerical** on production or elevator capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Powered Scrapers and Graders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self-Powered Scrapers and Graders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Self-Powered (Self-Propelled) Scrapers

A **self-propelled scraper** is a heavy-duty earthmoving machine that integrates four independent operations — **loading, hauling, spreading, and dumping** — into a single, self-contained unit. It is essentially a tractor unit (or twin tractor units) coupled to a scraper bowl that cuts, scoops, conveys, and deposits soil over distances ranging typically from **300 m to 2,000 m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A self-propelled scraper is a wheel-mounted (or wheel/tractor combination) earthmover that performs excavation, transport, and placement cycles without external pushing assistance, except during the loading phase where a pusher (or "push-pull" arrangement) may assist.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**

Imagine you are digging sand from one corner of a beach to build a small hill at another corner. Normally, you would:
1. Scoop sand with a bucket (excavation),
2. Carry the sand in a wheelbarrow (hauling),
3. Tip the wheelbarrow and spread the sand (spreading/dumping).

A self-propelled scraper performs all three of these actions **continuously and in one machine**, much like a giant, motorized, self-loading wheelbarrow with a built-in digging blade. The "pusher" analogy is like a friend pushing you from behind to help you scoop the sand faster — this is the **pusher tractor** in front of the scraper during loading.

**Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards):**

| Metric | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Bowl Capacity (struck) | **8 – 35 m³** |
| Bowl Capacity (heaped) | **11 – 50 m³** |
| Travel Speed (loaded) | **20 – 55 km/h** |
| Travel Speed (empty) | **40 – 60 km/h** |
| Operating Weight | **20 – 130 tonnes** |

### 1.2 Self-Propelled Graders (Motor Graders)

A **self-propelled grader**, also called a **motor grader**, is a construction machine equipped with a long adjustable blade (moldboard) mounted **between the front and rear axles**, primarily used for **fine grading, leveling, road maintenance, ditching, and snow removal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from a Bulldozer:** In a bulldozer, the blade is at the **front** of the tractor. In a motor grader, the moldboard is in the **center**, which gives superior precision because the blade follows the line of the rear wheels and is not affected by blade loading forces pushing the machine off course.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**

Think of a motor grader like a giant **hand-held paint scraper**, but motorized and mounted on a long, articulated chassis. Just as a painter runs a scraper along a wall to produce a perfectly smooth, even surface, a motor grader drives along a road, with its central blade shaving off high spots and filling low spots. The long frame ensures the blade "sees" the ground profile well in advance and corrects it smoothly, much like a measuring caliper guiding your hand.

**Standard Specifications:**

| Specification | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Engine Power | **100 – 500 hp** |
| Blade Length (moldboard) | **3.7 m – 7.3 m** |
| Operating Weight | **12 – 35 tonnes** |
| Frame Articulation | **±25° (typical)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Self-Propelled Scraper

A self-propelled scraper consists of the following primary sub-systems:

- **Tractor Unit (or Twin Tractors):** Houses the prime mover (diesel engine), operator cab, and drive axles. Twin-engine scrapers have a separate engine at the rear of the scraper bowl for added traction.
- **Scraper Bowl:** The main earth container. It is a steel, open-topped box that can be lowered or raised hydraulically to engage the ground.
- **Cutting Edge / Blade:** A hardened steel plate at the front bottom of the bowl that slices the soil during loading.
- **Apron:** A vertical hydraulically-actuated wall that closes the front of the bowl to retain the loaded material and acts as a partial loading gate.
- **Elevator (Optional):** Found in **elevating scrapers**; uses powered flight chains to assist in loading the bowl, reducing the need for a pusher.
- **Ejection System:** A hydraulically-driven rear wall that pushes the soil out of the bowl during dumping.
- **Draft Frame and Hitch:** Connects the scraper to the tractor and transmits horizontal pulling forces.

### 2.2 Working Cycle of a Scraper

1. **Loading (15–60 seconds):** Bowl is lowered; cutting edge engages soil; apron is partially open; pusher tractor assists.
2. **Hauling (Loaded travel):** Bowl is raised; scraper travels to the fill area at high speed.
3. **Spreading/Dumping (10–30 seconds):** Bowl is lowered to desired thickness; apron is raised; ejector pushes material out in controlled layers.
4. **Returning (Empty travel):** Scraper returns to the cut area at maximum speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Pusher is Required:** Loading consumes the highest amount of power. The tractive effort of a single tractor unit is often insufficient to overcome the rolling resistance + cutting resistance of the soil. A **pusher tractor (or push-pull arrangement)** applies external force at the scraper's rear hitch, allowing the bowl to fill efficiently.

### 2.3 Production Capacity Equation of a Scraper

The KTU board examiners highly value this derivation. The **theoretical production** of a scraper in **loose volume (LCM/hr)** is given by:

$$Q = \frac{V_b \times 3600 \times f \times E}{T_c}$$

Where the variables are described in the formula sheet below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** is the sum of: Loading time + Haul time + Dump time + Return time + Spotting time. In a KTU numerical problem, if haul/return distances differ, their speeds are usually different.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_b$ | Bowl capacity (heaped) | m³ | Use **heaped** unless specified |
| $T_c$ | Total cycle time | seconds | Sum of all phases |
| $f$ | Fill factor | dimensionless | **0.5 – 0.9** depending on material |
| $E$ | Efficiency (job / operator) | dimensionless | **0.75 – 0.85** (KTU uses 0.80 as default) |
| $R$ | Rolling resistance | N | $= W \times R_f$ |
| $R_f$ | Rolling resistance factor | dimensionless | **0.02** (smooth soil) to **0.10** (soft, sandy) |
| $W$ | Total machine weight | N | Use **total loaded weight** for haul, empty for return |
| $F_T$ | Tractive effort required | N | $= R + Grade \; resistance$ |
| $P$ | Power required | kW | $= F_T \times V \; / \; 1000$ |
| $V$ | Travel speed | m/s | Convert km/h by multiplying 1000/3600 |
| $S$ | Shrinkage factor | dimensionless | $S = \frac{LL - CL}{CL}$ where LL = Loose, CL = Compacted |
| $CL$ | Compacted volume | m³ | $CL = LL \times (1 - S)$ |
| $\sigma$ | Soil swell | % | $\sigma = \frac{LL - BL}{BL} \times 100$, BL = Bank volume |

> [!TIP]
> **Caveat on Volume Conversions:** Scrapers are rated in **heaped (loose) cubic metres**. When the KTU question gives a target embankment volume in **compacted cubic metres**, you **MUST** apply the shrinkage factor to convert to loose volume before dividing by bowl capacity.

### 2.5 Anatomy of a Motor Grader

A motor grader has the following major components:

- **Main Frame (Long Wheelbase Chassis):** Allows the front and rear axles to be far apart for stability.
- **Moldboard (Main Blade):** The curved cutting blade mounted in the center. It can be rotated 360° around its vertical axis, tilted forward/backward, and shifted sideways.
- **Drawbar and Circle:** The circle is a rotating ring that allows the moldboard to be slewed (rotated) without moving the chassis.
- **Blade Controls (Hydraulic):** Lift, tilt, shift, and rotate functions operated from the cab.
- **Scarifier / Ripper (Rear-mounted):** Used to break up hard surfaces before grading.
- **Articulated Frame:** Allows the front and rear sections to pivot, reducing turning radius.
- **Front Dozer Blade (Optional):** Sometimes fitted for snow plowing or light pushing.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Scrapers are indispensable in **highway construction** (cut-to-fill operations), **airport runway** construction, **dam embankments**, and **large irrigation canal** projects.
- Motor graders are the **finishing machines** — they prepare the final subgrade before paving, perform **routine maintenance of unpaved haul roads**, and execute **snow removal** in cold-climate regions like Northern Europe, Canada, and high-altitude KTU-relevant Indian hill regions (Kashmir, Sikkim, Himachal Pradesh border projects).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scraper Production Rate (Q)

Let us derive the production rate of a self-propelled scraper from first principles.

**Step 1: Define production rate.**
Production rate is the volume of material moved per unit time. For a scraper, the useful volume per cycle is the bowl capacity multiplied by the fill factor:

$$V_{useful} = V_b \times f$$

**Step 2: Define cycle time.**
Cycle time $T_c$ is the total time for one complete load-haul-dump-return cycle, in seconds.

**Step 3: Calculate cycles per hour.**
Number of cycles per hour is given by dividing total seconds in an hour by the cycle time:

$$n = \frac{3600}{T_c}$$

**Step 4: Apply efficiency factor.**
Operator and job conditions reduce effective production. Multiplying by efficiency E:

$$n_{eff} = \frac{3600 \times E}{T_c}$$

**Step 5: Combine.**
Multiplying useful volume per cycle by effective cycles per hour:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= V_{useful} \times n_{eff} \\
Q &= \frac{V_b \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard KTU scraper production formula**. Units: **m³ (loose) per hour**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tractive Effort Required by a Scraper

The scraper must overcome both **rolling resistance** and **grade resistance** during hauling.

**Step 1: Rolling resistance.**
Rolling resistance is the force required to overcome tire deformation and ground deformation:

$$R = W \times R_f$$

**Step 2: Grade resistance.**
Grade resistance is the component of gravity acting along the slope. For a slope of $\theta$ degrees (or grade percentage $g$%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= W \times \sin\theta \\
&\approx W \times \frac{g}{100} \quad \text{(for small angles, } g = \tan\theta \approx \sin\theta \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total tractive effort.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_T &= R + G \\
F_T &= W \times \left( R_f + \frac{g}{100} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Power required.**
Power is force multiplied by velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_T \times V \\
P_{kW} &= \frac{F_T \times V}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V$ is in m/s and $F_T$ is in N. If $V$ is in km/h, the formula becomes:

$$P_{kW} = \frac{F_T \times V_{km/h}}{3600}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A self-propelled scraper has a heaped bowl capacity of 22 m³. The haul distance is 800 m (one way) and the return is along the same route. The scraper travels at 32 km/h loaded and 48 km/h empty. Loading takes 50 s, dumping takes 20 s, and spotting time is 15 s. The fill factor is 0.85 and job efficiency is 0.80 (a standard 45 minute hour). Calculate the **theoretical production in loose m³/hr**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate haul time.

$$T_{haul} = \frac{D}{V} = \frac{800}{32 \times \frac{1000}{3600}} = \frac{800}{8.888} = 90 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate return time.

$$T_{return} = \frac{800}{48 \times \frac{1000}{3600}} = \frac{800}{13.333} = 60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the cycle time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 50 + 90 + 20 + 60 + 15 \\
T_c &= 235 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the production formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{V_b \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c} \\
Q &= \frac{22 \times 0.85 \times 3600 \times 0.80}{235} \\
Q &= \frac{53856}{235} \\
Q &= 229.17 \text{ loose m}^3 / \text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The scraper's theoretical production is approximately **229.17 m³/hr (loose)**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Scraper vs Motor Grader (Comparison Table)

This is a **high-frequency KTU 14-mark question** in Module 3.

| Parameter | Self-Propelled Scraper | Motor Grader |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Function | Excavate + Haul + Spread | Fine grading, leveling, finishing |
| Blade Location | Front-bottom of bowl | Center, between front & rear axles |
| Typical Capacity | **8 – 50 m³** (heaped) | Blade length **3.7 – 7.3 m** |
| Cycle Operation | Cyclic (load-haul-dump-return) | Continuous (linear passes) |
| Auxiliary Equipment | Pusher tractor, elevator | Scarifier, ripper, dozer blade |
| Speed Range | **20 – 60 km/h** | **0 – 40 km/h** |
| Optimum Haul Distance | **300 m – 2 km** | Short distance, repetitive passes |
| Ground Pressure | High (requires firm soil) | Lower (rubber tires) |
| Power Source | 1 or 2 diesel engines | Single diesel engine |
| Major Application | Highway cut-fill, dam embankment | Road maintenance, snow removal |
| Tractive Effort Source | Engine + pusher (optional) | Engine only |
| Bucket/Bowl Hydraulics | Lift, apron, ejector | Lift, tilt, shift, rotate (circle) |
| Volumetric Productivity | Measured in **m³/hr** | Measured in **m²/hr (area covered)** |

### 3.5 Derivation 3 — Motor Grader Area Coverage

Unlike scrapers, motor graders are rated by **area covered per hour** since they work in thin layers.

$$A = \frac{L_b \times V \times 3600 \times E}{N_p}$$

where $L_b$ is the effective blade length (m), $V$ is travel speed (m/s), $E$ is efficiency, and $N_p$ is the number of passes required (typically 2–4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often test whether students confuse **scraper productivity (volumetric)** with **grader productivity (areal)**. This is one of the most common pitfalls in the Module 3 paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Self-Propelled Scraper Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Diesel Engine Tractor Unit] --> B[Hydraulic Transmission]
    B --> C[Rear Drive Axle]
    B --> D[Front Steerable Axle]
    A --> E[Operator Cab with Joystick Controls]
    A --> F[Rear Hitch and Draft Frame]
    F --> G[Scraper Bowl Assembly]
    G --> H[Cutting Edge Blade]
    G --> I[Hydraulic Apron Gate]
    G --> J[Hydraulic Ejector Wall]
    G --> K[Optional Elevator Flight Chain]
    P[Pusher Tractor] -.->|During Loading Only| F
    H --> H1[Cuts Soil]
    I --> I1[Closes Front of Bowl]
    J --> J1[Pushes Material Out During Dumping]
    K --> K1[Assists Loading Without Pusher]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Motor Grader Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENG[Front Engine Bay] --> TRANS[Transmission and Torque Converter]
    TRANS --> RA[Rear Tandem Drive Axles]
    TRANS --> FA[Front Steerable Axle]
    CHAS[Long Articulated Chassis] --> FRAME[Front Frame Section]
    CHAS --> RFRAME[Rear Frame Section]
    FRAME --> CIRC[Rotating Drawbar Circle]
    CIRC --> BLADE[Central Moldboard Blade]
    BLADE --> LIFT[Hydraulic Lift Cylinder]
    BLADE --> TILT[Hydraulic Tilt Cylinder]
    BLADE --> SHIFT[Hydraulic Side Shift Cylinder]
    RFRAME --> SCAR[Rear Mounted Scarifier and Ripper]
    BLADE --> SNOW[Optional Front Dozer or Snow Plow]
    CAB[Operator Cab with Multi Lever Controls] --> LIFT
    CAB --> TILT
    CAB --> SHIFT
    CAB --> CIRC
```

### 4.3 Operational State Diagram — Scraper Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Load
    Load: Load Bowl with Pusher
    Load --> Haul
    Haul: Travel Loaded to Fill Site
    Haul --> Spread
    Spread: Lower Bowl and Eject Material
    Spread --> Return
    Return: Travel Empty to Cut Site
    Return --> Load
    Load --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Operational State Diagram — Grader Working Pattern

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Position
    Position: Position Machine at Start of Pass
    Position --> Cut1
    Cut1: First Cut Pass Right Side
    Cut1 --> Cut2
    Cut2: Second Cut Pass Left Side
    Cut2 --> Finish
    Finish: Final Smoothing Pass
    Finish --> Advance
    Advance: Move Forward One Blade Width
    Advance --> Cut1
    Finish --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are Mermaid block-level functional architecture flows. They abstract the physical appearance to emphasize component interactions, control pathways, and operational sequencing — the level of detail most relevant to a KTU Module 3 answer script.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a self-propelled scraper. State any four applications.*

**Model Answer:**
A self-propelled scraper is a self-contained wheel-mounted earthmoving machine designed to **scrape, load, haul, spread, and dump** soil in cyclic operation without needing a separate excavator or dumper. It consists of a tractor unit and a scraper bowl with a cutting edge, hydraulic apron, and ejector.

**Four applications:**
1. Highway cut-to-fill earthwork operations.
2. Construction of dam embankments and large irrigation canals.
3. Airport runway earthwork.
4. Large-scale land leveling for industrial parks and townships.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the main components of a motor grader with their functions.*

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Moldboard (central blade) | Cuts, spreads, and mixes the surface material |
| Drawbar circle | Rotates the moldboard for angle adjustment |
| Scarifier/Ripper | Breaks up hard soil before grading |
| Articulated frame | Reduces turning radius, improves maneuverability |
| Hydraulic blade controls | Allow lift, tilt, shift, and rotate operations |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> KTU ESE Module 3 — Choose **ONE** of the following:

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **working principle of a self-propelled scraper** and explain the function of the **pusher tractor** during loading. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A self-propelled scraper with a heaped bowl capacity of **18 m³** is used in a highway project. The fill factor is **0.85**, job efficiency is **0.80**, and the total cycle time is **210 seconds**. Determine the **theoretical production in loose m³/hr**. If the target embankment requires **1,00,000 compacted m³** and the shrinkage factor is **12%**, estimate the **number of hours** required to complete the embankment using this scraper. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The self-propelled scraper operates in four phases:

**1. Loading Phase:**
The tractor unit positions the scraper at the cut area. The bowl is lowered until the cutting edge engages the soil. The pusher tractor comes from behind and pushes the scraper forward, providing the tractive effort needed to fill the bowl. Simultaneously, the apron is partially raised to allow material to enter. Loading typically takes 40–60 seconds.

**2. Hauling Phase:**
Once the bowl is full, the apron is closed, the bowl is raised, and the scraper travels at high speed (20–55 km/h) to the fill or dump site. The pusher tractor detaches and moves to assist the next scraper.

**3. Spreading/Dumping Phase:**
At the fill area, the bowl is partially lowered to a pre-set thickness (typically 100–300 mm). The ejector wall pushes the soil out from the rear while the apron is lifted, allowing uniform layer spreading.

**4. Return Phase:**
The empty scraper returns at maximum speed to the cut area for the next cycle.

**Function of the Pusher Tractor:**
- Provides **supplementary tractive effort** during loading when single-engine tractive force is insufficient.
- **Reduces loading time** by 30–50%, increasing production.
- In **push-pull scrapers**, the pusher is also a scraper; they alternately push each other, eliminating the need for a dedicated pusher.

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute production rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{V_b \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c} \\
Q &= \frac{18 \times 0.85 \times 3600 \times 0.80}{210} \\
Q &= \frac{44064}{210} \\
Q &= 209.83 \text{ loose m}^3 / \text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert target compacted volume to loose volume.
Shrinkage $S = 12\% = 0.12$. Using the formula $LL = \dfrac{CL}{1 - S}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{loose} &= \frac{1,00,000}{1 - 0.12} \\
V_{loose} &= \frac{1,00,000}{0.88} \\
V_{loose} &= 1,13,636.36 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute total hours required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{V_{loose}}{Q} \\
T &= \frac{1,13,636.36}{209.83} \\
T &= 541.66 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Production rate = **209.83 m³/hr (loose)**. Total time required = **541.66 hours** (≈ 22.6 days at 24-hour operation, or 67.7 days at 8-hour shifts).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question A(b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert compacted to loose volume.** Many students divide 1,00,000 directly by 209.83 and get an incorrect answer. KTU examiners will deduct 3 marks for this.
> 2. **Using struck capacity instead of heaped capacity** if the question explicitly mentions "heaped."
> 3. **Unit errors in cycle time** — ensure $T_c$ is in seconds when 3600 is in the numerator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **working of a self-propelled grader (motor grader)** with a neat block diagram. Highlight **why the moldboard is positioned between the front and rear axcles** and not at the front like a bulldozer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **self-propelled scrapers and motor graders** with respect to: (i) primary function, (ii) blade location and control, (iii) cycle vs continuous operation, (iv) productivity measurement, (v) optimum operating distance, (vi) typical application, and (vii) tractive effort source. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Working of a Motor Grader:**

A motor grader operates by driving along a surface with its central moldboard lowered to a preset cutting depth. The sequence of operations is:

1. The machine approaches the area to be graded at low speed.
2. The moldboard is lowered, tilted, and rotated (typically 30°–45° from the direction of travel) using hydraulic controls.
3. The rotating action **shears and casts** material sideways while the machine moves forward.
