4. For rough grading, a rear-mounted **scarifier** breaks up hardpan or compacted soil first; the moldboard then spreads the loosened material.
5. Successive overlapping passes at slightly different angles produce a **smooth, precisely graded surface**.

**Why the Moldboard is at the Center (not at the front like a bulldozer):**

- **Stability and precision:** A centrally mounted blade is supported by both front and rear axles, so it does not pitch the machine forward under load.
- **Reaction force management:** Cutting forces push the blade backward into the chassis, neutralizing the effect rather than deflecting the machine off course.
- **Predictable line:** The blade follows the path of the rear wheels, which are not affected by blade loading — this is essential for **fine grading to millimeter tolerances**.
- **Articulation:** The long wheelbase allows the operator to see the cutting edge clearly and make fine adjustments.

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

Refer to the **Analytical Matrix table in Section 3.4** above. The seven comparison points are tabulated there. In a KTU answer script, present the comparison as a **two-column table** with bold headers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> 1. Do not write more than 5–6 lines per comparison point — KTU expects a **table format**, not paragraphs.
> 2. For point (iv), explicitly state that scraper productivity is in **m³/hr (volumetric)** while grader productivity is in **m²/hr (areal)**. Conflating these is a common loss of 2 marks.
> 3. For point (v), emphasize that scrapers are economical at **300 m – 2 km** hauls, while graders operate on **short, repetitive passes**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- **Scraper** = integrated load + haul + dump machine; rated in **heaped m³**.
- **Motor Grader** = finishing machine; moldboard is **central**, not frontal.
- **Production formula:** $Q = \dfrac{V_b \times f \times 3600 \times E}{T_c}$ — memorize in this exact form.
- **Always convert compacted volume → loose volume** using $LL = CL / (1 - S)$ before applying the formula.
- **Rolling resistance** = $W \times R_f$; **Grade resistance** ≈ $W \times (g/100)$ where $g$ is grade percentage.
- **Tractive effort** $F_T = R + G$; **Power** $P = F_T \times V$.
- **Scraper productivity is volumetric (m³/hr)**, **grader productivity is areal (m²/hr)**.
- **Twin-engine scrapers** offer better traction; **elevating scrapers** reduce pusher dependency.
- **Articulated frame** in motor grader reduces turning radius to as low as **7 m**.
- **Pusher tractor** improves scraper loading by 30–50%; **push-pull** configuration eliminates dedicated pusher.
- **Scarifier/ripper** in motor grader breaks hardpan **before** the moldboard cuts.
- **Fill factor ($f$)** depends on material: sand ≈ 0.80–0.90, clay ≈ 0.65–0.75, rock ≈ 0.50–0.60.
- **Job efficiency ($E$)** for KTU problems: default is **0.80 (45-min hour)** unless specified otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Road Rollers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Road Rollers

# ROAD ROLLERS — A Special Type of Construction Vehicle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus (PEMUT523):** Road Rollers fall under the category of *Special Types of Vehicles* used extensively in earthwork, road construction, dam construction, and agricultural land preparation. The 2024 scheme emphasizes types, working principles, applications, and selection criteria.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Road Roller** is a compactor-type engineering vehicle used to compact soil, gravel, concrete, or asphalt in the construction of roads, foundations, railway embankments, and large earth-fill dams. It accomplishes compaction by means of its own static weight (dead weight), or by applying dynamic forces through vibrating drums, or a combination of both.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Worthy):**
> A road roller is a self-propelled or towed compactive machine employing one or more heavy steel drums (or pneumatic tyres) which transmit pressure to the surface being compacted, thereby reducing the air-void ratio and increasing the density of the underlying material.

### Key Terminology (Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Compaction** | The process of mechanically increasing the density of soil by reducing air voids. |
| **Drum** | The cylindrical steel rolling element of the roller that applies pressure. |
| **Static Roller** | Compacts purely by virtue of its own dead weight. |
| **Dynamic Roller** | Applies an additional impulsive force (vibration or tamping). |
| **Centrifugal Force** | The radial force generated by an eccentric rotating mass in a vibratory drum. |
| **Amplitude** | The peak vertical displacement of the vibrating drum during a cycle. |
| **Frequency** | The number of vibration cycles per second, measured in **Hz (Hertz)** or **vpm (vibrations per minute)**. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real-World Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine rolling a heavy steel cylinder over a fresh cake of wet sand on a beach.** The sand beneath the cylinder becomes visibly harder and more compact, while loose sand around it remains soft. This is precisely the principle of a road roller — apply a heavy, concentrated load over a small contact area, repeatedly, to squeeze out air voids and bind particles tightly together.

Now scale this beach cylinder to **8–20 tonnes** of steel, mount it on a powered chassis, and you have a road roller — the *workhorse of every highway, airport runway, and dam project.*

## 1.3 Why Road Rollers Are Studied in PEMUT523

Road Rollers belong to the **Special Types of Vehicles** category because:

1. They have **non-standard drive configurations** (often hydrostatic or rear-wheel drive only).
2. They use **vibratory mechanisms** uncommon in regular automobiles.
3. Their **load distribution and ground-pressure design** is purpose-built.
4. They form a critical link between **automotive engineering** and **earthmoving machinery**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Road Rollers

Road rollers can be classified on the basis of:
- **Method of compaction** (static or vibratory)
- **Surface of the drum** (smooth, sheepsfoot, grid, pneumatic)
- **Number of drums** (single, tandem, three-wheel)
- **Mode of propulsion** (self-propelled or towed)

### 2.1.1 Classification by Method of Compaction

**(a) Static Road Rollers** — Compact purely by **dead-weight loading**.
- The compactive effort is directly proportional to the weight of the drum and the contact pressure.
- Best suited for thin lifts of granular soils and finishing asphalt layers.

**(b) Vibratory Road Rollers** — Add a **dynamic (centrifugal) force** to the static weight.
- An eccentric shaft inside the drum rotates at high speed, generating an alternating force vector.
- The combined *static + dynamic* force produces much higher compaction efficiency in fewer passes.

**(c) Pneumatic-Tyred Rollers** — Use a row of rubber tyres (typically 7–13 wheels) to apply a **kneading action**.
- Excellent for sealing surfaces and compacting cohesive soils.

**(d) Tamping-Foot / Sheepsfoot Rollers** — The drum is studded with club-shaped or "sheep's foot" projections.
- Best for **compacting cohesive (clayey) soils** in thick lifts because the feet penetrate and break clay lumps.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Drum Configuration

| Configuration | Description | Typical Use |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single-Drum (or Three-Wheel)** | One large steel drum at front, rubber tyres at rear. | Earth fills, subgrades. |
| **Tandem Roller** | Two steel drums (front and rear). | Asphalt compaction, road surfaces. |
| **Double-Drum (Vibratory)** | Two vibrating drums. | Highway asphalt layers. |
| **Pneumatic Multi-Wheel** | 7–13 rubber tyres in tandem. | Seal coats, clayey soils. |
| **Sheepsfoot / Padfoot** | Drum studded with tapered feet. | Clayey embankments, dams. |

## 2.2 Components of a Typical Road Roller

1. **Frame & Chassis** — Welded steel construction, supports all components.
2. **Drum(s)** — Hollow steel cylinders, ballasted with water, sand, or wet sand to vary weight.
3. **Vibration Mechanism** (in vibratory rollers) — Eccentric shaft driven by hydraulic motor.
4. **Engine** — Typically a **diesel engine**, 30–150 kW depending on size.
5. **Transmission** — Hydrostatic (most modern) or torque-converter with powershift.
6. **Steering System** — Articulated frame steering (modern) or rear-wheel steering.
7. **Scraper Blades** — Clean mud from the drum surface to prevent slippage.
8. **Water Spray System** — Wets the drum to prevent asphalt pickup.
9. **Operator Cabin** — ROPS/FOPS certified for safety.

## 2.3 Working Principle — How a Vibratory Roller Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The most commonly asked question in PEMUT523 is *"Explain the working principle of a vibratory road roller."* Master this section.

**Step 1 — Static Loading:**
The weight of the drum $W$ (ballasted) presses on the ground, generating a static contact pressure $p_s$.

**Step 2 — Generation of Centrifugal Force:**
An eccentric mass $m_e$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ produces a centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_e$$

where $r_e$ is the eccentricity (distance of mass from rotation axis).

**Step 3 — Total Compactive Force:**
The drum is now pressed against the ground with a **total force**:

$$F_{total} = W + F_c$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Stress Wave Propagation:**
The vibration creates a stress wave that propagates into the soil. The amplitude and frequency must be tuned to the natural frequency of the soil particles for resonance — this is the secret of high efficiency.

**Step 5 — Directional Vibration Modes:**
- **Vertical vibration** (most common) → soil compaction.
- **Tangential vibration** → propulsion assist.
- **Combined vertical + horizontal** → high-frequency shakers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force | $F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_e$ | **N** | Core formula for vibratory rollers. |
| 2 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | **rad/s** | $f$ in Hz. |
| 3 | Frequency (vpm) | $N = 60 \cdot f$ | **vibrations/min** | Often quoted as vpm in catalogues. |
| 4 | Total Compactive Force | $F_{total} = W + F_c$ | **N** | Static + dynamic. |
| 5 | Static Linear Load | $LL_s = W / L$ | **kg/cm** | $L$ = drum width. |
| 6 | Dynamic Linear Load | $LL_d = F_c / L$ | **kg/cm** | Comparison metric. |
| 7 | Ground Contact Pressure | $p = F_{total} / A$ | **kPa** | $A$ = contact patch area. |
| 8 | Area of Contact (cylindrical drum) | $A = L \cdot b$ | **m²** | $b$ = contact width. |
| 9 | Compactive Energy per Pass | $E_p = F_{total} \cdot d$ | **J** | $d$ = depth of effective compaction. |
| 10 | Roller Productivity | $Q = \frac{v \cdot W_e \cdot h}{n}$ | **m³/h** | $v$ = speed, $W_e$ = effective width, $h$ = layer thickness, $n$ = passes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize formulas **1, 2, 3, 4, and 10** — these alone can fetch you **7 out of 14 marks** in any numerical sub-question.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Highways & Expressways:** Vibratory tandem rollers achieve 95–100% Modified Proctor density.
- **Airport Runways:** Heavy three-wheel rollers compact the sub-base to support aircraft loads of **~400 kN per wheel**.
- **Earthen Dams:** Sheepsfoot rollers compact clay cores in 20–30 cm thick lifts.
- **Land Reclamation:** Pneumatic-tyred rollers seal reclaimed land surfaces.
- **Agricultural Use (under PEMUT523 scope):** Compaction of farm roads, irrigation channel beds, and silage pit floors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Centrifugal Force in a Vibratory Drum

> **Objective:** Derive the expression for the centrifugal force generated by an eccentric rotating mass inside a vibratory roller drum.

### Statement
A mass $m_e$ is fixed at a radial distance $r_e$ (the eccentricity) from the axis of rotation. The shaft rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega$. Determine the centrifugal force experienced by the drum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the position vector of the eccentric mass.**
At any instant $t$, the eccentric mass is located at:

$$x(t) = r_e \cos(\omega t)$$
$$y(t) = r_e \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute velocity by differentiation.**

$$\dot{x}(t) = -r_e \omega \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\dot{y}(t) = r_e \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the speed (magnitude of velocity).**

$$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} = \sqrt{r_e^2 \omega^2 \sin^2(\omega t) + r_e^2 \omega^2 \cos^2(\omega t)} = r_e \omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the centripetal acceleration.**
Since the mass moves in a circle of radius $r_e$ at constant speed $v = r_e \omega$:

$$a_c = \frac{v^2}{r_e} = \frac{(r_e \omega)^2}{r_e} = r_e \omega^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law to obtain centrifugal force.**
By Newton's third law, the reaction on the drum is the centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m_e \cdot a_c = m_e \cdot r_e \cdot \omega^2$$

$$\boxed{F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_e \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$

> **Conversion Note (Textual Logic):**
> The term "centrifugal" refers to the reaction force (in the non-inertial rotating frame) that the eccentric mass exerts on the drum. In the inertial frame, this is simply the *centripetal force* required to keep the mass in circular motion, transmitted through the shaft to the drum housing.

## 3.2 Conversion Between Hz and vpm

Many KTU problems quote roller frequency in **vibrations per minute (vpm)**. The conversion to Hz is:

$$f \text{ (Hz)} = \frac{N \text{ (vpm)}}{60}$$

and reverse:

$$N = 60 \cdot f$$

For a typical compaction frequency range of **25–50 Hz**:

$$N_{min} = 60 \times 25 = 1500 \text{ vpm}$$
$$N_{max} = 60 \times 50 = 3000 \text{ vpm}$$

These align with industry-standard vibratory rollers.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

> **Problem (14-mark style, part a):**
> A vibratory road roller has an eccentric mass of **$m_e = 12$ kg** rotating at an eccentricity of **$r_e = 0.18$ m**. The vibration frequency is **30 Hz**. The static weight of the drum is **$W = 80$ kN**. Calculate:
> (i) The centrifugal force $F_c$.
> (ii) The total compactive force $F_{total}$.
> (iii) The frequency in vpm.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert frequency to angular velocity.**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 30 = 188.496 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the centrifugal force.**

$$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_e$$
$$F_c = 12 \times (188.496)^2 \times 0.18$$
$$F_c = 12 \times 35530.7 \times 0.18$$
$$\boxed{F_c = 76746 \text{ N} \approx 76.75 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total compactive force.**

$$F_{total} = W + F_c = 80 + 76.75 = \boxed{156.75 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert frequency to vpm.**

$$N = 60 \times f = 60 \times 30 = \boxed{1800 \text{ vpm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The vibration mechanism contributes nearly **as much force as the static weight** — this is precisely why vibratory rollers achieve superior compaction in fewer passes.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Roller Types

| Feature | Smooth Static | Smooth Vibratory | Sheepsfoot | Pneumatic-Tyred |
|---------|---------------|------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Compaction Mechanism** | Static weight | Static + centrifugal | Static + kneading | Kneading + static |
| **Best Soil Type** | Granular, asphalt | Granular, mixed | Cohesive (clay) | Cohesive + sealed surfaces |
| **Lift Thickness** | 10–20 cm | 30–50 cm | 20–40 cm | 15–25 cm |
| **Achievable Density** | 90–95% Proctor | 95–100% Proctor | 92–97% Proctor | 93–98% Proctor |
| **Typical Weight** | 8–15 t | 10–20 t | 12–25 t | 8–30 t |
| **Speed of Operation** | 2–5 km/h | 1.5–3 km/h | 3–8 km/h | 4–10 km/h |
| **Water Spray Needed?** | Yes (asphalt) | Yes | No | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `dr1`, `vi1`). No reserved keywords used. No markdown formatting inside node labels.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vibratory Road Roller

```mermaid
graph TD
    eng[ENG1 Diesel Engine] --> trans[TR1 Hydrostatic Transmission]
    trans --> rearAxle[RA1 Rear Axle and Tyres]
    trans --> hydPump[HP1 Hydraulic Pump]
    hydPump --> vibMotor[VM1 Vibration Motor]
    vibMotor --> eccShaft[ES1 Eccentric Shaft]
    eccShaft --> drum[DR1 Vibrating Drum]
    drum --> ground[GR1 Ground Contact Patch]
    ground --> soilForce[SF1 Soil Reaction Force]
    drum --> scraper[SC1 Scraper Blade]
    waterTank[WT1 Water Tank] --> spray[SW1 Spray Nozzles]
    spray --> drum
    cabin[CB1 Operator Cabin with Controls] --> trans
    cabin --> vibMotor
    frame[FR1 Articulated Frame] --> drum
    frame --> rearAxle
    steer[ST1 Steering Hydraulic Cylinder] --> frame
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The diesel engine drives both the hydrostatic transmission (for propulsion) and a hydraulic pump that powers the vibration motor. The vibration motor spins the eccentric shaft, which in turn vibrates the drum. The drum contacts the ground, and the soil reaction force combined with centrifugal force compacts the material.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Compaction Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[STEP1 Roller Positioned at Start of Lane] --> stepB[STEP2 Lower Drum and Engage Vibration]
    stepB --> stepC[STEP3 Roller Moves Forward at Operating Speed]
    stepC --> stepD[STEP4 Drum Applies Static plus Centrifugal Force]
    stepD --> stepE[STEP5 Soil Particles Rearrange and Densify]
    stepE --> stepF[STEP6 Roller Reaches End of Pass]
    stepF --> stepG[STEP7 Roller Returns in Adjacent Lane]
    stepG --> stepH[STEP8 Density Check after N Passes]
    stepH --> stepI{Density Achieved?}
    stepI -->|Yes| stepJ[STEP9 Compaction Complete]
    stepI -->|No| stepB
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree — Road Roller Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[ROLLER Types]
    root --> r1[By Drive]
    root --> r2[By Compaction Method]
    root --> r3[By Drum Surface]
    r1 --> r1a[Self Propelled]
    r1 --> r1b[Towed]
    r2 --> r2a[Static]
    r2 --> r2b[Vibratory]
    r2 --> r2c[Impact Tamping]
    r3 --> r3a[Smooth Wheel]
    r3 --> r3b[Sheepsfoot]
    r3 --> r3c[Pneumatic Tyred]
    r3 --> r3d[Grid Roller]
    r2a --> r2a1[Three Wheel Static]
    r2a --> r2a2[Tandem Static]
    r2b --> r2b1[Single Drum Vibratory]
    r2b --> r2b2[Double Drum Vibratory]
    r2b --> r2b3[Padfoot Vibratory]
```

## 4.4 Drum Construction Detail (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1[SHELL1 Outer Cylindrical Shell] --> s2[BEARING1 Heavy Duty Bearings]
    s2 --> s3[SHAFT1 Main Drum Shaft]
    s3 --> s4[ECC1 Eccentric Weights Adjustable]
    s4 --> s5[HYD1 Hydraulic Vibration Motor Coupling]
    s5 --> s6[BRACKET1 Mounting Bracket to Frame]
    s7[BALLAST1 Water or Sand Ballast] --> s1
    s8[SCRAPER1 Scraper Bar] --> s1
```

> **KTU Diagram Note:** In the examination, you can sketch a simple cross-section showing the drum as a hollow cylinder with an internal eccentric weight mounted on a shaft, with bearings at both ends. Label: (1) Drum shell, (2) Eccentric weight, (3) Drive shaft, (4) Bearing, (5) Ballast port, (6) Scraper.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse frequency (Hz) with angular velocity (rad/s).** Use $\omega = 2\pi f$ correctly.
> 2. **Always convert vpm to Hz** (divide by 60) before computing $\omega$.
> 3. **Do not omit units** in the final answer — examiners deduct 1 mark for unit-less numerics.
> 4. **In classification questions**, mention *both* the category (by method) AND give an example for full 3 marks.
> 5. **For sheepsfoot rollers**, always justify *why* they are suited for clay (penetration + kneading). A statement without justification loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
**Define a road roller and list any four types of road rollers.**

**Model Answer:**
A road roller is a self-propelled or towed compactor used to densify soil, gravel, asphalt, or concrete by means of static weight, dynamic vibration, or kneading action of its drums/wheels.

**Four Types:**
1. Smooth-wheel static roller
2. Vibratory tandem roller
3. Sheepsfoot / padfoot roller
4. Pneumatic-tyred roller

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for the four types.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**State the formula for the centrifugal force generated in a vibratory roller drum and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_e$$

Where:
- $F_c$ = centrifugal force (N)
- $m_e$ = eccentric mass (kg)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the eccentric shaft (rad/s)
- $r_e$ = eccentricity — radial distance of the mass from the rotation axis (m)

**Significance:** The centrifugal force adds to the static weight of the drum, producing a total compactive force that pulverizes soil particles and increases density in fewer passes. *(3 marks: 1.5 for formula + 1.5 for term explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module-Level Internal Choice:** KTU 2024 scheme provides a choice within each module. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design + Working**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q7]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **vibratory tandem road roller**. Discuss the role of the eccentric shaft, drum ballasting, and the water spray system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vibratory roller has an eccentric mass of **$m_e = 15$ kg** with eccentricity **$r_e = 0.20$ m**, rotating at **$f = 28$ Hz**. The static weight on the drum is **$W = 90$ kN**.
   (i) Calculate the centrifugal force developed.
   (ii) Determine the total compactive force.
   (iii) Express the frequency in vibrations per minute (vpm).
   (iv) If the drum width is **$L = 1.7$ m** and the rolling speed is **$v = 2.5$ km/h**, calculate the productivity for **$n = 6$ passes** and layer thickness **$h = 0.30$ m**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

> **Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a side view showing: engine, hydrostatic pump, hydraulic motor, eccentric shaft, vibrating drum, scraper, water tank, articulated frame, operator cabin. (Use a clean labelled diagram — not a Mermaid render on paper.)

> **Description (5 marks):**

A vibratory tandem road roller consists of two smooth steel drums (front and rear), each driven by a hydrostatic motor. The chassis is articulated for tight turning radius.

**Constructional Features:**
1. **Diesel Engine** — typically a 4-cylinder, water-cooled, turbocharged diesel producing **75–120 kW**.
2. **Hydrostatic Transmission** — variable-displacement pump driving fixed-displacement motors for infinite speed control.
3. **Vibration System** — A hydraulic motor inside each drum spins an eccentric shaft. By adjusting the angle of two eccentric weights (from 0° to 180° between them), the operator varies the resultant centrifugal force from **zero (weights opposed)** to **maximum (weights aligned)**.
4. **Drum Ballasting** — Drums are hollow and can be filled with water (density 1000 kg/m³), sand (1600 kg/m³), or wet sand (1800 kg/m³) to vary the static weight.
5. **Water Spray System** — Pressurized water from a tank (capacity 600–1500 L) is sprayed on the drum surface to prevent asphalt sticking and to cool the drum.
6. **Scraper Blade** — A spring-loaded blade scrapes mud/asphalt off the drum to maintain friction.

**Working Principle:**
- The roller is positioned and the drums lowered.
- Vibration is engaged; the eccentric weights spin, generating centrifugal force $F_c = m_e \omega^2 r_e$.
- The combined *static + dynamic* force compacts the asphalt layer in 4–6 passes.
- The operator can switch between two amplitudes (high for thick layers, low for thin layers) by repositioning the eccentric weights.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Centrifugal Force:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 28 = 175.93 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_e$$
$$F_c = 15 \times (175.93)^2 \times 0.20$$
$$F_c = 15 \times 30951.4 \times 0.20$$
$$\boxed{F_c = 92,854 \text{ N} \approx 92.85 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Total Compactive Force:**

$$F_{total} = W + F_c = 90 + 92.85 = \boxed{182.85 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iii) Frequency in vpm:**

$$N = 60 \times f = 60 \times 28 = \boxed{1680 \text{ vpm}}$$

**(iv) Productivity:**

First, convert speed to consistent units. $v = 2.5$ km/h $= 2500/3600 = 0.6944$ m/s.
Effective width $W_e = L = 1.7$ m.
Layer thickness $h = 0.30$ m.
Number of passes $n = 6$.

$$Q = \frac{v \cdot W_e \cdot h}{n} = \frac{0.6944 \times 1.7 \times 0.30}{6}$$

$$Q = \frac{0.3542}{6} = 0.05903 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$Q = 0.05903 \times 3600 = \boxed{212.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

> **Examiner's Note:** The 7 marks for part (b) are typically distributed as: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 1 mark, (iii) 1 mark, (iv) 3 marks. Show every step to avoid mark deductions.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q8]**

**(a)** Classify road rollers in detail, giving a comparison of **sheepsfoot rollers** and **pneumatic-tyred rollers** with respect to soil suitability, compaction mechanism, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the term **"Modified Proctor Density"** in the context of roller compaction. A three-wheel static roller has a front drum of diameter **1.5 m**, width **1.3 m**, and the rear wheels have a contact pressure of **550 kPa** each. The total weight of the roller is **$W = 120$ kN**. Calculate:
   (i) The static linear load on the front drum.
   (ii) The average ground contact pressure under the drum, assuming full-width contact and contact angle **30°** (arc subtended).
   *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Classification and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Classification of Road Rollers (4 marks):**

| Basis | Categories |
|-------|-----------|
| By propulsion | Self-propelled, Towed |
| By compaction method | Static, Vibratory, Impact (tamping) |
| By drum surface | Smooth, Sheepsfoot/Padfoot, Pneumatic-tyred, Grid |
| By number of drums | Single-drum, Tandem (double-drum), Three-wheel |

**Comparison: Sheepsfoot vs Pneumatic-Tyred (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sheepsfoot Roller | Pneumatic-Tyred Roller |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Soil Suitability** | Cohesive soils (clay, silt) — the feet penetrate and break lumps. | Both cohesive and granular; ideal for sealing surface layers. |
| **Compaction Mechanism** | High localised contact pressure from foot tips; kneading action. | Uniform kneading and slight impact; contact pressure adjustable by tyre inflation. |
| **Typical Applications** | Earth dam cores, clay embankments, subgrade in clayey terrain. | Seal coats, reclaimed land, base course, asphalt rolling. |
| **Lift Thickness** | 25–40 cm | 15–25 cm |
| **Foot Pressure** | 1400–7000 kPa (very high) | 200–700 kPa (lower and uniform) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Concept: Modified Proctor Density (1 mark for explanation):**
Modified Proctor density is the maximum dry density of a soil achieved under a specific laboratory compaction test (ASTM D1557) using **2700 kN-m/m³** of compactive energy (5 layers, 56 blows per layer, 4.9 kg hammer, 457 mm drop). On site, rollers must achieve **95–100% of Modified Proctor density** to certify quality.

**Calculations:**

**(i) Static Linear Load on Front Drum:**

The weight is distributed between the front drum and rear wheels. For a three-wheel roller, typically **50% of total weight** is on the front drum:

$$W_{drum} = 0.5 \times 120 = 60 \text{ kN}$$

Linear load:

$$LL_s = \frac{W_{drum}}{L} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ N}}{1.3 \text{ m}} = \boxed{46{,}154 \text{ N/m} \approx 46.15 \text{ kN/m}}$$

**(ii) Average Ground Contact Pressure:**

For a cylindrical drum, the contact patch is approximately a segment of the drum. With contact angle $\theta = 30° = \pi/6$ rad:

$$b = R \cdot \theta$$

where $R = 1.5/2 = 0.75$ m, so:

$$b = 0.75 \times \frac{\pi}{6} = 0.75 \times 0.5236 = 0.3927 \text{ m}$$

Contact area:

$$A = L \times b = 1.3 \times 0.3927 = 0.5105 \text{ m}^2$$

Contact pressure:

$$p = \frac{W_{drum}}{A} = \frac{60{,}000}{0.5105} = \boxed{117{,}535 \text{ Pa} \approx 117.5 \text{ kPa}}$$

> **Examiner's Note:** Mark distribution for Q.B(b) — (i) 3 marks, (ii) 3 marks, conceptual explanation 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-This-Before-Exam Checklist:**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] A **road roller** is a compactor that densifies soil/asphalt by static weight, dynamic vibration, or kneading.
- [ ] The **four main types** are: Smooth Static, Vibratory, Sheepsfoot, Pneumatic-Tyred.
- [ ] **Vibratory rollers** add a **centrifugal force** $F_c = m_e \omega^2 r_e$ to the static weight.
- [ ] **Sheepsfoot rollers** are best for **clayey (cohesive) soils**; pneumatic-tyred for **seal coats**.
- [ ] **Tandem rollers** (two drums) are standard for **asphalt compaction**.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas
- [ ] $F_c = m_e \omega^2 r_e$ — **Centrifugal Force**
- [ ] $\omega = 2\pi f$ — **Angular Velocity**
- [ ] $N = 60 f$ — **vpm Conversion**
- [ ] $F_{total} = W + F_c$ — **Total Compactive Force**
- [ ] $Q = \frac{v W_e h}{n}$ — **Roller Productivity**

### ✅ Conversion Facts
- [ ] 1 Hz = 60 vpm
- [ ] Typical roller frequency: **25–50 Hz** (= 1500–3000 vpm)
- [ ] 1 kN/m linear load $\approx$ 100 kg/m
- [ ] Modified Proctor field requirement: **≥ 95%** of lab maximum dry density.

### ✅ Component Recall
- [ ] **Eccentric shaft** → generates vibration
- [ ] **Scraper blade** → cleans drum
- [ ] **Water spray** → prevents asphalt pickup
- [ ] **Ballast** → varies weight (water, sand, wet-sand)
- [ ] **Articulated frame** → for tight turning
- [ ] **Hydrostatic transmission** → infinite speed control

### ✅ Application Mapping (High-Yield for MCQs)
- [ ] Highway asphalt → **Vibratory Tandem**
- [ ] Earthen dam core → **Sheepsfoot**
- [ ] Sub-base course → **Three-Wheel Static**
- [ ] Seal coat → **Pneumatic-Tyred**
- [ ] Airport runway base → **Heavy Three-Wheel**

### ✅ Examiner Pet Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to convert Hz → rad/s before squaring.
- [ ] ❌ Quoting vpm but treating it as Hz.
- [ ] ❌ Omitting units.
- [ ] ❌ Stating "sheepsfoot for all soils" — it's specifically for **cohesive** soils.
- [ ] ❌ Skipping the sketch in long-answer questions (2-mark deduction).

> **Final Word (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In the 2024 NEP-aligned OBE pattern, examiners expect *outcome-based* answers. Whenever you describe a roller type, **always close with its application** (e.g., *"…hence it is preferred for clayey embankments in dam construction"*) — this signals **Course Outcome CO3: Application** and earns the higher Bloom's level marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity of Shovels, Power Shovel, Revolving and Stripper Shovels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacity of Shovels, Power Shovel, Revolving and Stripper Shovels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capacity of Shovels — Formal Academic Definition

In the context of earthmoving machinery, the **capacity of a shovel** refers to the **volumetric holding capacity of its bucket (dipper) under specified loading conditions**. It is the primary design parameter that determines the production rate, cycle time, and the economic viability of any excavating equipment. Capacity is generally expressed in three standard ways used by KTU examiners and the industry:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Three Standard Definitions of Capacity**
> 1. **Struck Capacity (Vs):** The geometric volume contained within the bucket outline, measured as a level fill at the bucket lip, with no heaping. It is the *plain water level* volume.
> 2. **Heaped Capacity (Vh):** The volume of material held in the bucket when it is piled above the top edge at the natural angle of repose of the material.
> 3. **Rated Capacity (Vr):** The manufacturer's published operational capacity, typically **between 85 % and 110 %** of the heaped capacity, as recommended by standards bodies such as SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) and CECE (Committee for European Construction Equipment).

The general governing relationship for any open-top bucket is:

$$V_h = V_s \left(1 + k \cdot \tan\alpha\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **angle of repose** of the excavated material and $k$ is a geometric constant of the bucket (typically 0.5 to 0.7 depending on bucket width and length).

## 1.2 The Capacity Equation — Why It Matters

Capacity is the *first* number an estimator, contractor, or KTU board examiner looks at. It directly drives:

- **Theoretical production rate** (m³/hr or yd³/hr)
- **Truck-loader matching** (number of haul trucks per shovel)
- **Fuel consumption** and **hourly owning-and-operating cost**
- **Site logistics** including bench height, cut width, and swing angle

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine you are filling a **rectangular sand-pit toy** with sand using a small cup. The "struck capacity" is the volume of sand when you scrape the top of the cup perfectly flat with a ruler. The "heaped capacity" is when you let the sand pile up into a small mountain above the rim of the cup — it can hold *more* than its geometric volume, just like a shovel bucket. Now imagine the cup is your excavator's bucket, the sand is the earth at the construction site, and the rate at which you can fill, swing, and dump the cup is the **cycle time**. The capacity tells you *how much*; the cycle time tells you *how fast*. Together, they determine production.

## 1.3 Power Shovel — Formal Definition

A **Power Shovel** is a self-propelled, crawler- or wheel-mounted, hydraulic or cable-operated excavating machine equipped with a bucket (dipper) that *digs into the working face by pulling the bucket toward the machine* (i.e., the material is **pulled into the bucket** from below the machine's track level). The primary function is **excavation above the track or wheel level** — the machine sits on the bench and digs the face in front of it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"A power shovel is a heavy-duty, mounted, self-propelled digging machine that uses a rigid bucket to excavate material from a face located above the level of the machine. The bucket is filled by being pulled toward the machine, lifted by a hoist line, and discharged into a hauling unit positioned alongside."*

## 1.4 Revolving Shovel — Formal Definition

A **Revolving Shovel** is a power shovel whose **upper works (house, boom, stick, and bucket) can rotate through a full 360°** about a vertical axis on the undercarriage. The modern hydraulic excavator is the direct evolutionary descendant of the cable-operated revolving shovel. It combines the digging action of a power shovel with the swing freedom of a crane, allowing it to load haul trucks on either side, dig trenches perpendicular to its travel, and operate in confined urban sites.

## 1.5 Stripper Shovel — Formal Definition

A **Stripper Shovel** (also called a *mining shovel* or *stripping shovel*) is an **extra-large-capacity revolving shovel** specifically engineered for **open-pit surface mining**, where its primary job is to **strip the overburden** (waste rock and topsoil) that lies above a coal, iron, or copper ore seam. Stripper shovels are characterized by:

- **Bucket capacities from 10 m³ to 80 m³** (some ultra-class machines exceed 100 m³)
- **Boom lengths of 30 m to 60 m** for reaching the top of high benches
- **Walking mechanisms** (instead of crawlers) on the largest units to distribute the enormous ground-bearing pressure
- **Diesel-electric or AC-drive powertrains** in the megawatt class

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of a **power shovel** as a person standing at the edge of a swimming pool, scooping water with a bucket from above. A **revolving shovel** is the same person, but now mounted on a spinning turntable so they can throw the water in any direction. A **stripper shovel** is a **giant industrial version** of that spinning person, used at open-pit mines — bucket so big that a small car could fit inside it, designed to peel off thousands of tonnes of overburden every hour.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting Capacity of a Shovel Bucket

The capacity of a shovel bucket is **not a fixed number** — it is heavily influenced by site conditions, material properties, and operator skill. The following structured list captures every factor a KTU board examiner expects a student to enumerate:

### A. Geometric / Design Factors
1. **Bucket size and shape** — rectangular, elliptical, or spade-nosed.
2. **Bucket opening (lip width and bucket depth)** — wider opening → easier fill.
3. **Number and shape of teeth** — straight or rock teeth, penetration aids.
4. **Bucket bottom angle** — affects penetration and material flow.

### B. Material Properties
1. **Angle of repose ($\alpha$)** — sandy soil ≈ 30°, moist clay ≈ 45°, blasted rock ≈ 35–40°.
2. **Swell factor (S)** — ratio of bank volume to loose volume.
3. **Moisture content** — sticky wet clay reduces effective capacity.
4. **Material density ($\rho$)** — rock loads heavier than topsoil for the same volume.
5. **Fragmentation** — well-blasted rock flows into the bucket easily.

### C. Operational Factors
1. **Depth of cut / bench height** — optimum depth is typically **30 % to 50 %** of boom length for full bucket fill.
2. **Swing angle ($\theta$)** — typical values 45° (load beside), 90° (90° load), 180° (rear dump).
3. **Operator skill and training.**
4. **Hydraulic pressure / hoist line speed** for cable machines.
5. **Job efficiency (E)** — accounts for breaks, delays, positioning.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — it covers 90 % of KTU Part B numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) | Typical Boundary Values |
|---|---------|-------------|------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $V_h = V_s(1 + k\tan\alpha)$ | Heaped capacity from struck capacity | m³ | $k \in [0.4, 0.7]$ |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{V_{loose}}{V_{bank}}$ | Swell factor (always > 1) | – | Sand ≈ 1.10, Rock ≈ 1.30–1.50 |
| 3 | $P_t = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C \cdot B \cdot E \cdot D}{T}$ | Theoretical production per hour (mass basis) | tonnes/hr | – |
| 4 | $P_v = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C \cdot B \cdot E}{T}$ | Volumetric production per hour | m³/hr (loose) | – |
| 5 | $T = t_d + t_s + t_u + t_r$ | Total cycle time | seconds (s) | Cable: 20–30 s; Hydraulic: 12–20 s |
| 6 | $t_s = k_s \cdot \theta$ | Swing time proportional to swing angle | s | $k_s \approx 0.10$ s/deg (typical) |
| 7 | $V_r = (0.85 \text{ to } 1.10)\,V_h$ | Rated (SAE) bucket capacity | m³ | – |
| 8 | $F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a$ | Total digging resistance | N | Resisting force on bucket lip |
| 9 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta}$ | Required power for digging | kW | $\eta \approx 0.80$ (hydraulic) |
| 10 | $W = C \cdot \rho \cdot g$ | Weight of one bucket load | N | – |

**Where:**
- $C$ = effective bucket capacity (m³)
- $B$ = number of buckets per truck / pass
- $E$ = job efficiency (decimal, e.g. 0.83 for 50 min/hr)
- $D$ = bank density of material (tonnes/m³)
- $T$ = cycle time (s)
- $t_d$ = digging time, $t_s$ = swing time, $t_u$ = dump time, $t_r$ = return swing time
- $\theta$ = swing angle in degrees

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by mining engineers and contractors for **production estimating, equipment selection, and cost bidding**. A 1 % error in assumed cycle time or fill factor can shift a tender price by lakhs of rupees on a 6-month project.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Material is dug:** Force $F_t$ overcomes resistance. The bucket is pulled through the bank; the teeth fragment the material.
- **Step 2 — Bucket is lifted:** Hoist rope or hydraulic cylinder lifts the loaded bucket clear of the face. Time $t_d$.
- **Step 3 — Upper works swing to the haul unit:** Rotation about the vertical axis. Time $t_s$ depends on angle $\theta$.
- **Step 4 — Bucket is dumped:** Door opens or bucket rotates. Time $t_u$ ≈ 1.5–3 s.
- **Step 5 — Bucket returns empty:** Reverse swing to dig position. Time $t_r$.

The **sum** is the cycle time $T$, and the **reciprocal** multiplied by capacity gives production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heaped Capacity Formula

The heaped capacity of an open-top rectangular bucket can be derived geometrically by treating the heaped material as a **prism with a triangular cross-section** sitting on top of the struck (rectangular) volume.

### Step 1 — Define the bucket geometry
Let the bucket have:
- Length (along the floor) $= L$ (m)
- Width (across the lip) $= W$ (m)
- Height (from floor to lip) $= H$ (m)
- Angle of repose of the material $= \alpha$ (degrees)

The **struck volume** is the simple rectangular block:

$$V_s = L \times W \times H$$

### Step 2 — Approximate the heaped pile as a triangular prism
Above the lip, the piled material forms a long, low ridge. A common engineering approximation is to model this heaped portion as a **triangular prism** whose base equals the width of the bucket, and whose height rises until the slope equals the angle of repose $\alpha$.

For a typical bucket where the heaped pile runs the full length $L$ of the bucket, the cross-section of the heap is a triangle with:
- Base $= W$
- Height $= \dfrac{W}{2}\tan\alpha$

So the heaped (extra) volume is:

$$V_{heap} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{W}{2}\tan\alpha\right) \cdot L = \dfrac{W^2 L}{4}\tan\alpha$$

### Step 3 — Express the heaped volume in terms of $V_s$

Since $V_s = L \cdot W \cdot H$, we can write $W \cdot L = V_s / H$. Substituting:

$$V_{heap} = \dfrac{W}{4H} \cdot V_s \cdot \tan\alpha = k \cdot V_s \cdot \tan\alpha$$

where $k = \dfrac{W}{4H}$ is a **dimensionless bucket-shape constant** (typically 0.4 to 0.7).

### Step 4 — Add to obtain total heaped capacity

$$\begin{aligned}
V_h &= V_s + V_{heap} \\
V_h &= V_s + k \cdot V_s \cdot \tan\alpha \\
V_h &= V_s\left(1 + k \cdot \tan\alpha\right)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact form used in KTU board examinations. For very large mining buckets with elliptical cross-sections, $k$ is calibrated empirically against SAE J67 / J1363 test data, but the algebraic form remains the same.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Production Rate Formula

The **theoretical production rate** of a shovel is the *mass* (or *loose volume*) of material it moves per unit time, under a given job efficiency.

### Step 1 — Define the bucket load mass
For one bucket pass, the mass of material is:

$$m = C \cdot \rho \cdot k_f$$

where $C$ is the heaped/rated bucket capacity (m³), $\rho$ is the bank density (kg/m³ or t/m³), and $k_f$ is the **bucket fill factor** (decimal, 0 to 1).

### Step 2 — Number of cycles per hour
Each cycle takes $T$ seconds. In 3600 seconds (1 hour), the number of cycles is:

$$N = \dfrac{3600}{T}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the job efficiency
Real machines do not run continuously — operators take breaks, the machine is repositioned, and minor delays occur. The **job efficiency** $E$ (a decimal, e.g. 0.83 = 50 min/hour) accounts for this:

$$N_{eff} = E \cdot \dfrac{3600}{T}$$

### Step 4 — Total production per hour

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= m \cdot N_{eff} \\
P &= C \cdot \rho \cdot k_f \cdot E \cdot \dfrac{3600}{T}
\end{aligned}$$

Combining $k_f$ with $C$ (or simply using effective $C$) and denoting bank density $D$:

$$\boxed{P = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C \cdot B \cdot E \cdot D}{T}}$$

where $B$ is included if multiple buckets are loaded per haul unit per pass.

> [!NOTE]
> **If $B$ is omitted, $P$ is the hourly production in m³/hr (loose). If $D$ is included, $P$ is in tonnes/hr (bank). Always specify the units in your KTU answer.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Values (for KTU Numerical Practice)

| Material | Angle of Repose $\alpha$ | Swell $S$ | Bank Density $D$ (t/m³) | Fill Factor $k_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sand (dry) | 30° | 1.10 | 1.60 | 0.95 |
| Sand (moist) | 35° | 1.12 | 1.70 | 0.90 |
| Common earth | 37° | 1.25 | 1.80 | 0.85 |
| Clay (dry) | 40° | 1.30 | 1.85 | 0.80 |
| Clay (wet, sticky) | 45° | 1.35 | 2.00 | 0.65 |
| Well-blasted rock | 35° | 1.45 | 2.40 | 0.75 |
| Poorly-blasted rock | 38° | 1.60 | 2.60 | 0.55 |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> **Problem:** A hydraulic power shovel has a struck bucket capacity of 1.5 m³. The angle of repose of the material is 37°, and the bucket-shape constant is 0.55. The cycle time is 18 seconds, job efficiency is 80 %, and the material has a bank density of 1.8 t/m³. Find: (a) Heaped capacity, (b) Hourly production in bank cubic metres per hour, and (c) Hourly production in tonnes per hour.

### Solution

**(a) Heaped capacity:**

$$V_h = V_s (1 + k \tan\alpha) = 1.5 \times (1 + 0.55 \times \tan 37°)$$

Computing $\tan 37° \approx 0.7536$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_h &= 1.5 \times (1 + 0.55 \times 0.7536) \\
V_h &= 1.5 \times (1 + 0.4145) \\
V_h &= 1.5 \times 1.4145 \\
V_h &= 2.122 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Hourly production in bank m³/hr:**

First, account for swell. Loose volume = bank volume × swell. Equivalently, we use $C$ as bank m³ per cycle. The fill factor for common earth ≈ 0.85, so effective bank capacity = $0.85 \times 2.122 = 1.804$ m³.

$$P_v = \dfrac{3600 \times C_{eff} \times E}{T} = \dfrac{3600 \times 1.804 \times 0.80}{18} = 288.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (bank)}$$

**(c) Hourly production in tonnes/hr:**

$$P_t = P_v \times D = 288.6 \times 1.8 = 519.5 \text{ t/hr}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Power Shovel / Hydraulic Excavator

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a power shovel and the three main families — power shovel, revolving shovel, and stripper shovel — and how they relate to one another in the earthmoving-equipment hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Prime Mover Diesel Engine or Electric Motor] --> B[Power Transmission System]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Pumps and Valves]
    B --> D[Mechanical Gearbox for Track Drive]
    C --> E[Boom Cylinder]
    C --> F[Stick Cylinder also called Dipper Cylinder]
    C --> G[Bucket Cylinder]
    C --> H[Swing Motor and Slewing Ring]
    D --> I[Undercarriage Crawlers or Wheels]
    E --> J[Boom Assembly]
    F --> K[Stick or Dipper Arm]
    G --> L[Bucket with Teeth]
    J --> M[Working Attachment End Effector]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Digging Action}
    N --> O[Power Shovel Digs from Above]
    N --> P[Revolving Shovel 360 Degree Swing]
    N --> Q[Stripper Shovel Mega Scale Mining]
    H --> R[Swing Function 90 to 180 degree typical]
    I --> S[Walking Mechanism for Stripper Shovel]
    S --> Q
    style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Comparison of the Three Shovel Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Power Shovel] --> P1A[Fixed or Limited Swing often front only]
    P1 --> P1B[Smaller Capacity 0.5 to 4 m3]
    P1 --> P1C[Tracks or Wheels]
    P1 --> P1D[General Construction and Quarry Use]

    P2[Revolving Shovel] --> P2A[Full 360 Degree Rotation]
    P2 --> P2B[Medium Capacity 1 to 10 m3]
    P2 --> P2C[Hydraulic or Cable Operated]
    P2 --> P2D[Urban Construction and Trenching]

    P3[Stripper Shovel] --> P3A[Full 360 Degree plus Walking]
    P3 --> P3B[Ultra Large Capacity 10 to 100 m3]
    P3 --> P3C[Electric Drive often AC]
    P3 --> P3D[Open Pit Mining and Overburden Removal]

    style P1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Cycle-Time Architecture for a Shovel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Cycle Bucket Empty] --> B[Digging Phase Pull Bucket into Face]
    B --> C[Hoisting Phase Lift Bucket Clear of Bench]
    C --> D[Swing Loaded Rotate to Truck]
    D --> E[Dumping Phase Empty into Haul Unit]
    E --> F[Return Swing Rotate to Dig Face]
    F --> A
    B -.Time td.-> C
    C -.Time th.-> D
    D -.Time ts.-> E
    E -.Time tu.-> F
    F -.Time tr.-> A
    style A fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **cycle time** $T = t_d + t_h + t_s + t_u + t_r$. For KTU board problems, $t_s$ and $t_r$ are typically lumped together as the *swing time* and given as a function of swing angle $\theta$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define the terms: (i) Struck capacity, (ii) Heaped capacity, and (iii) Rated capacity of a shovel bucket. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Struck capacity (Vs):** The volume of material held in the bucket when the contents are levelled flush with the top edge of the bucket. It is the geometric volume of the bucket interior — sometimes called the *water-level* capacity.

**(ii) Heaped capacity (Vh):** The volume of material held in the bucket when the contents are piled above the top edge at the natural angle of repose of the material. It is greater than the struck capacity.

**(iii) Rated capacity (Vr):** The manufacturer's published operational capacity, recommended by SAE standards, typically equal to **85 % to 110 %** of the heaped capacity. It is the figure used in production estimating.

**Relationship:**

$$V_s < V_r \leq 1.10 \, V_h \quad \text{and} \quad V_h = V_s (1 + k \tan\alpha)$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**List the main factors that affect the capacity of a power shovel.**

**Model Answer:**

The capacity of a power shovel is affected by:
1. **Bucket geometry** — size, shape, number of teeth, lip design.
2. **Material properties** — angle of repose, density, moisture, fragmentation.
3. **Depth of cut / bench height** — should be 30–50 % of boom length.
4. **Swing angle** to the haul unit (45°, 90°, 180°).
5. **Operator skill and machine condition.**
6. **Job efficiency** — accounting for delays and repositioning.
7. **Hydraulic pressure or hoist-line tension** (affects fill).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the heaped capacity and the struck capacity of an open-top shovel bucket. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cable-operated power shovel with a 2.0 m³ struck bucket is excavating moist common earth. The angle of repose is 35° and the bucket-shape constant is 0.50. The average cycle time is 24 s, and the job efficiency is 50 min/hr. Calculate (i) the heaped capacity, (ii) the loose production in m³/hr, and (iii) the bank production in tonnes/hr if the bank density is 1.85 t/m³ and the swell factor is 1.25. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The bucket is rectangular and the heap forms a triangular-prism ridge along the full bucket length.
2. The slope of the heap equals the natural angle of repose $\alpha$ of the material.
3. The material is homogeneous and free-flowing.

**Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

Let $L$ = bucket length, $W$ = bucket width, $H$ = bucket height.

$$V_s = L \cdot W \cdot H$$

Heaped volume = struck volume + triangular-prism heap:

$$V_{heap} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{W}{2}\tan\alpha\right) \cdot L = \dfrac{W^2 L}{4}\tan\alpha$$

Substituting $W \cdot L = V_s / H$:

$$V_{heap} = k \cdot V_s \cdot \tan\alpha, \quad \text{where } k = \dfrac{W}{4H}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_h = V_s \left(1 + k \tan\alpha\right)}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $V_s = 2.0 \text{ m}^3$, $\alpha = 35°$, $k = 0.50$, $T = 24$ s, $E = 50/60 = 0.833$, $D = 1.85$ t/m³, $S = 1.25$.

**(i) Heaped capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_h &= 2.0 \times (1 + 0.50 \times \tan 35°) \\
\tan 35° &\approx 0.7002 \\
V_h &= 2.0 \times (1 + 0.50 \times 0.7002) \\
V_h &= 2.0 \times (1 + 0.3501) \\
V_h &= 2.0 \times 1.3501 \\
V_h &= 2.70 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Loose production in m³/hr:**

For common earth, fill factor $k_f \approx 0.85$. Effective heaped capacity = $0.85 \times 2.70 = 2.295$ m³.

$$P_v = \dfrac{3600 \times C_{eff} \times E}{T} = \dfrac{3600 \times 2.295 \times 0.833}{24} = \dfrac{6883.4}{24} \approx 286.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (loose)}$$

**(iii) Bank production in tonnes/hr:**

Convert loose m³ to bank m³ using swell $S = 1.25$:

$$P_{v,bank} = \dfrac{P_v}{S} = \dfrac{286.8}{1.25} = 229.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (bank)}$$

Mass production:

$$P_t = 229.4 \times 1.85 = 424.4 \text{ t/hr}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **revolving power shovel**. Compare it with a **stripper shovel** in terms of capacity, application, and walking mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mining company operates a fleet of stripper shovels with a heaped bucket capacity of 30 m³. The bucket fill factor is 0.85, the cycle time is 40 s, and the job efficiency is 45 min/hr. The overburden density is 2.0 t/m³ (bank). Calculate the daily production in tonnes if the shovel operates for 20 hours per day. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Construction of a Revolving Power Shovel:**

A revolving power shovel consists of:
- **Undercarriage** — crawler tracks or rubber tyres for mobility.
- **Swing platform (house)** — rotates 360° on a large diameter slewing ring mounted on the undercarriage.
- **Power plant** — diesel engine or electric motor housed in the upper structure.
- **Operator cab** — at the front-left of the house for visibility.
- **Boom** — pivots at the front of the house.
- **Stick (dipper arm)** — pivots at the end of the boom.
- **Bucket** — pivots at the end of the stick; equipped with teeth.
- **Hydraulic cylinders or cables** — actuate the boom, stick, bucket, and swing.

**Working Cycle:**
1. Bucket is positioned at the dig face.
2. **Digging** — bucket is pulled into the face by the crowd cylinder (hydraulic) or by the stick-and-hoist geometry (cable).
3. **Hoisting** — boom is raised by the hoist cylinder to clear the bench.
4. **Swing** — upper house rotates to the haul unit.
5. **Dumping** — bucket door opens or bucket rotates to discharge.
6. **Return swing** — house rotates back to the dig position.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Revolving Shovel | Stripper Shovel |
|---|---|---|
| Bucket capacity | 1 – 10 m³ | 10 – 100 m³ |
| Application | General construction, quarrying | Open-pit mining, overburden stripping |
| Mobility | Crawlers / wheels | Walking mechanism (pads and shoes) |
| Power | Diesel-hydraulic | Diesel-electric or AC drive |
| Boom length | Short to medium | Very long (30–60 m) |
| Cycle time | 12 – 25 s | 30 – 60 s |

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $C = 30$ m³, $k_f = 0.85$, $T = 40$ s, $E = 45/60 = 0.75$, $D = 2.0$ t/m³ (bank), operating hours = 20 hr/day.

**Effective bucket capacity:**

$$C_{eff} = 0.85 \times 30 = 25.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle (heaped)}$$

**Cycles per hour:**

$$N = \dfrac{3600 \times E}{T} = \dfrac{3600 \times 0.75}{40} = 67.5 \text{ cycles/hr}$$

**Hourly production (loose m³):**

$$P_v = 25.5 \times 67.5 = 1721.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (loose)}$$

**Daily production (loose m³):**

$$P_{day,loose} = 1721.25 \times 20 = 34{,}425 \text{ m}^3/\text{day (loose)}$$

**Convert to bank m³ using swell** (assume swell of blasted overburden $S = 1.40$):

$$P_{day,bank} = \dfrac{34{,}425}{1.40} = 24{,}589.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day (bank)}$$

**Daily production in tonnes:**

$$P_{t,day} = 24{,}589.3 \times 2.0 = 49{,}178.6 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams**
> 1. **Forgetting the fill factor $k_f$** — always multiply the heaped capacity by the appropriate $k_f$ for the material, otherwise the examiner deducts **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing loose and bank units** — clearly state whether your answer is in *loose* or *bank* m³/hr, and apply the swell factor $S$ only **once** in the correct direction.
> 3. **Using degrees vs. radians** — $\tan 35°$ expects degrees, but if you accidentally pass radians to the calculator, your answer will be off by an order of magnitude.
> 4. **Cycle-time components** — the cycle time $T$ is the **sum** of dig + hoist + swing + dump + return. Some students only include 2 or 3 components and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Job efficiency $E$** — expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., 0.83 for 50 min/hr), not as a percentage in the formula.
> 6. **Bucket-shape constant $k$** — must be between 0.4 and 0.7; if the question gives a value outside this range, double-check.
> 7. **Stripper-shovel question pitfalls** — these usually require conversion of bank m³ to tonnes; forgetting the density $D$ costs 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Capacity** has three forms: **Struck ($V_s$), Heaped ($V_h$), Rated ($V_r$)**.
- ✅ The master equation is $V_h = V_s (1 + k \tan\alpha)$.
- ✅ **Production rate** $= 3600 \cdot C \cdot B \cdot E \cdot D / T$.
- ✅ **Cycle time** $= t_d + t_s + t_u + t_r$ (or dig + hoist + swing + dump + return).
- ✅ **Job efficiency $E$** is a decimal between 0 and 1, never a percentage inside the formula.
- ✅ **Swell $S > 1$** — always; loose volume is always greater than bank volume.
- ✅ **Power shovel** = digs from above, pulls bucket toward machine, smaller capacity (0.5–4 m³).
- ✅ **Revolving shovel** = full 360° rotation, medium capacity (1–10 m³), most common modern excavator.
- ✅ **Stripper shovel** = 10–100 m³, walking mechanism, used in surface mining to remove overburden.
- ✅ **Angle of repose** $\alpha$ is the angle the heaped material makes with the horizontal — sandy 30°, clay 40–45°, blasted rock 35–40°.
- ✅ **Bucket fill factor $k_f$** accounts for incomplete filling — sticky/wet materials have low $k_f$ (0.55–0.65), dry granular have high $k_f$ (0.90–0.95).
- ✅ **SAE rated capacity** is between 85 % and 110 % of the heaped capacity.
- ✅ Always **draw a neat labelled sketch** of the power shovel components for full-mark answers.
- ✅ For numerical answers, **show every step** — KTU examiners award step-marks liberally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draglines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT523 - TRACTORS, FARM EQUIPMENTS AND SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Draglines # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **dragline** is a heavy-duty, cable-operated excavating machine in which a long lattice **boom** supports a **bucket** suspended on wire ropes. Unlike a shovel or backhoe that uses a rigid arm, the dragline bucket is **dragged** toward the machine by a tensioned cable, while a separate **hoist cable** raises it. This cable-based geometry gives the dragline an exceptionally long working radius and the unique ability to excavate material lying **below the level of its own base**, making it indispensable in surface mining, large-scale civil earthworks, and canal excavation. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4** > Draglines fall under the category of *Special Types of Construction & Earthmoving Equipment*. The syllabus specifically emphasizes their **working principle, components, applications, and selection criteria** for open-cast mining and large earthmoving projects. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of a Dragline as a Giant Fisherman Casting a Net** > - The **boom** is the fisherman's rod — a long, slender, angled structure. > - The **hoist cable** is the fishing line that raises/lowers the bucket. > - The **drag cable** is the hand line — it pulls the bucket (net) toward the operator through the water (soil). > - The **bucket** is the net itself, scooping material as it is dragged inward. > - The **swing mechanism** is the fisherman's waist rotation, sweeping the load sideways to unload at a different location. > - The **walking mechanism** (in large draglines) is like wading through a stream — taking measured "steps" to a new casting spot. This analogy immediately conveys the *reach* advantage of the dragline: just as a long fishing rod lets a fisherman cast far out into deep water, a long boom lets a dragline excavate far away and deep below its own base — places other excavators cannot reach. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - Standard bucket capacities for **mining draglines**: **15 m³ to 130 m³** (with ultra-class machines up to 170 m³). - Boom lengths range from **45 m to 100 m** for large walking draglines. - Operational weight: **600 tonnes to 13,000 tonnes** (ultra-class). - Power source: Electric motors driven by on-site substations (AC), or diesel-electric drives in smaller units. - Standard cycle time: **30 to 60 seconds** per bucket load. > [!NOTE] > **Why a Dragline and Not a Power Shovel?** > When the digging face is **below the machine's own level** (e.g., a deep pit or canal), a power shovel cannot reach. The dragline's suspended bucket solves this — it is essentially a **gravity-fed, cable-suspended excavation system**. --- # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Functional Anatomy — Step-by-Step Logic The dragline is built around **seven major functional systems**, each with a clear purpose: ### (A) The Boom (Lattice Girder) - A triangular or tubular **lattice structure**, usually tapered, made of high-strength steel. - Mounted on a **gantry/A-frame** at an inclined angle (typically 20° to 45° with horizontal). - **Why a lattice?** Lattice gives high strength-to-weight ratio, reducing dead load on the hoisting drums. - For ultra-class draglines, the boom is **over 90 m long** and is assembled in sections. ### (B) The Bucket - A heavy, scoop-shaped vessel with **replaceable cutting teeth** at the lip. - Available in two main types: - **Light-duty bucket** — for loose/soft material (sand, coal, topsoil). - **Heavy-duty bucket** — for blasted rock, with reinforced wear plates. - Bucket is **rigged** (suspended) by three cables: hoist, drag, and dump. ### (C) Wire Rope Cables (Hoist, Drag, Dump) | Cable | Function | Direction of Force | |---|---|---| | **Hoist cable** | Raises the loaded bucket | Vertical / Upward | | **Drag cable** | Pulls the bucket through the material toward the machine | Horizontal / Inward | | **Dump cable** | Releases the bucket bottom to unload the material | Releases a latch | ### (D) The Machinery House (House) - Contains all **driving machinery**: hoist drums, drag drums, swing motors, operator's cabin, and electrical switchgear. - Counterweight is mounted at the rear to balance the load on the boom. ### (E) The Swing System - A ring-gear mounted on the **tub** (revolving frame). - Multiple **swing motors** (typically 2 to 8) drive pinion gears that engage the ring gear. - Enables 360° rotation under load. ### (F) The Walking Mechanism (for walking draglines) - A **triangular base** with three large "shoes" (or pontoons). - The machine "walks" by **lifting alternate shoes** using hydraulic or rope-operated cams, and shifting the center of gravity — a remarkably efficient non-continuous motion. ### (G) The Undercarriage - **Crawler-mounted draglines** use multi-tracked crawlers for mobility on rough terrain. - **Walking draglines** use the shoes/pontoon system. ## 2.2 Operational Cycle — The "Why" Behind Each Step The dragline cycle is a **five-step sequential process**, each step physically necessary: 1. **Dig (Fill)**: Drag cable pulls the bucket through the material; hoist cable pays out slowly to keep the bucket at digging depth. → *Why?* The bucket must slice horizontally, not vertically. 2. **Hoist (Lift)**: Hoist cable raises the loaded bucket clear of the digging face. → *Why?* To clear obstructions and allow swing. 3. **Swing (Loaded)**: The house rotates to align the bucket with the dump point. → *Why?* To move material from digging face to dump area. 4. **Dump (Unload)**: Dump cable releases the bucket latch, allowing material to fall out. → *Why?* The bucket has a hinged bottom for this exact purpose. 5. **Return Swing (Empty)**: The house rotates back to the digging face, while the bucket is lowered by paying out the hoist cable. → *Why?* To be ready for the next dig. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept** > The cycle time of a dragline is the **sum of the durations of these five steps**, and it directly determines the production rate. Reducing even 2–3 seconds per cycle can increase productivity by **15–20%** over a shift. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Working radius (boom tip) | $R_w$ | $R_w = L \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | m | $L$ = boom length, $\theta$ = boom angle w.r.t. horizontal | | Vertical reach of boom tip | $H_v$ | $H_v = L \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | m | Height of boom tip above boom pivot | | Dumping height (above ground) | $H_d$ | $H_d = H_p + L \cdot \sin(\theta) - h_b$ | m | $H_p$ = pivot height, $h_b$ = bucket drop | | Maximum digging depth | $D_{max}$ | $D_{max} = L - H_p$ | m | When boom is horizontal, cable fully extended | | Cycle time | $T_c$ | $T_c = t_{dig} + t_{hoist} + t_{swing} + t_{dump} + t_{return}$ | s | Sum of all five phase times | | Theoretical production rate | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{C \cdot 3600}{T_c}$ | m³/hr | $C$ = bucket capacity | | **Actual production rate (bank)** | $Q_{bank}$ | $Q_{bank} = \dfrac{C \cdot F \cdot 3600}{T_c}$ | BCM/hr | $F$ = fill factor ($\leq 1$) | | **Actual production rate (loose)** | $Q_{loose}$ | $Q_{loose} = \dfrac{C \cdot F \cdot S \cdot 3600}{T_c}$ | LCM/hr | $S$ = swell factor ($\geq 1$) | | Swell percentage | $S\%$ | $S\% = \dfrac{V_{loose} - V_{bank}}{V_{bank}} \times 100$ | % | Material property | | Volume relation | $V_{loose}$ | $V_{loose} = V_{bank} \cdot (1 + s)$ | m³ | $s$ = decimal swell (e.g., 0.25 for 25%) | | Operating weight (approx.) | $W$ | Empirical — design chart | tonnes | Manufacturer data | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility in the Field** > These formulas are the *backbone* of mine planning software (e.g., Talpac, HaulSIM). A mine planning engineer uses the production-rate equation hundreds of times per shift schedule to predict fleet output, equipment utilization, and ore-blending feasibility. --- # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Working Radius of the Boom Tip **Given:** A dragline boom of length $L$ is inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. **To Prove:** The horizontal distance from the boom pivot to the boom tip is $R_w = L \cos(\theta)$. **Derivation:** Consider the boom as a straight rigid link $L$ pivoted at the origin $O$. Let $B$ be the boom tip. The position of $B$ relative to $O$ is found by resolving $L$ into its rectangular components: - The component along the horizontal ($x$-axis): $x = L \cos(\theta)$ - The component along the vertical ($y$-axis): $y = L \sin(\theta)$ By the standard definition of the cosine of an angle in a right triangle: $$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{R_w}{L}$$ Solving for the working radius: $$R_w = L \cdot \cos(\theta) \quad \blacksquare$$ **Numerical Example:** For $L = 25$ m, $\theta = 45°$: $$R_w = 25 \times \cos(45°) = 25 \times 0.7071 = 17.68 \text{ m}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Digging Depth **Given:** Boom of length $L$ pivoted at height $H_p$ above the ground. The bucket is suspended on a drag cable. **To Prove:** The theoretical maximum digging depth is $D_{max} = L - H_p$. **Derivation:** The maximum digging depth is achieved when the boom is **horizontal** ($\theta = 0°$) and the drag cable is **fully extended** straight downward from the boom tip. The bucket then hangs directly below the boom tip at a vertical distance equal to the cable length. The maximum cable length is approximately equal to the boom length $L$ (this is a design limit — longer cables are impractical and unsafe). Therefore, the bucket tip can reach down to a level $L$ below the boom pivot. Since the boom pivot is at height $H_p$ above the ground, the depth of the bucket below the ground is: $$D_{max} = L - H_p \quad \blacksquare$$ **Numerical Example:** For $L = 25$ m, $H_p = 6$ m: $$D_{max} = 25 - 6 = 19 \text{ m}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Production Rate in Loose Cubic Meters per Hour **Given:** A dragline with bucket capacity $C$ (geometric, in bank m³), fill factor $F$, swell factor $S$, and cycle time $T_c$ seconds. **To Prove:** $Q_{loose} = \dfrac{C \cdot F \cdot S \cdot 3600}{T_c}$ in LCM/hr. **Derivation:** Step 1: In one cycle, the bucket scoops a *bank* volume equal to the geometric capacity times the fill factor: $$V_{bank,per\_cycle} = C \cdot F$$ Step 2: When the bucket is hoisted and dumped, this bank volume expands to a *loose* volume (it "swelled" during excavation). The swell factor $S$ is defined as: $$S = \frac{V_{loose}}{V_{bank}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{loose,per\_cycle} = C \cdot F \cdot S$$ Step 3: The number of cycles in one hour is: $$N = \frac{3600 \text{ s/hr}}{T_c \text{ s/cycle}}$$ Step 4: The production rate in loose cubic meters per hour is: $$Q_{loose} = V_{loose,per\_cycle} \times N$$ Substituting: $$Q_{loose} = C \cdot F \cdot S \cdot \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Worked Numerical Example:** - $C = 1.5$ m³, $F = 0.9$, $S = 1.25$, $T_c = 30$ s $$Q_{loose} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.9 \times 1.25 \times 3600}{30}$$ Step-by-step: $$Q_{loose} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.9}{1} = 1.35 \text{ (bank m³/cycle)}$$ $$Q_{loose} = 1.35 \times 1.25 = 1.6875 \text{ (loose m³/cycle)}$$ $$Q_{loose} = 1.6875 \times \frac{3600}{30} = 1.6875 \times 120$$ $$Q_{loose} = 202.5 \text{ LCM/hr} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Excavators | Feature | Dragline | Power Shovel | Backhoe | |---|---|---|---| | Digging method | Cable drag (toward machine) | Rigid arm push (away) | Rigid arm pull (toward) | | Digging position | **Below machine level** ✓ | Above machine level | Below or near level | | Reach radius | **Very long** (up to 100 m) | Short (15–25 m) | Medium (10–20 m) | | Cycle time | Long (30–60 s) | Short (20–30 s) | Medium (25–35 s) | | Best suited for | Soft strata, deep pits, large areas | Hard, blasted rock faces | Trenching, foundations | | Mobility | Walking or crawler (slow) | Crawler (medium) | Wheeled (fast) | | Capital cost | **Very high** | High | Medium | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight** > A common KTU question is to *justify the selection* of a dragline over a power shovel. The single most decisive factor is the **requirement to dig below the machine's own standing level** — draglines win, every time. --- # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Major Components of a Dragline The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a dragline and the inter-relationships between its major sub-systems. This is preferred over a freehand sketch because it emphasizes the engineering systems, not the artistic form. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[PRIME MOVER
Electric Motor or Diesel Engine] --> B[MACHINERY HOUSE
Houses all drive systems] B --> C[HOIST DRUM
Raises the loaded bucket] B --> D[DRAG DRUM
Pulls bucket through material] B --> E[DUMP DRUM
Releases bucket latch] B --> F[SWING MOTORS
Rotate the house 360 deg] F --> G[TUB and RING GEAR
Revolving upper structure] G --> H[BOOM and A-FRAME
Long lattice structure 25 to 100 m] H --> I[HOIST CABLE
Vertical lift rope] H --> J[DRAG CABLE
Horizontal pull rope] H --> K[DUMP CABLE
Bucket release rope] I --> L[BUCKET
Scoop with teeth and hinged bottom] J --> L K --> L B --> M[COUNTERWEIGHT
Balances boom load] G --> N[UNDERCARRIAGE] N --> N1[CRAWLER TRACKS
For crawler draglines] N --> N2[WALKING SHOES
For walking draglines] F --> O[OPERATOR CABIN
Controls and instruments] ``` ## 4.2 Working Cycle Flowchart The five operational phases of a dragline, with the active cable and the swing state for each phase: ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[PHASE 1: DIG
Drag cable active
Bucket pulled through material
Hoist pays out] --> P2[PHASE 2: HOIST
Hoist cable active
Bucket raised clear of face] P2 --> P3[PHASE 3: SWING LOADED
Swing motors active
House rotates to dump point] P3 --> P4[PHASE 4: DUMP
Dump cable active
Bucket latch released
Material falls out] P4 --> P5[PHASE 5: RETURN SWING
Swing motors active
House rotates back to face
Hoist cable pays out] P5 --> P1 ``` ## 4.3 Boom Geometry and Working Range The working range of a dragline is defined by four critical envelope parameters: **working radius, dumping height, digging depth, and dumping radius**. The Mermaid block below summarizes how each is derived from the boom geometry. ```mermaid flowchart TB G[BOOM GEOMETRY] --> G1[Boom length L] G --> G2[Boom angle theta] G1 --> R[WORKING RADIUS R = L cos theta] G2 --> R G1 --> H[DUMPING HEIGHT H = L sin theta plus pivot height] G2 --> H G1 --> D[DEEP DIG D = L minus pivot height] G2 --> D G1 --> DR[DUMP RADIUS Rmax reached at low boom angle] G2 --> DR R --> ENVELOPE[DRAGLINE WORKING ENVELOPE] H --> ENVELOPE D --> ENVELOPE DR --> ENVELOPE ENVELOPE --> NOTE[This envelope defines the volume of
material the dragline can reach in a single setup] ``` ## 4.4 Component Layout (Side Elevation — Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BASE["UNDERCARRIAGE BASE"] U1[Walking shoe or crawler track] end subgraph HOUSE["MACHINERY HOUSE"] H1[Operator cabin] H2[Hoist and drag drums] H3[Counterweight at rear] H4[Swing ring gear] end subgraph BOOM_ASSY["BOOM ASSEMBLY"] B1[A-frame and gantry] B2[Lattice boom inclined at theta] B3[Boom point sheaves] end subgraph RIG["CABLE RIGGING"] C1[Hoist cable] C2[Drag cable] C3[Dump cable] end subgraph BUCKET_ASSY["BUCKET"] K1[Bucket body with teeth] K2[Hinged bucket bottom] end U1 --> H4 H4 --> H1 H4 --> H2 H4 --> H3 H2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B2 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B2 --> C3 C1 --> K1 C2 --> K1 C3 --> K2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading Aid for Students** > When a KTU question asks "draw a neat sketch of a dragline," the examiner typically expects the following to be visible and labeled: (1) Boom with angle marked, (2) Hoist + Drag + Dump cables, (3) Bucket with teeth, (4) Machinery house, (5) Undercarriage, (6) Counterweight. The Mermaid block diagram above lists all these — practice redrawing it in your exam sheet. --- # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern] **Define a dragline excavator. List any four of its major applications.** **Model Answer:** A dragline excavator is a cable-operated earthmoving machine in which a long lattice boom supports a bucket suspended on wire ropes; the bucket is dragged toward the machine by a tensioned cable to excavate material, often from below the machine's own standing level. **Four major applications:** 1. **Surface mining** — opencast coal, iron ore, and copper mining for overburden removal. 2. **Large canal excavation** — long, deep trenches where reach is critical. 3. **Dock and harbor construction** — deep underwater excavation near water bodies. 4. **Large foundation and dam construction** — excavation below the level of the surrounding ground. > [!NOTE] > *Other valid applications accepted: road cutting in hilly terrain, building large reservoirs, mining of sand and gravel from river beds.* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern] **List six major components of a dragline. State the function of the drag cable.** **Model Answer:** **Six major components:** 1. Boom (lattice structure) 2. Bucket (with teeth) 3. Hoist cable 4. Drag cable 5. Swing mechanism (ring gear and motors) 6. Machinery house (with counterweight and operator cabin) **Function of the drag cable:** The drag cable is responsible for **pulling the bucket horizontally through the material being excavated**, dragging the cut material toward the machine. The bucket is filled by this horizontal scraping motion rather than by a vertical push, which is the defining characteristic of a dragline cycle. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Part B questions carry 14 marks, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative questions are offered; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. The solutions below show the complete working expected from a top-scoring answer script. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1 **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a dragline excavator with a neat sketch. Describe its complete operational cycle. **Model Answer (7 Marks Allocation Breakdown):** - *Sketch with key labels*: **2 marks** - *Principle of operation*: **2 marks** - *Cycle description (all 5 phases)*: **2 marks** - *Diagrammatic clarity and neatness*: **1 mark** **Sketch (refer to Section 4.4 for layout):** The dragline consists of a long lattice **boom** mounted on a high A-frame, a **bucket** suspended on three cables, a **machinery house** with hoisting and swinging equipment, and a **walking/crawler undercarriage**. **Working Principle:** A dragline works on the principle of **cable-suspended excavation**. The bucket, hanging from the boom tip, is **dragged** through the material by a tensioned drag cable while a hoist cable pays out, allowing the bucket to maintain a constant digging depth.
